summary: Audit neo-rs against C# Neo v3.9.1/2 for protocol parity + crate boundaries
logs: [stall] agent "rpc-api-surface" stalled (no progress) after 2079s — 1 StructuredOutput validation failure (last input: {"dimension":"RPC/REST API surface parity (neo-rpc vs C# RpcServer + StateService/ApplicationLogs/TokensTracker/OracleService plugins, v3.9.1)","parity_verdict":"minor-gaps","domain_summary":"Method-level parity is strong: all 55 JSON-RPC methods registered across the C# RpcServer + StateService + A…) — retrying (1/5)

agents.summary: The 11 real native contracts (ContractManagement, StdLib, CryptoLib, Ledger, NeoToken, GasToken, Policy, RoleManagement, Oracle, Notary, Treasury) are present with correct IDs (-1..-11), correct hashes (computed via the same ABORT+sender+checksum+name script), and the consensus-critical machinery (NEF dummy-script generation, method sort by (name,paramCount) with StringComparer.Ordinal, NEF checksum, method offsets, hardfork-gated method/event variants) faithfully mirrors C# v3.9.1. Constant-level parity is excellent: Policy/NeoToken/Gas/Oracle/Ledger/Notary/RoleManagement storage prefixes, fee/price defaults, reward ratios, role byte values, and Notary deltas all match exactly. However there are several genuine consensus-breaking divergences. The two most severe are (1) the Rust node hardcodes HF_Faun activation heights (mainnet 8800000, testnet 12960000) that do NOT exist in C# v3.9.1's config.mainnet.json/config.testnet.json (which stop at HF_Echidna), and (2) Rust registers a fabricated 12th native contract "TokenManagement" (id -12) that has no counterpart in C# at all and is gated to deploy at HF_Faun. Together these guarantee a hard fork at the fabricated Faun height. Additional consensus divergences exist in StdLib (base64Url methods gated at the wrong hardfork; over-broad/incorrect MaxLength enforcement) and OracleContract.request (userData limit 65535 raw bytes vs C# 512 serialized bytes). The headline conclusion: native-contract scaffolding is high-fidelity, but fabricated hardfork scheduling + a fabricated native contract + a handful of gate/limit mismatches would fork the chain.

The static opcode metadata is solid: the canonical OpCode enum in neo-vm-rs (src/vm/opcode.rs) matches C# Neo.VM/OpCode.cs byte-for-byte including all reserved gaps, and the neo-core opcode price table (smart_contract/application_engine_op_code_prices.rs) matches ApplicationEngine.OpCodePrices.cs exactly across all 256 entries (verified programmatically, 0 mismatches). StackItemType tags, ScriptBuilder emit semantics, and rpc_json envelope rendering are also faithful. However, the execution-limit and reference-counting semantics have multiple consensus-breaking divergences. The most serious structural problem is that neo-rs runs TWO different VM engines on the consensus path: a fast-path "external VM" (the external neo-vm-rs interpreter, used for pure scripts via try_execute_with_external_vm) and the neo-core jump_table engine (fallback). These two engines enforce limits differently from each other AND from C#: MaxItemSize is 65535 in one place, 1MB in another, vs C#'s 131070; MaxComparableSize is not enforced at all in neo-core EQUAL; the external VM caps call depth at 64 vs C#'s 1024 and uses eval-stack-length instead of the C# reference-counter for MaxStackSize; and neo-core's post-instruction MaxStackSize check is off-by-one. Any of these will desync block execution from C# nodes. The headline conclusion: opcode/price tables are interop-safe, but limit enforcement and the dual-engine architecture are not consensus-safe and require remediation before mainnet.

The Neo N3 P2P wire format is faithfully reproduced. MessageCommand byte values (0x00–0x40), MessageFlags (None=0, Compressed=0x01), NodeCapabilityType bytes (TcpServer=0x01, WsServer=0x02, DisableCompression=0x03, FullNode=0x10, ArchivalNode=0x11, Extension0=0xF0), the header layout (flags+command+var-length payload, no magic/checksum which is correct for N3), the var-int length framing with PayloadMaxSize=0x02000000, the LZ4 4-byte-LE-length-prefixed block compression format, the network magic numbers (mainnet 0x334F454E/860833102, testnet 0x3554334E/894710606), and the handshake (Version→Verack with network-magic validation, compression negotiation, full-node detection) all match the C# v3.9 reference. Every audited payload (VersionPayload, NetworkAddressWithTime/AddrPayload, Inv/GetData/NotFound, GetBlocks, GetBlockByIndex, Headers, MerkleBlock, FilterLoad/FilterAdd/FilterClear, Ping/Pong, Extensible, and all NodeCapability variants) serializes byte-for-byte identically with matching limits (MaxCapabilities=32, MaxCountToSend=200, MaxHashesCount=500, MaxHeadersCount=2000, filter≤36000/K≤50, FilterAdd≤520, UserAgent≤1024, category≤32). The biggest issues are a duplicate, incorrect compression implementation in neo-p2p's RawMessage (unused but publicly exported), an extra InventoryType::Consensus=0x2d that C# rejects on read, and outbound/inbound queue priority sets whose doc-comments claim C# parity but diverge from the actual C# mailbox policy. None of these are consensus-breaking; the canonical send/receive path used by the live network layer is correct.

The dBFT message layer in neo-consensus is a faithful, byte-accurate port of C# DBFTPlugin: ConsensusMessageType bytes (0x00/0x20/0x21/0x30/0x40/0x41), ChangeViewReason bytes, the common header order (type, blockIndex, validatorIndex, viewNumber), and every message body (PrepareRequest, PrepareResponse, Commit 64-byte sig, ChangeView timestamp+reason, RecoveryRequest timestamp, RecoveryMessage with ChangeView/Preparation/Commit compact payloads, 1024-byte invocation cap, hasPrepareRequest flag + PreparationHash varbytes path) all match C# serialization exactly. The ExtensiblePayload-unsigned bytes used for the dBFT witness hash (varstring "dBFT" | u32 ValidBlockStart=0 | u32 ValidBlockEnd=BlockIndex | sender(20) | varbytes(data)) and the sign-data layout (network||single-SHA256(unsigned)) are correct, as is the F/M threshold math and primary selection ((index - view) mod n). The crate boundary is clean — neo-consensus owns the protocol and state machine, the node (neo-node/src/consensus.rs) wires it, no duplication in neo-core. The real risks are in block assembly and timing wired at the node layer: NextConsensus is computed from the current-round validators instead of the next-block validator set, which diverges from C# at committee-refresh / validator-rotation boundaries (consensus-breaking on those blocks); the primary's proposed timestamp is not clamped to max(prevTimestamp+1, now); and the view-change timeout formula is a simplified single curve with a shift clamp. None of these break steady-state Rust-only consensus, but the NextConsensus issue is consensus-breaking at rotation boundaries and the timestamp/timer issues are interop/liveness behavioral diffs vs C#.

agents.dimension: Cryptography parity (neo-crypto vs neo_csharp/src/Neo/Cryptography + CryptoLib native)
agents.summary: The core hashing (SHA256/512, Hash160, Hash256, RIPEMD160, Keccak256, SHA3, Blake2b with/without salt), Murmur32/Murmur128 (canonical constants + little-endian output), Merkle root computation, Base58/Base58Check (Bitcoin alphabet), Base64, bloom-filter seed derivation (i*0xFBA4C795+tweak), and the dominant secp256r1 ECDSA verification path (System.Crypto.CheckSig/CheckMultiSig via RustCrypto p256, which accepts high-s like C#'s .NET ECDsa) are all faithful to the C# v3.9 reference. However, I found two concrete parity defects. CRITICAL: the NamedCurveHash enum in neo-crypto encodes the Keccak256 variants as 0x18/0x19 instead of C#'s 122/123 (0x7A/0x7B), so CryptoLib.verifyWithECDsa with Keccak256 curves diverges from consensus. HIGH: the secp256k1 verification path (Secp256k1Crypto::verify, used by CryptoLib.verifyWithECDsa secp256k1 variants and Notary's verify_signature_bytes) uses the libsecp256k1 binding which rejects high-s signatures, whereas C# (BouncyCastle/.NET) accepts them — a malleability divergence. Separately, NEP-2 wallet key encryption (neo-core, not neo-crypto) uses AES-256-CBC with a zero IV instead of C#'s AES-256-ECB, breaking wallet interop on the second 16-byte block. Crate boundary: address encoding (0x35) and Scrypt/NEP-2 live in neo-core rather than neo-crypto; the neo-crypto Bls12381Crypto signature scheme is unused by the consensus native BLS (which correctly lives in neo-core via blst raw arithmetic).

The core binary-IO layer (neo-io var-int/var-bytes/var-string, BinaryWriter/MemoryReader primitives, UInt160/UInt256) is byte-exact with C# Neo v3.9: var-int 0xFD/0xFE/0xFF prefixes, all multi-byte integers little-endian, UInt160 (20-byte LE, 3 fields) and UInt256 (32-byte LE, 4 fields) read/write/parse/ToString and CompareTo (most-significant field first) all match. Consensus-critical structures are largely correct: Transaction (version/nonce/sysfee/netfee/validuntilblock/signers/attributes/script/witnesses), Header, Signer + WitnessScope flags, Witness, WitnessRule/WitnessCondition (including depth-counting and type bytes), all TransactionAttribute variants (HighPriority/OracleResponse/NotValidBefore/Conflicts/NotaryAssisted) with correct type bytes and payload widths, and ExtensiblePayload all serialize byte-for-byte identically. The most significant finding is an interop-breaking ECPoint-encoding gap: C# accepts both compressed (33-byte) and uncompressed (65-byte) group points in WitnessCondition::Group/CalledByGroup and in Signer.AllowedGroups, but the Rust deserializers force exactly 33 bytes and reject/misalign on 65-byte points. Secondary issues: Block::deserialize moves the duplicate-tx and Merkle-root checks out of deserialization (C# enforces them inline) and adds a cumulative MAX_BLOCK_SIZE check C# does not have at deserialize time; and a duplicate/divergent neo-p2p Message::to_bytes has incorrect LZ4 compression gating (the live neo-core NetworkMessage path is correct). No little-endianness, field-width, or field-order divergences were found in any consensus structure.

agents.dimension: Ledger / blockchain semantics parity (Block, Transaction, Header, TransactionAttribute, MemoryPool, verification pipeline, genesis)
agents.summary: Core wire-format and hashing parity is strong: Transaction/Header/Block unsigned serialization, single-SHA256 hashing preimage, Signer scope rules (MAX_SUBITEMS=16, Global exclusivity), TransactionAttribute type bytes (0x01/0x11/0x20/0x21/0x22) and AllowMultiple, PoolItem fee-sort comparator, MAX_VERIFICATION_GAS (150M), and the mainnet genesis block (verified by a hash-equality test against 0x1f4d1def...) all match C# v3.9 byte-for-byte. However, the block-import verification path diverges substantially from C# in ways that can fork the chain. C# Block.Verify is header-only; the Rust Block::verify additionally enforces a fabricated 2MB MAX_BLOCK_SIZE, a 512 tx-count cap, a 15-minute timestamp-future-drift bound, witness-script-size limits, and—most seriously—re-runs full per-transaction state-independent AND state-dependent verification during block import, none of which C# does (C# only executes tx scripts in Persist without a verification gate). Any block C# accepts but that trips one of these extra Rust gates causes a consensus halt/fork. Separately, OracleResponse::verify omits three of C#'s five checks (request existence, exact GasForResponse fee match, designated-oracle signer), so the Rust node accepts oracle-response transactions C# rejects. Block deserialization also fails to enforce C#'s merkle-root-match and duplicate-tx FormatExceptions at parse time. Mempool semantics are close but contain a non-C# per-sender limit and an AlreadyInPool check that also covers the unverified set.

The Rust neo-rpc server implements all 55 JSON-RPC methods present across the C# RpcServer, StateService, ApplicationLogs, OracleService and TokensTracker plugins, with matching lowercase method names, the full Neo error-code table (minus 101 to minus 109, minus 300 to minus 305, minus 500 to minus 512, minus 600 to minus 608 plus standard JSON-RPC codes), and faithful request dispatch (GET base64 params, POST batch handling, notification-without-id handling, max-depth guard). No methods are missing; getblocksysfee is the only extraneous one (removed in C# v3.9). Most result JSON shapes match C# key for key (getversion, getblock and header, getrawtransaction, getrawmempool, getnep17 and nep11 balances and transfers, findstates, getcandidates, getnextblockvalidators, getcommittee, getapplicationlog, validateaddress, invoke methods). Several interop-breaking value or shape divergences exist: getunclaimedgas returns decimal-formatted GAS instead of raw integer datoshi; getrawtransaction verbose injects an extra vmstate field C# never emits; the relay-result error path drops the data and message suffix C# attaches. The headline 37K versus 4K LOC is apples to oranges: about 13K LOC is test code, about 18K LOC is a full RpcClient SDK matching the C# Neo.Network.RPC package which is NOT vendored in the C# tree, and the server-only non-test code is about 10.5K. Real bloat exists: a faithful-but-dead ParameterConverter, three overlapping RpcError and RpcErrorCode types, and about 1K LOC of WebSocket subscriptions the C# RpcServer does not have. No consensus-breaking issues since RPC is non-consensus, but the value-format bugs will break clients and wallets relying on byte-identical responses.</domain_summary>
</invoke>

neo-core is 102,451 LOC (54% of repo) and conflates three distinct concerns: (1) genuine C# Neo.csproj core (smart_contract 35K, network 17K, ledger 6K, neo_system 3.9K, wallets 3.5K, builders, witness, extensions — these correctly belong in core and match C# layering); (2) inlined C# *plugin* assemblies (oracle_service 5.3K, state_service 4.4K, tokens_tracker 2K, application_logs 0.5K — ~12.2K LOC that C# ships as separate Neo.Plugins.* projects); and (3) infrastructure that is either a thin facade or belongs in a sibling crate (compression 23 LOC is a pure neo-io facade; the RocksDB store/provider 1.3K lives in neo-core but implements neo-storage traits and is a plugin in C#; the generic actor runtime 1.8K has zero neo-coupling and is already consumed cross-crate as neo_core::runtime). Active consolidation is underway and correct: recent commits made neo-p2p the canonical MessageCommand/MessageFlags and neo-core/network now re-exports neo-p2p wire payloads/types rather than duplicating them, while keeping ledger-coupled payloads (Block/Tx/Signer/Header) local — that coupling is genuine and justifies them staying in core. No consensus/protocol risk arises from the boundary layout itself; this dimension is organizational. A realistic decomposition (extract RocksDB→neo-storage, actor runtime→neo-actors, and the four inlined plugins→their own crates) would reduce neo-core by ~15K LOC (~15%) and bring crate layering into 1:1 alignment with C# core-vs-plugins separation.

neo-rpc is 37,818 LOC across 131 files, but ~13,242 LOC (35%) are in-tree tests, leaving ~24,576 LOC of source. Source splits into a JSON-RPC server (~10,476 non-test) and a full RPC client SDK (~13,373 non-test). There is NO REST server — only JSON-RPC — so the headline "vs C# RpcServer 4.1K + RestServer 5.8K = 10K" comparison is misleading: the correct server-to-server comparison is Rust ~10.5K vs C# RpcServer 4.1K (~2.5x), and the entire 5.8K C# RestServer has no Rust counterpart. The other ~13.4K is a standalone client SDK that mirrors C#'s separate Neo.Network.RPC NuGet package and has NO production consumer inside this workspace (neo-node enables only the `server` feature). The 2.5x server inflation is driven by: hand-rolled HTTP/TLS plumbing (warp + manual hyper + tokio_rustls dual accept loops + p12/sha1 cert parsing, ~870 LOC) where C# uses Kestrel config; two parallel parameter-parsing systems (a trait-based `parameter_converter` module used exactly once, plus ad-hoc `expect_*` helpers); triple-encoded wire schemas (server map-builder + client from_json/to_json + serde derive for the same block/header shape); and genuinely-added features absent from C# RpcServer (per-IP/per-method rate limiter ~661 LOC, WebSocket subscriptions ~1,053 LOC). Protocol parity is largely intact (RPC error codes and block/header field names verified against C#), but the triple-maintained wire encodings and divergent param-error messages are drift risks rather than current breaks. Realistically ~6–9K of source is reducible via: extracting the client into its own crate, replacing hand-rolled HTTP/TLS with axum/tower or jsonrpsee's server, collapsing the two param systems, and centralizing block/header JSON in neo-core.

The actual on-disk serialization is C#-compatible: StorageKey persists as `id (i32 LE) ++ prefix ++ content` (neo-storage/src/types/storage_key.rs `build`/`to_array`, matching C# StorageKey.Build at StorageKey.cs:368), StorageItem persists as raw value bytes only (commit path uses `item.to_value()` in store_cache.rs:202 and data_cache/cache.rs:772, matching C# StorageItem.Serialize = `writer.Write(Value.Span)`), and block commits are atomic via RocksDB WriteBatch (rocksdb/store.rs:696 `db.write_opt`), matching C# RocksDBStore.Snapshot. The trait hierarchy (ReadOnlyStore/WriteStore/Store/StoreSnapshot/StoreProvider/StoreFactory) and cache hierarchy (DataCache/Trackable/StoreCache + `clone_cache` ClonedCache analog) faithfully mirror Neo.Persistence. The headline structural issue is split ownership: neo-storage holds the traits, types, DataCache, StoreCache and the memory backend, while the only persistent backend (RocksDB) lives in neo-core/persistence — this actually mirrors C#'s core-vs-RocksDBStore-plugin split, but it is buried inside neo-core rather than its own crate, and neo-core/persistence is otherwise a thin re-export facade over neo-storage (storage_key.rs, storage_item.rs, providers.rs, data_cache/mod.rs are pure re-exports). Two latent footguns exist but are NOT on live consensus paths: StorageItem carries an `is_constant` field/flag that does not exist in C# v3.9 (and a `StorageValue::to_storage_bytes` that prepends it), and persistence/serialization.rs ships bincode helpers documented "matches C# Neo exactly" that are unrelated to C# serialization. Neither is wired into the DB write path today. Mature crate usage is appropriate (the `rocksdb`/librocksdb-sys crate, like reth-over-mdbx; no hand-rolled DB).

agents.dimension: Foundation crates cohesion + idiomatic Rust + ecosystem reuse
agents.summary: The foundation crates are generally well-built and idiomatic, with strong ecosystem reuse. neo-crypto is exemplary: it uses RustCrypto/audited crates throughout (k256, p256, ed25519-dalek, secp256k1, sha2, sha3, ripemd, blake2, blst, murmur3, subtle, zeroize) with no hand-rolled primitive crypto, and merkle/BLS/hash logic matches the C# reference (verified Concat order and BLS variant). neo-primitives backs UInt160/UInt256 with u64/u32 words whose Ord/CompareTo exactly matches C# word-level comparison (consensus-correct), and BigDecimal sits on num-bigint matching C# BigInteger+byte. The two biggest issues are duplication/boundary problems, not byte-level parity: (1) there are TWO divergent ProtocolSettings structs — the canonical consensus one in neo-core/protocol_settings.rs (StandbyCommittee as ECPoint, Hardfork enum dict) and a different-shaped one in neo-config (validators as hex strings, custom hardfork struct) that the RPC client uses for transaction signing; and (2) a JSON serialization escaping divergence where C# RpcServer emits via Utf8JsonWriter (JavaScriptEncoder.Default escapes <, >, &, ', + and all non-ASCII) while the Rust RPC path serializes serde_json::Value with minimal escaping, producing byte-different RPC responses for any non-ASCII/special-char field. neo-json itself is a justified thin wrapper over serde_json but is largely bypassed in the RPC hot path.

agents.dimension: Ecosystem / best-in-class crate adoption (reth + polkadot-sdk patterns)
agents.summary: This dimension is about engineering hygiene, not protocol parity — none of the findings touch byte layouts, consensus hashes, or wire format, so the parity verdict is not-applicable. Overall the workspace is in better ecosystem shape than the prompt implies: P2P framing already uses tokio-util Framed/Decoder/Encoder (neo-core/src/network/p2p/framed_codec.rs), the rate limiter uses governor, the CLI uses clap derive, config uses toml+serde, graceful shutdown uses idiomatic tokio signals, the actor scheduler uses tokio-util CancellationToken/DropGuard, and storage is cleanly abstracted behind Store/StoreProvider traits with rocksdb as just one provider. The biggest hand-rolled liabilities are: (1) a full Akka.NET-style actor system (1.3K LOC excl. tests) transliterated from C#, now the orchestration spine of NeoSystem; (2) fragmented, duplicated telemetry with FOUR separate metrics surfaces and TWO crates (neo-telemetry + neo-core/telemetry) covering the same ground, one of which reinvents prometheus Counter/Gauge with raw AtomicU64; and (3) the RPC server built on warp 0.3.7 (the last warp release, locked to EOL hyper 0.14) while jsonrpsee is already a dependency and pulls in hyper 1.x — so the build carries two hyper major versions. The headline conclusion: prioritize consolidating telemetry and migrating RPC off warp to jsonrpsee (the reth/substrate standard); treat the actor-system replacement as a larger, lower-urgency refactor.

The 14-crate workspace has a healthy, acyclic dependency graph with a clean foundation layer (neo-primitives → neo-io/neo-crypto/neo-storage → neo-core), and neo-tee/neo-hsm are correctly isolated optional integrations (each depends only on neo-crypto, with node wiring feature-gated in neo-node). However, three cohesion defects undermine the layering: (1) a P2P ownership inversion where neo-p2p (2.1K LOC) holds only protocol enums while the entire networking stack (16.6K LOC: LocalNode/RemoteNode/TaskManager/handshake) lives in neo-core/network/p2p — the crate named "p2p" is a thin type-bag and neo-core owns the real P2P node; (2) a duplicated ProtocolSettings: neo-config::ProtocolSettings (consumed by 22 neo-rpc client sites for transaction building) is an entirely separate type from neo-core::protocol_settings::ProtocolSettings (consumed by NeoSystem/consensus), with two independent hardcoded copies of consensus-critical params (network magic, address_version, max_valid_until_block_increment) that coincide today but can silently diverge; (3) two parallel telemetry stacks (neo-core/telemetry 796 LOC unused inside core, plus neo-telemetry 1988 LOC) both consumed simultaneously by neo-node. neo-node also carries non-trivial logic (the 1315-LOC dBFT ConsensusActor runtime) that arguably belongs in neo-consensus. No cycles or hard layering violations were found; the issues are misplacement and duplication, not breakage. Headline: protocol parity is not currently at risk, but the dual-ProtocolSettings and P2P inversion are the two structural items most likely to cause future interop bugs and should anchor the refactor.

consensus_breaking.title: Rust fabricates HF_Faun activation heights absent from C# v3.9.1 config (hard fork at Faun height)
consensus_breaking.problem: neo-core/src/hardfork.rs hardcodes HfFaun at mainnet block 8800000 (line 77) and testnet 12960000 (line 91). C# v3.9.1 src/Neo.CLI/config.mainnet.json and config.testnet.json schedule hardforks ONLY through HF_Echidna (mainnet 7300000, testnet 5870000); there is NO HF_Faun (or HF_Gorgon) entry in either file. At block 8800000 (mainnet) the Rust node would activate Faun and, via ContractManagement OnPersist initialization (contract_management/native_impl.rs:257 calling is_initialize_block), deploy Treasury + TokenManagement, switch StdLib to expose hexEncode/hexDecode, and change Policy MaxExecFeeFactor scaling (PolicyContract.cs:517) — none of which C# v3.9.1 nodes would do. This produces an immediately divergent state root: a guaranteed chain split.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Do not hardcode Faun/Gorgon heights. Source hardfork heights exclusively from the loaded ProtocolSettings/config JSON (matching C# behavior where omitted hardforks are disabled). Leave HfFaun and HfGorgon unscheduled for mainnet/testnet until the official C# config adds them, and verify against the in-tree neo_csharp/src/Neo.CLI/config.*.json at build/test time.

consensus_breaking.title: Fabricated native contract TokenManagement (id -12) does not exist in C#
consensus_breaking.problem: neo-core registers a 12th native contract TokenManagement (native/token_management/mod.rs:37 ID=-12, hash 0xae00c57daeb20f9b654f3265a918f44a8a40e049, active_in=HfFaun) and lists it in NativeRegistry::register_standard_contracts (native/mod.rs:190). No TokenManagement exists anywhere in neo_csharp (grep across the whole C# tree returns zero matches). C# v3.9.1 has exactly 11 native contracts: ContractManagement(-1), StdLib(-2), CryptoLib(-3), Ledger(-4), NEO(-5), GAS(-6), Policy(-7), RoleManagement(-8), Oracle(-9), Notary(-10), Treasury(-11), per NativeContract.cs idCounter (`Id { get; } = --idCounter`, line 144) and the static-property construction order (lines 61-111). If Faun ever activates, Rust deploys a native contract and its storage that C# never has, diverging the state root.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Remove the TokenManagement native contract entirely (delete native/token_management/, the mod.rs declarations, registry registration, and the -12 id). It is not part of the Neo v3.9 protocol.

consensus_breaking.title: StdLib base64UrlEncode/base64UrlDecode gated at HfCockatrice instead of HF_Echidna
consensus_breaking.problem: Rust gates base64UrlEncode/base64UrlDecode with active = HfCockatrice (native/std_lib/metadata.rs:20-21). C# gates both with Hardfork.HF_Echidna (StdLib.cs:143,154). Between the Cockatrice (mainnet 5450000) and Echidna (7300000) activation heights, the Rust StdLib native manifest/NEF would include these two methods while C#'s would not. This changes the StdLib NEF dummy-script bytes (extra PUSH0/SYSCALL/RET per method shifts all subsequent method offsets) and the manifest method set, producing a different StdLib ContractState and a different state root for that entire ~1.85M-block window; calls to these methods would also succeed in Rust but FAULT in C#.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Change active = HfCockatrice to active = HfEchidna for base64UrlEncode and base64UrlDecode in native/std_lib/metadata.rs to match StdLib.cs.

consensus_breaking.title: OracleContract.request enforces wrong userData limit (65535 raw bytes) vs C# (512 serialized bytes)
consensus_breaking.problem: Rust request() checks user_data.len() > self.config.max_user_data_length where the default is BLOCK_MAX_TX_WIRE_LIMIT = 65535 (oracle_contract/config.rs:38, request.rs:65) against the RAW argument bytes. C# does not bound the raw bytes at all; instead it serializes the userData StackItem via BinarySerializer.Serialize(userData, MaxUserDataLength=512, MaxStackSize) (OracleContract.cs:36-39,265), faulting if the SERIALIZED form exceeds 512 bytes. So a request with serialized userData of, e.g., 600 bytes succeeds in Rust but faults in C#; and raw-byte measurement differs from serialized-StackItem measurement even below 512. Either case changes VM fault outcome -> divergent execution result.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Set the Oracle userData limit to 512 and measure the BinarySerializer-serialized form of the userData StackItem (not the raw argument length), exactly mirroring OracleContract.cs:265's BinarySerializer.Serialize(userData, 512, MaxStackSize). Also align url/filter/callback size checks to C#'s GetStrictUtf8ByteCount semantics.

consensus_breaking.title: StdLib MaxLength(1024) enforced on params C# does not annotate (memorySearch.value, stringSplit.separator, jsonDeserialize)
consensus_breaking.problem: C# applies [MaxLength(1024)] only to specific parameters: MemorySearch annotates ONLY mem (StdLib.cs:223,229,235), StringSplit annotates ONLY str (lines 250,256), and JsonDeserialize has NO [MaxLength] at all (line 53, uses JToken.Parse(json,10)). Rust over-enforces: it caps memorySearch BOTH mem and value (std_lib/memory.rs:43-44), stringSplit BOTH str and separator (strings.rs:31-32), and jsonDeserialize input (serialization.rs:72). A memorySearch with a >1024-byte value, a stringSplit with a >1024-byte separator, or a jsonDeserialize of >1024-byte JSON would FAULT in Rust but execute in C# -> divergent VM result.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Remove ensure_max_input_len on memorySearch's value arg (keep only mem), on stringSplit's separator arg (keep only str), and on jsonDeserialize entirely. Match the exact set of [MaxLength]-annotated parameters in StdLib.cs.

consensus_breaking.title: Default MaxItemSize is 65535, must be 131070 (ushort.MaxValue * 2)
consensus_breaking.problem: C# ExecutionEngineLimits.MaxItemSize = ushort.MaxValue * 2 = 131070 (ExecutionEngineLimits.cs:40). The Rust default in neo-vm-rs (src/vm/limits.rs:43) sets max_item_size: u16::MAX as u32 = 65535. neo-core's ApplicationEngine uses neo_vm_rs::ExecutionEngineLimits via ExecutionEngine::new -> default() (neo_vm/execution_engine/mod.rs:193, execution_engine/core.rs:18; confirmed no override constructs 131070). This limit is enforced on NEWBUFFER, CAT, MEMCPY, conversions and BinarySerializer ByteString/Buffer deserialization (neo_vm/jump_table/splice.rs:106-126; smart_contract/binary_serializer.rs:51-122). Any stack item or serialized blob in the 65536..=131070 byte range succeeds in C# but FAULTs in Rust, diverging transaction/block execution.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Set neo-vm-rs ExecutionEngineLimits::DEFAULT.max_item_size = u16::MAX as u32 * 2 (131070). Add a parity test that builds a 131070-byte buffer via NEWBUFFER and asserts HALT, and a 131071-byte buffer asserts FAULT, matching C#.

consensus_breaking.title: External VM (neo-vm-rs) caps NEWBUFFER/CAT item size at 1MB instead of 131070
consensus_breaking.problem: The external-VM fast path (used for pure scripts via ApplicationEngine::try_execute_with_external_vm, external_vm.rs:254) enforces NEWBUFFER and CAT against the free-standing constant MAX_ITEM_SIZE = 1024*1024 = 1MB (neo-vm-rs/src/semantics/collections.rs:98-99 'buffer size exceeds MaxItemSize (1MB)'; src/semantics/splice.rs:14-15). C# limits both to MaxItemSize = 131070 (JumpTable.Splice.cs:31 NewBuffer AssertMaxItemSize, :77 Cat AssertMaxItemSize). A NEWBUFFER/CAT producing 131071..=1048576 bytes HALTs in the external VM but FAULTs in C#. Note this is also internally inconsistent with neo-vm-rs's own ExecutionEngineLimits (65535) and neo-core's enforcement (65535) — three different effective limits for the same operation.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Make the external VM enforce the real MaxItemSize (131070) for NEWBUFFER/CAT/SUBSTR/LEFT/RIGHT, ideally by threading the engine's ExecutionEngineLimits into the semantics layer rather than using the hard-coded 1MB MAX_ITEM_SIZE. Unify all three locations on one constant after fixing finding #1.

consensus_breaking.title: External VM enforces MaxStackSize as eval-stack length, not C# reference count
consensus_breaking.problem: C# enforces MaxStackSize (2048) against ReferenceCounter.Count, which counts the evaluation stack items PLUS slot items PLUS every nested sub-item inside compound types, re-checked after each instruction (ExecutionEngine.cs:300-304; ReferenceCounter.cs). The external VM (neo-vm-rs) instead checks only the evaluation-stack vector length: `if stack.len() > MAX_STACK_SIZE (2048)` (interpreter/executor/mod.rs:144-147; state.rs:108) and performs no reference counting of nested compound elements. A script that builds, e.g., one Array containing 3000 elements has C# Count ~= 3001 (FAULT) but external-VM stack.len() == 1 (HALT). Since pure scripts (those whose only syscalls are external-VM-supported, external_vm.rs:413 script_uses_application_engine_host) are routed to this engine, this desyncs execution from C#.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Either (a) do not route consensus execution through the external VM and use only the neo-core engine (which has a real ReferenceCounter), or (b) implement C#-equivalent reference counting (eval stack + slots + nested compound references with CheckZeroReferred) in neo-vm-rs. Option (a) is strongly preferred given the external VM appears designed for a constrained zk/TEE context (fixed-capacity 64-frame call stack, PolkaVM allocator comments).

consensus_breaking.title: External VM caps call depth at 64 instead of MaxInvocationStackSize=1024
consensus_breaking.problem: C# MaxInvocationStackSize = 1024 (ExecutionEngineLimits.cs:52), enforced in LoadContext (ExecutionEngine.cs:182). The external VM uses a fixed-capacity call stack of MAX_CALL_DEPTH = 64 (neo-vm-rs/src/interpreter/state.rs:110; state/call_stack.rs:14,45,75 'if self.len >= MAX_CALL_DEPTH'). A pure script that recurses via CALL/CALLA to a depth between 65 and 1024 FAULTs in the external VM but HALTs in C#. The neo-core engine itself is correct (execution_engine/context.rs:10 uses limits.max_invocation_stack_size with >= and the 1024 default), so this divergence is specific to the external-VM path.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Remove the external VM from the consensus path (preferred), or raise/par its call-depth limit to 1024 and make it dynamic rather than a fixed-capacity array. If kept, add cross-engine differential tests asserting identical FAULT/HALT for CALL recursion at depths 64, 65, 1023, 1024.

consensus_breaking.title: MaxComparableSize is never enforced by neo-core EQUAL/NOTEQUAL
consensus_breaking.problem: neo-core's StackItem::equals_with_limits ignores the limits argument entirely and delegates to plain deep equality: the parameter is named `_limits` and the body is `self.equals(other)` (neo_vm/stack_item/stack_item.rs:646-652). It is called by EQUAL/NOTEQUAL (neo_vm/jump_table/bitwisee.rs:90,99). C# throws 'operand exceeds the maximum comparable size' when a ByteString/Struct operand size exceeds MaxComparableSize=65536 (ByteString.cs:60-72) and additionally decrements a shared comparable budget plus a per-item count budget (=MaxStackSize) across nested Struct comparisons (Struct.cs:98-130). Consequently, comparing large (>65536 byte) values or deeply/large nested structs HALTs in Rust but FAULTs in C#. The external-VM comparison path (neo-vm-rs/src/semantics/comparison.rs:12 equal_values returns bool with no limits) shares this gap.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Implement C#-equivalent comparison: in equals_with_limits, fault when operand Size > MaxComparableSize, and for Struct comparison maintain the iterative stack with both a count budget (MaxStackSize) and a decrementing maxComparableSize budget exactly as Struct.cs:91-132. Apply the same in the external VM's comparison.rs.

consensus_breaking.title: neo-core MaxStackSize post-instruction check is off-by-one (>= vs >)
consensus_breaking.problem: C# PostExecuteInstruction faults only when CheckZeroReferred() is STRICTLY greater than MaxStackSize: `if (ReferenceCounter.CheckZeroReferred() > Limits.MaxStackSize) throw` (ExecutionEngine.cs:303-304). The neo-core engine uses >=: `if current >= self.limits.max_stack_size as usize { return Err(...MaxStackSize exceed...) }` (neo_vm/execution_engine/execution.rs:239). When the reference count equals exactly 2048, C# does NOT fault but Rust does. A contract that legitimately reaches exactly MaxStackSize references HALTs in C# but FAULTs in Rust, diverging execution.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Change line 239 from `current >= self.limits.max_stack_size as usize` to `current > self.limits.max_stack_size as usize` to match C# strict greater-than. Add a boundary test reaching exactly 2048 references.

consensus_breaking.title: Default MaxComparableSize is 65535, must be 65536
consensus_breaking.problem: C# MaxComparableSize = 65536 (ExecutionEngineLimits.cs:47). neo-vm-rs default sets max_comparable_size: u16::MAX as u32 = 65535 (src/vm/limits.rs:44). Even once MaxComparableSize enforcement is added (see finding #5), the boundary will be off by one: a 65536-byte operand comparison is allowed in C# (Size > limits is 65536 > 65536 = false) but would fault in Rust with the 65535 default. This is latent until enforcement exists, but must be fixed alongside it to avoid a one-byte consensus divergence.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Set neo-vm-rs ExecutionEngineLimits::DEFAULT.max_comparable_size = 65536 (literal), not u16::MAX. Pair with finding #5's enforcement work and a 65536/65537 boundary test.

consensus_breaking.title: ENDTRY executed inside a FINALLY block: Rust pops+jumps, C# faults
consensus_breaking.problem: C# ExecuteEndTry throws 'The opcode ENDTRY can't be executed in a FINALLY block' when the current try context state is Finally (JumpTable.Control.cs:585-586). The neo-core execute_end_try treats state==Finally as a valid case: it pops the try context and jumps to end (neo_vm/execution_engine/exception.rs:77-84) with no error. For an adversarial or malformed script that hits ENDTRY while in a finally block, C# FAULTs while Rust continues, which can diverge HALT/FAULT outcome.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: In execute_end_try, when current_try_snapshot.state() == ExceptionHandlingState::Finally, return an error mirroring C# ('The opcode ENDTRY can't be executed in a FINALLY block') instead of popping and jumping. Add a test script that triggers ENDTRY inside FINALLY.

consensus_breaking.title: Two parallel VM execution engines on the consensus path (neo-vm-rs + neo-core/neo_vm)
consensus_breaking.problem: ApplicationEngine::execute_allow_fault first attempts the external neo-vm-rs interpreter (try_execute_with_external_vm) and only falls back to the neo-core jump_table engine when the script uses unsupported host features (load_execute_storage.rs:93-95; external_vm.rs:254-332, predicate at external_vm.rs:413). This means consensus-critical bytecode runs through one of two complete, independently-maintained VM implementations that must agree with each other AND with C# byte-for-byte. The divergences in findings #2/#3/#4 exist precisely because the two engines enforce limits differently (e.g. MaxItemSize 1MB in external VM vs 65535 in neo-core vs 131070 in C#). This is the structural root cause of the parity gaps and a large ongoing maintenance/audit hazard.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Pick a single canonical execution engine for consensus. Given neo-core's engine already has a real ReferenceCounter, correct MaxInvocationStackSize, and host integration, route ALL consensus execution through it and reserve neo-vm-rs for its intended constrained (zk/TEE/proof) use case, or gate the external-VM fast path behind a strict differential-fuzz harness that proves identical results. Until unified, add cross-engine differential tests over the opcode set with edge-case sizes/depths.

consensus_breaking.title: Block NextConsensus computed from current-round validators, not the next-block validator set
consensus_breaking.problem: At block assembly the header's NextConsensus is set to NativeHelpers::get_bft_address(block_data.validator_pubkeys) where validator_pubkeys are the CURRENT round's validators (neo-consensus prepare_block_data copies self.context.validators). C# ConsensusContext.Reset(0) (ConsensusContext.cs:208-211) instead sets NextConsensus = Contract.GetBFTAddress(ShouldRefreshCommittee(height+1) ? NEO.ComputeNextBlockValidators(...) : NEO.GetNextBlockValidators(...)) — i.e. the validator set that will sign the NEXT block. On a steady-state chain current==next so the two coincide, but at a committee-refresh boundary (height % CommitteeMembersCount == 0, NeoToken.cs:206) where the freshly elected committee differs, the C# header's NextConsensus reflects the new set while the Rust header reflects the old set. NextConsensus is part of the SerializeUnsigned header that the commit signatures sign (Header.cs SerializeUnsigned order Version,PrevHash,MerkleRoot,Timestamp,Nonce,Index,PrimaryIndex,NextConsensus), so the produced block hash and witness diverge from C# on those blocks, producing an invalid/incompatible chain at rotation boundaries.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Compute NextConsensus from the next-block validator set: get_bft_address(ShouldRefreshCommittee(block_index+1) ? compute_next_block_validators : get_next_block_validators) and pass it through BlockData, mirroring C# Reset(0). Keep the current-round validators only for the signing multisig contract.

consensus_breaking.title: load_validators applies the committee-refresh condition to the signing validator set instead of only to NextConsensus
consensus_breaking.problem: ConsensusActor::load_validators(next_index) picks the round's validators via should_refresh_committee(next_index) ? compute_next_block_validators : get_next_block_validators (neo-node/src/consensus.rs:297-312), and these become both the signers and (via the previous finding) NextConsensus. In C#, ConsensusContext.Reset(0) always sets the round Validators = NEO.GetNextBlockValidators(Snapshot) (ConsensusContext.cs:216) — the previously-elected set — and applies the ShouldRefreshCommittee/ComputeNextBlockValidators choice ONLY to NextConsensus (lines 208-211). So on a refresh boundary the C# block is signed by the OLD committee and merely announces the NEW committee in NextConsensus, whereas the Rust node would have the NEW committee both sign and be announced. This changes who is authorized to produce the block at the boundary, diverging from C# consensus.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Always load the round signing validators with get_next_block_validators (no refresh branch); use the refresh-conditional set exclusively for the NextConsensus address. Confirm against C# that ComputeNextBlockValidators is never used to select the current round's signers.

consensus_breaking.title: NamedCurveHash Keccak256 variants use wrong byte values (0x18/0x19 vs C# 122/123)
consensus_breaking.problem: neo-crypto/src/named_curve_hash.rs defines Secp256k1Keccak256 = 0x18 and Secp256r1Keccak256 = 0x19. The C# reference (neo_csharp/src/Neo/SmartContract/Native/NamedCurveHash.cs:22-37) defines secp256k1Keccak256 = 122 (0x7A) and secp256r1Keccak256 = 123 (0x7B). CryptoLib.verifyWithECDsa (crypto_lib.rs:190 parse_named_curve_hash → NamedCurveHash::from_byte) reads the curve selector byte directly from the VM argument. A post-Cockatrice contract that calls verifyWithECDsa(msg, pubkey, sig, 123) — the real secp256r1Keccak256 value — yields from_byte(123)=None in Rust, which returns 0 (verification failure), whereas C# performs a valid Keccak256 ECDSA verification. Conversely values 24/25 (0x18/0x19) are accepted by Rust but rejected by C#. This changes contract execution results and therefore consensus state/VM results for any contract using Keccak256 ECDSA verification (e.g. Ethereum-signature interop). The SHA256 variants (0x16/0x17) are coincidentally correct because C# uses 22/23. The in-tree unit test (named_curve_hash.rs:144-150) even asserts the wrong serialized value ("25" for Secp256r1Keccak256), locking in the bug.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Change the protocol_enum byte values to match C#: Secp256k1Keccak256 = 122 (0x7A), Secp256r1Keccak256 = 123 (0x7B). Update the unit tests (to_byte, from_byte, serde) accordingly and add a cross-check test asserting exact C# values 22/23/122/123. Verify no other code hard-codes 0x18/0x19 for these curves.
consensus_breaking.dimension: Cryptography parity (neo-crypto vs neo_csharp/src/Neo/Cryptography + CryptoLib native)

consensus_breaking.title: secp256k1 ECDSA verification rejects high-s signatures that C# accepts (malleability divergence)
consensus_breaking.problem: Secp256k1Crypto::verify (neo-crypto/src/signature.rs:76-93) uses the libsecp256k1 C-binding crate secp256k1 v0.28.2. That crate's verify_ecdsa requires a low-s normalized signature: its own docs state 'The signature must be normalized or verification will fail' (verified in ~/.cargo/.../secp256k1-0.28.2/src/ecdsa/mod.rs:194). The code does NOT call normalize_s() before verifying. C# Crypto.VerifySignature (neo_csharp/src/Neo/Cryptography/Crypto.cs:140-164) uses .NET ECDsa.VerifyData / BouncyCastle ECDsaSigner.VerifySignature, neither of which enforces low-s — both accept high-s signatures. Therefore a high-s secp256k1 signature that C# Neo accepts is rejected by the Rust node. This path is reachable via CryptoLib.verifyWithECDsa for secp256k1SHA256/secp256k1Keccak256 (crypto_lib.rs:132 → Crypto::verify_signature_with_curve → verify_ecdsa_raw64_with_hash secp256k1 arms → Secp256k1Crypto::verify) and via Crypto::verify_signature_bytes used by the Notary native contract (notary.rs:632). The dominant secp256r1 path is NOT affected because it uses RustCrypto p256 whose ecdsa-0.16.9 verify_prehashed performs plain ECDSA without a low-s check (verified in ~/.cargo/.../ecdsa-0.16.9/src/hazmat.rs:270-292), matching C#.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Make secp256k1 verification s-agnostic to match C#. Either (a) call sig.normalize_s() on a clone before verify_ecdsa in Secp256k1Crypto::verify and in the secp256k1 arms of verify_ecdsa_raw64_with_hash, or (b) switch the secp256k1 verification path to RustCrypto k256 (which, like p256, does not reject high-s) instead of the libsecp256k1 binding — note ecc.rs already has a k256-based verify_signature_secp256k1. Add a regression test that constructs a high-s secp256k1 signature and asserts it verifies true.
consensus_breaking.dimension: Cryptography parity (neo-crypto vs neo_csharp/src/Neo/Cryptography + CryptoLib native)

consensus_breaking.title: Block import re-verifies every transaction (state-independent + state-dependent); C# does not
consensus_breaking.problem: C# Block.Verify is literally `return Header.Verify(...)` (Block.cs:170-178). During OnNewBlock/OnImport C# never calls VerifyStateIndependent/VerifyStateDependent on the block's transactions, and Persist (Blockchain.cs:435-453) only EXECUTES tx scripts (Application trigger) — a faulting tx is committed as a no-op, the block still persists. The Rust Block::verify_internal calls verify_transactions (block/verification.rs:197-253) which runs verify_state_independent on every tx (incl. signature checks) and then verify_state_dependent_at_height (expiry, policy-blocked sender, attribute verify, fee/witness checks) and rejects the whole block on any failure. This path is live: block_processing.rs:64 and handlers.rs:241 call block.verify(_with_cache) on the import/relay path. A block that C# accepts but that contains a tx now failing state-dependent re-verification (e.g. ValidUntilBlock relative to the persisting snapshot, a sender blocked by Policy after consensus, or insufficient-funds after conflict reordering) is rejected by Rust -> chain halt/fork.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Make Rust Block::verify match C# semantics: it should perform only header verification (Header::verify/verify_with_cache). Remove per-transaction verify_state_independent/verify_state_dependent from the block-import path. Transaction validity is guaranteed by consensus pre-verification + the deserialization-time merkle/duplicate checks; block import must only validate the header witness and chain continuity.
consensus_breaking.dimension: Ledger / blockchain semantics parity (Block, Transaction, Header, TransactionAttribute, MemoryPool, verification pipeline, genesis)

consensus_breaking.title: Fabricated 2MB MAX_BLOCK_SIZE enforced on block deserialize and verify (no such consensus rule in C#)
consensus_breaking.problem: Rust hard-caps blocks at MAX_BLOCK_SIZE=2_097_152 and enforces it both in Block::deserialize (serialization.rs:38-44,52-57) and Block::verify (validate_block_size). C# core has NO block-byte-size consensus limit: the only hard limit is the P2P Message.PayloadMaxSize=0x02000000 (32MB, Message.cs:30) which Rust already mirrors correctly (message.rs:20). C#'s MaxBlockSize is a DBFT *plugin* setting (default 262144 = 256KB; DbftSettings.cs:43) used only when PRODUCING a block, never when receiving one. Thus any valid block between 2MB and 32MB is accepted by C# but rejected by Rust at parse time -> fork. The constants.rs comment falsely claims 2MB is 'Neo N3 ProtocolSettings.Default'.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Remove the 2MB block-size gate from Block::deserialize and Block::verify. Rely solely on the P2P PAYLOAD_MAX_SIZE (32MB) limit at the message layer, exactly as C# does. Delete/correct the misleading MAX_BLOCK_SIZE comment.
consensus_breaking.dimension: Ledger / blockchain semantics parity (Block, Transaction, Header, TransactionAttribute, MemoryPool, verification pipeline, genesis)

consensus_breaking.title: Header/Block verify rejects future timestamps (15-min drift) and small witness scripts; C# checks neither
consensus_breaking.problem: Header::verify and verify_with_cache call validate_self -> validate_timestamp_bounds and validate_witness_scripts (header/verification.rs:87-97). validate_timestamp_bounds (validation.rs:163-184) rejects any header whose timestamp is < MIN_TIMESTAMP_MS or > now + 15min (MAX_TIMESTAMP_DRIFT_MS). validate_witness_scripts (validation.rs:287-325) rejects witness invocation/verification scripts > 1024 bytes and any verification script whose first byte is 0xFF. C# Header.Verify (Header.cs:203-226) does NONE of this — it only checks PrimaryIndex<ValidatorsCount, prev exists, prev.Index+1==Index, prev.Hash==PrevHash, prev.Timestamp>=Timestamp (strictly increasing), and the witness. A block whose timestamp is legitimately ahead of the receiving node's wall clock (common during sync or clock skew) is accepted by C# but rejected by Rust -> halt. The 1024-byte/0xFF witness gates are similarly absent in C# and could reject valid consensus multisig witnesses.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Strip validate_timestamp_bounds and validate_witness_scripts from header verification. Keep only the C# checks: primary_index < validators_count, prev block continuity, strictly-increasing timestamp (validate_timestamp_progression), and witness verification. The timestamp future-bound and witness-size heuristics are not part of Neo consensus.
consensus_breaking.dimension: Ledger / blockchain semantics parity (Block, Transaction, Header, TransactionAttribute, MemoryPool, verification pipeline, genesis)

consensus_breaking.title: OracleResponse::verify omits 3 of 5 C# checks (request existence, fee match, designated-oracle signer)
consensus_breaking.problem: C# OracleResponse.Verify performs five checks: (1) all signers Scopes==None, (2) tx.Script == FixedScript (Oracle.finish call), (3) Oracle.GetRequest(snapshot, Id) is non-null, (4) tx.NetworkFee + tx.SystemFee == request.GasForResponse, (5) a signer equals the BFT address of the role-designated Oracle nodes. The Rust OracleResponse::verify (oracle_response.rs:69-88) implements only checks (1) and (2), then returns true with a comment acknowledging the rest is unimplemented. Consequently a Rust node admits/relays oracle-response transactions that C# rejects — including responses not signed by the designated oracle nodes, with wrong fees, or for non-existent requests. On any oracle-enabled network this admits invalid transactions into the mempool and passes them through block-import attribute verification.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Implement the missing checks: look up the oracle request via OracleContract::get_request(snapshot, id) and fail if absent; verify network_fee + system_fee == request.gas_for_response; resolve the Oracle role's designated nodes via RoleManagement.GetDesignatedByRole(Oracle, current_index+1), compute the BFT address, and require a matching signer. Use the snapshot and settings parameters that are currently ignored.
consensus_breaking.dimension: Ledger / blockchain semantics parity (Block, Transaction, Header, TransactionAttribute, MemoryPool, verification pipeline, genesis)

consensus_breaking.title: MemoryPool block-import enforces MaxTransactionsPerBlock as a hard import gate (C# uses it only for reverify sizing)
consensus_breaking.problem: Rust Block::verify_internal calls validate_transaction_count (block/verification.rs:55) which rejects any block with > MAX_TRANSACTIONS_PER_BLOCK (512) transactions. In C# core, MaxTransactionsPerBlock is referenced ONLY in MemoryPool reverification sizing (MemoryPool.cs:567,687) and DBFT block production — never as a block-import validity gate (grep of Neo core confirms no other usage). While legitimately consensus-produced blocks stay <=512, a block the network accepts with more txs (e.g. after a settings change or a non-DBFT producer) would be rejected by Rust but not by C#.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Remove validate_transaction_count from the block-import verification path. The transaction-count ceiling belongs to consensus block production and mempool reverify sizing, not to block import validity.
consensus_breaking.dimension: Ledger / blockchain semantics parity (Block, Transaction, Header, TransactionAttribute, MemoryPool, verification pipeline, genesis)

consensus_breaking.title: StorageItem carries an is_constant flag that does not exist in C# v3.9 (latent serialization footgun)
consensus_breaking.problem: neo-storage/src/types/storage_item.rs:31 defines `is_constant: bool` and `StorageValue::to_storage_bytes()` (line 245) serializes `[is_constant_byte] ++ value`. The in-tree C# StorageItem (neo_csharp/src/Neo/SmartContract/StorageItem.cs) has NO IsConstant field at all in v3.9 and its only serialization is `Serialize(writer) => writer.Write(Value.Span)` (line 273-276) — raw value bytes with no flag. The `Constant` concept was removed from Neo's StorageItem. If `to_storage_bytes()`/`from_storage_bytes()` were ever wired into the DB or MPT value path (they currently are not — the live path uses `to_value()`), every stored value would gain a leading byte and corrupt state root / break interop. The `is_constant` field also pollutes PartialEq (line 186) so two items with identical bytes but different flags compare unequal, which diverges from C# StorageItem semantics.
consensus_breaking.recommendation: Remove the `is_constant` field and `constant()` constructor from StorageItem to match C# v3.9. If a non-consensus 'constant' marker is needed for the syscall layer, track it outside the persisted type. At minimum, delete the `StorageValue::to_storage_bytes/from_storage_bytes` impl for StorageItem (storage_item.rs:244-268) so no code can accidentally serialize the flag, and drop `is_constant` from the PartialEq.

interop_breaking.title: HF_Gorgon hardfork defined in C# enum but Rust handling unverified
interop_breaking.problem: C# Hardfork enum includes HF_Gorgon (Hardfork.cs:22) as the 7th hardfork. Rust hardfork.rs comments claim HfGorgon is defined but not activated (lines 78,92) and Hardfork::all() returns 7 variants, but the audit did not confirm the neo_primitives Hardfork enum actually contains HfGorgon with the correct ordinal/byte value used in _usedHardforks ordering and is_active comparisons. A missing or mis-ordered Gorgon variant would not matter today (unscheduled) but would break parity once Gorgon ships.
interop_breaking.recommendation: Verify neo_primitives::Hardfork defines HfGorgon as the last variant with byte value 6 (Aspidochelone=0..Gorgon=6) matching C# enum ordinals, since native _usedHardforks ordering sorts by (byte)hardfork. Add a test asserting Rust hardfork byte values equal the C# enum positions.

interop_breaking.title: Duplicate RawMessage in neo-p2p uses wrong LZ4 compression boundary (missing 64-byte threshold + wrong >= min-size comparison)
interop_breaking.problem: neo-p2p/src/message.rs RawMessage::to_bytes (lines 52-72) compresses when payload.len() >= COMPRESSION_MIN_SIZE (128) and keeps the compressed form whenever compressed.len() < self.payload.len(). The C# reference (Message.cs:100-107) compresses only when _payloadCompressed.Length > CompressionMinSize (strict >, i.e. > 128) AND keeps it only when compressed.Length < length - CompressionThreshold (a 64-byte margin). RawMessage therefore (a) compresses a 128-byte payload that C# would not, and (b) keeps marginally-compressed payloads C# would leave uncompressed. It also ignores the per-command compression whitelist (allows_compression) entirely, unlike C#'s ShallICompress. This would produce a different MessageFlags byte and different wire bytes vs C# for affected payloads. The code is dead in the live path (only re-exported at neo-p2p/src/lib.rs:232, never used) but is public API and a latent interop trap. The correct implementation already exists in neo-core/src/network/p2p/message.rs (lines 97-107), which is the path actually used by connection.rs/framed_codec.rs.
interop_breaking.recommendation: Either delete RawMessage entirely (it is unused) or fix its to_bytes to match C#: gate on command.allows_compression(), use strict payload.len() > COMPRESSION_MIN_SIZE, and require compressed.len() + COMPRESSION_THRESHOLD < payload.len() before setting COMPRESSED. Preferably remove it to eliminate the duplicate Message implementation and have neo-core consume the canonical neo-p2p type.

interop_breaking.title: RecoveryMessage compact arrays are sorted by validator_index before serialization (C# uses Dictionary insertion order)
interop_breaking.problem: RecoveryMessage::serialize sorts change_view_messages, preparation_messages, and commit_messages by validator_index before writing (neo-consensus/src/messages/recovery.rs:228-259). C# writes Dictionary.Values.ToArray() in insertion order, not sorted (RecoveryMessage.cs:117-131). The RecoveryMessage is gossip used to reconstruct state and is NOT a consensus-signed artifact (only the ExtensiblePayload witness over the message bytes is signed, and a node re-derives individual signed payloads from the compacts), so differing element order does not affect block hashes or consensus safety. However it means a Rust-serialized RecoveryMessage will not be byte-identical to the C# one for the same logical content, which can complicate cross-impl test vectors and any hash-based dedup of recovery payloads.
interop_breaking.recommendation: If exact byte parity of RecoveryMessage with C# is desired (e.g. for shared test vectors), preserve insertion/collection order rather than sorting; otherwise document explicitly that recovery payload byte-order intentionally differs and is consensus-irrelevant.

interop_breaking.title: NEP-2 key encryption uses AES-256-CBC (zero IV) instead of C# AES-256-ECB
interop_breaking.problem: NEP-2 encrypt/decrypt in neo-core/src/wallets/key_pair.rs uses the cbc crate: Encryptor::<Aes256>::new_from_slices(derived_half2, &[0u8;16]) and Decryptor with NoPadding (encrypt_nep2 lines 265-275, decrypt_nep2 lines 327-333). The C# reference KeyPair.Encrypt (neo_csharp/src/Neo/Wallets/KeyPair.cs:140-148) uses Aes with CipherMode.ECB and PaddingMode.None. The plaintext is the 32-byte XOR(privkey, derivedhalf1), i.e. two AES blocks. With a zero IV, CBC block 1 equals ECB block 1, but CBC block 2 XORs the plaintext with block 1's ciphertext before encryption, so byte range [16..32] of the encrypted key differs from ECB. Result: Rust-produced NEP-2 keys cannot be imported by C#/standard wallets, and Rust cannot decrypt standard NEP-2 keys (the Rust decrypt is symmetric so it round-trips only with itself; the address-hash check at decrypt_nep2:349 will fail against a genuine C# NEP-2 string). This breaks wallet portability for the NEP-2 export/import format.
interop_breaking.recommendation: Replace the cbc Encryptor/Decryptor with raw AES-256-ECB no-padding over the two 16-byte blocks (e.g. aes::Aes256 block cipher encrypting each 16-byte block independently). Add an interop test using a known C# NEP-2 vector (passphrase, address version, expected encrypted key string) to lock parity.
interop_breaking.dimension: Cryptography parity (neo-crypto vs neo_csharp/src/Neo/Cryptography + CryptoLib native)

interop_breaking.title: WitnessCondition Group/CalledByGroup reject uncompressed (65-byte) ECPoints that C# accepts
interop_breaking.problem: C# GroupCondition/CalledByGroupCondition deserialize the group via ECPoint.DeserializeFrom -> DecodePoint, which accepts BOTH compressed (prefix 0x02/0x03, 33 bytes) and uncompressed (prefix 0x04, 65 bytes) encodings (neo_csharp/src/Neo/Cryptography/ECC/ECPoint.cs:154-163, 78-103). The Rust deserializer reads the correct length via read_group_bytes (helpers.rs:39-51, which does handle 0x04 -> 65 bytes), but then deserialize_group_bytes rejects anything != 33 bytes with 'Invalid ECPoint length' (witness_rule/serialization.rs:71-77, 117-119,124-126). A transaction/witness-rule carrying an uncompressed group point is therefore accepted by C# nodes but rejected by Rust, so a block containing such a tx would fork the network (Rust rejects, C# accepts). Serialize side also hard-requires 33 bytes (serialization.rs:33-39).
interop_breaking.recommendation: Make Group/CalledByGroup deserialization accept both 33-byte and 65-byte ECPoint encodings exactly like C# ECPoint.DecodePoint: decode the point with the curve (validating it lies on secp256r1) and store/normalize it. Do not reject 65-byte uncompressed points. Ensure round-trip serialization preserves the original encoding C# would emit (C# re-serializes via ECPoint which uses compressed form, so confirm whether the wire byte form must be re-emitted compressed; match C#'s ISerializable.Serialize behavior).

interop_breaking.title: Signer.allowed_groups deserialization reads fixed 33 bytes, misaligns stream on uncompressed ECPoints
interop_breaking.problem: C# Signer.Deserialize reads AllowedGroups via reader.ReadSerializableArray<ECPoint>(MaxSubitems) (neo_csharp/src/Neo/Network/P2P/Payloads/Signer.cs:115-116), where each ECPoint accepts 33-byte compressed OR 65-byte uncompressed encodings. The Rust Signer deserialize reads exactly ECPOINT_COMPRESSED_SIZE (33) bytes per group regardless of the encoding prefix (neo-core/src/network/p2p/payloads/signer.rs:387-391 via reader.read_bytes(ECPOINT_COMPRESSED_SIZE)). If a signer carries an uncompressed (0x04, 65-byte) group point, Rust consumes only 33 bytes and then misreads the remainder of the signer/transaction stream, producing a different (failed) deserialization than C#. This is the same encoding-parity gap as the WitnessCondition Group case and is consensus-relevant because Signer is part of the transaction hash preimage.
interop_breaking.recommendation: Read each allowed-group ECPoint with prefix-aware length detection (33 vs 65 bytes) and decode/validate on secp256r1, mirroring C# ECPoint.DeserializeFrom. Reuse the same shared ECPoint deserialization helper used for the WitnessCondition fix so both code paths stay consistent.

interop_breaking.title: Duplicate Message type in neo-p2p has incorrect LZ4 compression gating (parity divergence vs C#)
interop_breaking.problem: Two Message implementations coexist: the live neo-core NetworkMessage (neo-core/src/network/p2p/message.rs:97-107) correctly mirrors C# Message.Create — gating on command.allows_compression(), using strict '> COMPRESSION_MIN_SIZE', and requiring 'compressed.len() + COMPRESSION_THRESHOLD < payload.len()' (i.e. must save > 64 bytes). The separate neo-p2p crate's Message::to_bytes (neo-p2p/src/message.rs:52-72) does NOT gate on allows_compression(), uses '>= COMPRESSION_MIN_SIZE' instead of '>', and uses 'compressed.len() < payload.len()' with no 64-byte threshold. Per C# (neo_csharp/src/Neo/Network/P2P/Message.cs:87,100,103) all three rules are required; the neo-p2p version would emit the COMPRESSED flag for messages C# would send uncompressed, producing different wire bytes. The neo-p2p Message::to_bytes is not on the active connection path (connection.rs uses NetworkMessage), so this is currently a latent duplication hazard rather than a live wire bug.
interop_breaking.recommendation: Eliminate the duplicate Message wire codec: have neo-p2p re-export/use the neo-core NetworkMessage serialization (or move the canonical Message codec into neo-p2p and have neo-core depend on it), so there is a single compression decision implementation. If neo-p2p must keep its own Message, fix to_bytes to apply allows_compression() gating, strict '>' min-size, and the COMPRESSION_THRESHOLD savings requirement.

interop_breaking.title: Block deserialization does not enforce merkle-root match nor reject duplicate transactions
interop_breaking.problem: C# Block.Deserialize -> DeserializeTransactions (Block.cs:104-126) throws FormatException if MerkleTree.ComputeRoot(tx hashes) != Header.MerkleRoot, and throws if any duplicate tx hash is present — both at parse time. Rust Block::deserialize (serialization.rs:23-66) reads transactions and returns Ok without computing/comparing the merkle root and without a duplicate-hash check. These checks are deferred to Block::verify (verify_merkle_root/verify_no_duplicate_transactions). This means a block with a tampered merkle root or duplicate txs is byte-accepted by Rust but rejected at the wire level by C#. Any code path that deserializes a block without later calling verify (relay, tooling, RPC) would propagate an inconsistent block, and the accept/reject boundary differs from C# at the deserialization layer.
interop_breaking.recommendation: Move the merkle-root recomputation/comparison and duplicate-tx rejection into Block::deserialize to fail fast exactly like C# DeserializeTransactions, returning IoError on mismatch. Keep verify_merkle_root/verify_no_duplicate_transactions as defense-in-depth but make the deserializer the authoritative gate matching C#.
interop_breaking.dimension: Ledger / blockchain semantics parity (Block, Transaction, Header, TransactionAttribute, MemoryPool, verification pipeline, genesis)

interop_breaking.title: getunclaimedgas returns decimal GAS instead of raw datoshi
interop_breaking.problem: C# GetUnclaimedGas returns unclaimed.ToString() of the raw datoshi BigInteger (100000000 for 1 GAS). Rust unclaimed_gas.rs line 38 returns BigDecimal::new(unclaimed, decimals).to_string(); the BigDecimal Display in neo-primitives/src/big_decimal.rs divides by 10^8 and trims trailing zeros so 100000000 renders as 1. Any non-zero unclaimed balance mismatches C#. The unit test only checks unclaimed is a string on a zero-balance server where BigDecimal of 0 equals 0, masking the bug.
interop_breaking.recommendation: Return the raw integer string unclaimed.to_string() instead of wrapping in BigDecimal; add a test asserting the exact string for a known non-zero datoshi amount.

interop_breaking.title: getrawtransaction verbose emits an extra vmstate field not present in C#
interop_breaking.problem: C# GetRawTransaction verbose (RpcServer.Blockchain.cs lines 373 to 381) adds only blockhash, confirmations and blocktime to Transaction.ToJson; Transaction.ToJson has no vmstate. Rust rpc_server_blockchain/mod.rs lines 267 to 276 additionally inserts a vmstate field. Strict clients and response-diff tooling see an unexpected field.
interop_breaking.recommendation: Remove the vmstate insertion to match C# v3.9, or gate it behind a non-default compatibility flag and adjust the dedicated test.

interop_breaking.title: Relay-result error path drops the reason data and message suffix C# attaches
interop_breaking.problem: C# GetRelayResult (RpcServer.Node.cs lines 82 to 111) throws every non-success case with WithData(reason.ToString()), so the message gains a suffix and a data field. Rust map_relay_result (rpc_relay.rs lines 18 to 27) omits with_data for AlreadyExists, AlreadyInPool, OutOfMemory, InvalidScript, InvalidAttribute, InvalidSignature, OverSize, Expired, InsufficientFunds and PolicyFail, so both the message suffix and the data field are missing. Affects sendrawtransaction and submitblock error responses.
interop_breaking.recommendation: Append with_data of the matching VerifyResult variant name to every error arm in map_relay_result so message and data match C#.

interop_breaking.title: Block/header JSON wire format is encoded in three independent places (drift risk)
interop_breaking.problem: The consensus/interop-visible block & header JSON shape is implemented three times: (1) server-authoritative builder `header_fields_to_map`/`block_to_json` in src/server/rpc_server_blockchain/mod.rs:621-662 (e.g. nonce as `format!("{:016X}")`, fields hash/size/version/previousblockhash/merkleroot/time/nonce/index/primary/nextconsensus/witnesses/confirmations/nextblockhash); (2) client parser/serializer `RpcBlockHeader::from_json`/`to_json` in src/client/models/rpc_block_header.rs:37+ (parses nonce via from_str_radix base16); (3) a `#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize)]` on the same struct (rpc_block_header.rs:22). Unlike C#, where serialization lives once on the core type (`block.ToJson(system.Settings)` then RpcServer just appends confirmations/nextblockhash — neo_csharp/src/Plugins/RpcServer/RpcServer.Blockchain.cs:103,230), neo-core's Block/Header have NO to_json (transactions do: src/network/p2p/payloads/transaction/json.rs, and the server reuses it at rpc_server_blockchain/mod.rs:601). The three copies can drift in field names/encodings and silently break interop.
interop_breaking.recommendation: Add `to_json(&ProtocolSettings)` to neo-core Block/Header (matching the existing Transaction::to_json), make the RPC server call it and append only confirmations/nextblockhash (as C# does), and have the client model parse that same canonical output. Eliminate the server's hand-built header map and the client's parallel to_json.

interop_breaking.title: RPC JSON output escaping diverges from C# Utf8JsonWriter (JavaScriptEncoder.Default)
interop_breaking.problem: C# serializes all RPC responses via JToken.ToString -> ToByteArray -> Utf8JsonWriter (neo_csharp/src/Neo.Json/JToken.cs:240-247, and RpcServer.cs:310 `response.ToString()`). The default Utf8JsonWriter encoder is JavaScriptEncoder.Default, which escapes '<', '>', '&', '\'', '+' and ALL non-ASCII characters to \uXXXX. The Rust RPC server instead builds a serde_json::Value and serializes it with serde_json::to_vec (neo-rpc/src/server/routes/mod.rs:172), and serde_json only escapes the JSON-mandatory set (\", \\, control chars) while emitting raw UTF-8 for non-ASCII and leaving <, >, &, +, ' unescaped. Any RPC field carrying a non-ASCII or special character (NEP-17/NEP-11 token name/symbol, contract manifest, notification string args, error data) yields byte-different responses. neo-json's own JToken serializer (j_token.rs:270-300) also uses serde_json's PrettyFormatter and therefore has the same divergence, so it would not fix it either.
interop_breaking.recommendation: Match C#'s escaping for RPC output. Implement a serde_json custom Formatter that escapes '<','>','&','+','\'' and all code points > 0x7F as \uXXXX (surrogate pairs for astral chars), matching JavaScriptEncoder.Default, and route all RPC response serialization (and neo-json JToken::to_byte_array) through it. Add round-trip parity tests against C# output for strings containing '<', '&', and non-ASCII (e.g. a token symbol like an emoji or CJK).
interop_breaking.dimension: Foundation crates cohesion + idiomatic Rust + ecosystem reuse

interop_breaking.title: Duplicate, divergent ProtocolSettings in neo-config vs neo-core (RPC client signs with the wrong one)
interop_breaking.problem: Two ProtocolSettings structs exist. The canonical consensus one is neo-core/src/protocol_settings.rs (19.5K): standby_committee: Vec<ECPoint>, validators_count: i32, hardforks: HashMap<Hardfork,u32>, memory_pool_max_transactions: i32, initial_gas_distribution: u64, with defaults matching C# (max_transactions_per_block=512, memory_pool=50_000, max_valid_until_block_increment=5760, max_traceable_blocks=2_102_400). The second is neo-config/src/protocol.rs: standby_validators: Vec<String> (hex), validators_count: u32, custom HardforkHeights/NativeActivationHeights structs, memory_pool_max_transactions: u32, initial_gas_distribution: i64, ms_per_block: u64. These model the same protocol concept with different field names, types, and a fundamentally different validator/hardfork representation. The neo-rpc CLIENT (transaction_manager.rs:58/204/209, utility.rs:78/144) reads neo_config::ProtocolSettings.network and .address_version to compute transaction sign data and script hashes. If the two ever drift, client-side signing produces invalid signatures / wrong addresses. Magic values currently agree (860833102) but are duplicated as literals in neo-config/protocol.rs:193,249 instead of referencing neo_primitives::constants::MAINNET_MAGIC/TESTNET_MAGIC (which the neo-core path uses).
interop_breaking.recommendation: Pick one canonical ProtocolSettings. Preferred: make neo-config own the single ProtocolSettings (aligned field-for-field with C# ProtocolSettings: StandbyCommittee as ECPoint, Hardforks as Hardfork-enum map, matching integer signedness) and have neo-core re-export/consume it instead of defining its own. At minimum, replace neo-config's hardcoded magic literals with neo_primitives::constants references and add a conversion + equality test asserting the two structs produce identical network/address_version/defaults to prevent signing drift.
interop_breaking.dimension: Foundation crates cohesion + idiomatic Rust + ecosystem reuse

interop_breaking.title: Duplicated ProtocolSettings: neo-config copy (used by RPC client for tx building) vs neo-core copy (used by NeoSystem/consensus)
interop_breaking.problem: Two independent ProtocolSettings types exist with overlapping consensus-critical fields. neo-core::protocol_settings::ProtocolSettings (484 LOC) is the authority for the running node — used by NeoSystem (neo-core/src/neo_system/core.rs:82,126,171,182,361,550). neo-config::ProtocolSettings (379 LOC) is a SEPARATE struct imported at 22 sites in the neo-rpc CLIENT (e.g. neo-rpc/src/client/rpc_client/client.rs:21, transaction_manager.rs:304, utility.rs:49) to build and sign transactions. Each maintains its OWN hardcoded mainnet/testnet defaults: neo-config/src/protocol.rs:193,249 hardcodes network=860833102/894710606 and default_address_version()=0x35 / default_max_valid_until_block_increment()=5760 (protocol.rs:140,148), while neo-core derives the same from neo_primitives::constants (MAINNET_MAGIC=0x334F454E=860833102, ADDRESS_VERSION=0x35, in neo-primitives/src/constants.rs:70,182). The values coincide today, but they are two unsynchronized sources of truth. If a future change (or a network parameter like address_version or max_valid_until_block_increment) is updated in neo-primitives/neo-core but not in neo-config's hardcoded copy, the RPC client will build transactions with a wrong magic or validUntilBlock window — rejected by the network or signed against the wrong network (replay/interop hazard).
interop_breaking.recommendation: Collapse to one ProtocolSettings type. Make neo-config the single definition (it is the correct layer for configuration) and have neo-core re-export/wrap it, OR have neo-config's mainnet()/testnet() defaults read from neo-primitives::constants instead of re-hardcoding magic/address_version/max_traceable_blocks. At minimum, add a cross-crate test asserting neo_config::ProtocolSettings::mainnet() field-by-field equals neo_core::ProtocolSettings::mainnet() for network, address_version, max_valid_until_block_increment, max_traceable_blocks, and validators_count, so divergence fails CI.

all_findings.title: Rust fabricates HF_Faun activation heights absent from C# v3.9.1 config (hard fork at Faun height)
all_findings.problem: neo-core/src/hardfork.rs hardcodes HfFaun at mainnet block 8800000 (line 77) and testnet 12960000 (line 91). C# v3.9.1 src/Neo.CLI/config.mainnet.json and config.testnet.json schedule hardforks ONLY through HF_Echidna (mainnet 7300000, testnet 5870000); there is NO HF_Faun (or HF_Gorgon) entry in either file. At block 8800000 (mainnet) the Rust node would activate Faun and, via ContractManagement OnPersist initialization (contract_management/native_impl.rs:257 calling is_initialize_block), deploy Treasury + TokenManagement, switch StdLib to expose hexEncode/hexDecode, and change Policy MaxExecFeeFactor scaling (PolicyContract.cs:517) — none of which C# v3.9.1 nodes would do. This produces an immediately divergent state root: a guaranteed chain split.
all_findings.recommendation: Do not hardcode Faun/Gorgon heights. Source hardfork heights exclusively from the loaded ProtocolSettings/config JSON (matching C# behavior where omitted hardforks are disabled). Leave HfFaun and HfGorgon unscheduled for mainnet/testnet until the official C# config adds them, and verify against the in-tree neo_csharp/src/Neo.CLI/config.*.json at build/test time.

all_findings.title: Fabricated native contract TokenManagement (id -12) does not exist in C#
all_findings.problem: neo-core registers a 12th native contract TokenManagement (native/token_management/mod.rs:37 ID=-12, hash 0xae00c57daeb20f9b654f3265a918f44a8a40e049, active_in=HfFaun) and lists it in NativeRegistry::register_standard_contracts (native/mod.rs:190). No TokenManagement exists anywhere in neo_csharp (grep across the whole C# tree returns zero matches). C# v3.9.1 has exactly 11 native contracts: ContractManagement(-1), StdLib(-2), CryptoLib(-3), Ledger(-4), NEO(-5), GAS(-6), Policy(-7), RoleManagement(-8), Oracle(-9), Notary(-10), Treasury(-11), per NativeContract.cs idCounter (`Id { get; } = --idCounter`, line 144) and the static-property construction order (lines 61-111). If Faun ever activates, Rust deploys a native contract and its storage that C# never has, diverging the state root.
all_findings.recommendation: Remove the TokenManagement native contract entirely (delete native/token_management/, the mod.rs declarations, registry registration, and the -12 id). It is not part of the Neo v3.9 protocol.

all_findings.title: StdLib base64UrlEncode/base64UrlDecode gated at HfCockatrice instead of HF_Echidna
all_findings.problem: Rust gates base64UrlEncode/base64UrlDecode with active = HfCockatrice (native/std_lib/metadata.rs:20-21). C# gates both with Hardfork.HF_Echidna (StdLib.cs:143,154). Between the Cockatrice (mainnet 5450000) and Echidna (7300000) activation heights, the Rust StdLib native manifest/NEF would include these two methods while C#'s would not. This changes the StdLib NEF dummy-script bytes (extra PUSH0/SYSCALL/RET per method shifts all subsequent method offsets) and the manifest method set, producing a different StdLib ContractState and a different state root for that entire ~1.85M-block window; calls to these methods would also succeed in Rust but FAULT in C#.
all_findings.recommendation: Change active = HfCockatrice to active = HfEchidna for base64UrlEncode and base64UrlDecode in native/std_lib/metadata.rs to match StdLib.cs.

all_findings.title: OracleContract.request enforces wrong userData limit (65535 raw bytes) vs C# (512 serialized bytes)
all_findings.problem: Rust request() checks user_data.len() > self.config.max_user_data_length where the default is BLOCK_MAX_TX_WIRE_LIMIT = 65535 (oracle_contract/config.rs:38, request.rs:65) against the RAW argument bytes. C# does not bound the raw bytes at all; instead it serializes the userData StackItem via BinarySerializer.Serialize(userData, MaxUserDataLength=512, MaxStackSize) (OracleContract.cs:36-39,265), faulting if the SERIALIZED form exceeds 512 bytes.
