So a request with serialized userData of, e.g., 600 bytes succeeds in Rust but faults in C#; and raw-byte measurement differs from serialized-StackItem measurement even below 512. Either case changes VM fault outcome -> divergent execution result.
all_findings.recommendation: Set the Oracle userData limit to 512 and measure the BinarySerializer-serialized form of the userData StackItem (not the raw argument length), exactly mirroring OracleContract.cs:265's BinarySerializer.Serialize(userData, 512, MaxStackSize). Also align url/filter/callback size checks to C#'s GetStrictUtf8ByteCount semantics.

all_findings.title: StdLib MaxLength(1024) enforced on params C# does not annotate (memorySearch.value, stringSplit.separator, jsonDeserialize)
all_findings.problem: C# applies [MaxLength(1024)] only to specific parameters: MemorySearch annotates ONLY mem (StdLib.cs:223,229,235), StringSplit annotates ONLY str (lines 250,256), and JsonDeserialize has NO [MaxLength] at all (line 53, uses JToken.Parse(json,10)). Rust over-enforces: it caps memorySearch BOTH mem and value (std_lib/memory.rs:43-44), stringSplit BOTH str and separator (strings.rs:31-32), and jsonDeserialize input (serialization.rs:72). A memorySearch with a >1024-byte value, a stringSplit with a >1024-byte separator, or a jsonDeserialize of >1024-byte JSON would FAULT in Rust but execute in C# -> divergent VM result.
all_findings.recommendation: Remove ensure_max_input_len on memorySearch's value arg (keep only mem), on stringSplit's separator arg (keep only str), and on jsonDeserialize entirely. Match the exact set of [MaxLength]-annotated parameters in StdLib.cs.

all_findings.title: Extra ContractManagement storage prefix PREFIX_CONTRACT_COUNT (16) not present in C#
all_findings.problem: Rust defines const PREFIX_CONTRACT_COUNT: u8 = 16 (contract_management/mod.rs:35) plus a contract-count storage key (mod.rs:100). C# ContractManagement only has prefixes 20 (MinimumDeploymentFee), 15 (NextAvailableId), 8 (Contract), 12 (ContractHash) — there is no count prefix. A code comment (native_impl.rs:360) states the count is intentionally NOT written to storage, so this currently appears dormant; but if any code path ever writes PREFIX_CONTRACT_COUNT it would create a storage entry C# never has, diverging the state root. It is also a latent foot-gun.
all_findings.recommendation: Remove PREFIX_CONTRACT_COUNT and the associated key helper, or assert in tests that it is never persisted. Compute contract count by iterating PREFIX_CONTRACT like C# (GetContractById/ListContracts) rather than maintaining a separate counter.

all_findings.title: Oracle config carries non-C# tunables (MAX_PENDING_PER_URL, request_timeout, max_response_length) that risk drift
all_findings.problem: OracleConfig introduces fields with no C# analog or with names that obscure the C# constant: MAX_PENDING_PER_URL=256 (oracle_contract.rs:30) mirrors C#'s inline `list.Count >= 256` (OracleContract.cs:274) but is now a configurable field; request_timeout=144 and max_response_length=MAX_SCRIPT_SIZE have no v3.9.1 consensus role. max_callback_length=HASH_SIZE relies on HASH_SIZE==32 to match C#'s MaxCallbackLength=32. Making these consensus constants runtime-configurable invites accidental divergence and obscures parity review.
all_findings.recommendation: Convert the consensus-relevant Oracle limits (256 pending, 256 url, 128 filter, 32 callback, 512 userData) to hard constants named after the C# fields, and drop config fields that have no C# consensus meaning. Keep them out of any user-tunable config surface.

all_findings.title: HF_Gorgon hardfork defined in C# enum but Rust handling unverified
all_findings.problem: C# Hardfork enum includes HF_Gorgon (Hardfork.cs:22) as the 7th hardfork. Rust hardfork.rs comments claim HfGorgon is defined but not activated (lines 78,92) and Hardfork::all() returns 7 variants, but the audit did not confirm the neo_primitives Hardfork enum actually contains HfGorgon with the correct ordinal/byte value used in _usedHardforks ordering and is_active comparisons. A missing or mis-ordered Gorgon variant would not matter today (unscheduled) but would break parity once Gorgon ships.
all_findings.recommendation: Verify neo_primitives::Hardfork defines HfGorgon as the last variant with byte value 6 (Aspidochelone=0..Gorgon=6) matching C# enum ordinals, since native _usedHardforks ordering sorts by (byte)hardfork. Add a test asserting Rust hardfork byte values equal the C# enum positions.

all_findings.title: Default MaxItemSize is 65535, must be 131070 (ushort.MaxValue * 2)
all_findings.problem: C# ExecutionEngineLimits.MaxItemSize = ushort.MaxValue * 2 = 131070 (ExecutionEngineLimits.cs:40). The Rust default in neo-vm-rs (src/vm/limits.rs:43) sets max_item_size: u16::MAX as u32 = 65535. neo-core's ApplicationEngine uses neo_vm_rs::ExecutionEngineLimits via ExecutionEngine::new -> default() (neo_vm/execution_engine/mod.rs:193, execution_engine/core.rs:18; confirmed no override constructs 131070). This limit is enforced on NEWBUFFER, CAT, MEMCPY, conversions and BinarySerializer ByteString/Buffer deserialization (neo_vm/jump_table/splice.rs:106-126; smart_contract/binary_serializer.rs:51-122). Any stack item or serialized blob in the 65536..=131070 byte range succeeds in C# but FAULTs in Rust, diverging transaction/block execution.
all_findings.recommendation: Set neo-vm-rs ExecutionEngineLimits::DEFAULT.max_item_size = u16::MAX as u32 * 2 (131070). Add a parity test that builds a 131070-byte buffer via NEWBUFFER and asserts HALT, and a 131071-byte buffer asserts FAULT, matching C#.

all_findings.title: External VM (neo-vm-rs) caps NEWBUFFER/CAT item size at 1MB instead of 131070
all_findings.problem: The external-VM fast path (used for pure scripts via ApplicationEngine::try_execute_with_external_vm, external_vm.rs:254) enforces NEWBUFFER and CAT against the free-standing constant MAX_ITEM_SIZE = 1024*1024 = 1MB (neo-vm-rs/src/semantics/collections.rs:98-99 'buffer size exceeds MaxItemSize (1MB)'; src/semantics/splice.rs:14-15). C# limits both to MaxItemSize = 131070 (JumpTable.Splice.cs:31 NewBuffer AssertMaxItemSize, :77 Cat AssertMaxItemSize). A NEWBUFFER/CAT producing 131071..=1048576 bytes HALTs in the external VM but FAULTs in C#. Note this is also internally inconsistent with neo-vm-rs's own ExecutionEngineLimits (65535) and neo-core's enforcement (65535) — three different effective limits for the same operation.
all_findings.recommendation: Make the external VM enforce the real MaxItemSize (131070) for NEWBUFFER/CAT/SUBSTR/LEFT/RIGHT, ideally by threading the engine's ExecutionEngineLimits into the semantics layer rather than using the hard-coded 1MB MAX_ITEM_SIZE. Unify all three locations on one constant after fixing finding #1.

all_findings.title: External VM enforces MaxStackSize as eval-stack length, not C# reference count
all_findings.problem: C# enforces MaxStackSize (2048) against ReferenceCounter.Count, which counts the evaluation stack items PLUS slot items PLUS every nested sub-item inside compound types, re-checked after each instruction (ExecutionEngine.cs:300-304; ReferenceCounter.cs). The external VM (neo-vm-rs) instead checks only the evaluation-stack vector length: `if stack.len() > MAX_STACK_SIZE (2048)` (interpreter/executor/mod.rs:144-147; state.rs:108) and performs no reference counting of nested compound elements. A script that builds, e.g., one Array containing 3000 elements has C# Count ~= 3001 (FAULT) but external-VM stack.len() == 1 (HALT). Since pure scripts (those whose only syscalls are external-VM-supported, external_vm.rs:413 script_uses_application_engine_host) are routed to this engine, this desyncs execution from C#.
all_findings.recommendation: Either (a) do not route consensus execution through the external VM and use only the neo-core engine (which has a real ReferenceCounter), or (b) implement C#-equivalent reference counting (eval stack + slots + nested compound references with CheckZeroReferred) in neo-vm-rs. Option (a) is strongly preferred given the external VM appears designed for a constrained zk/TEE context (fixed-capacity 64-frame call stack, PolkaVM allocator comments).

all_findings.title: External VM caps call depth at 64 instead of MaxInvocationStackSize=1024
all_findings.problem: C# MaxInvocationStackSize = 1024 (ExecutionEngineLimits.cs:52), enforced in LoadContext (ExecutionEngine.cs:182). The external VM uses a fixed-capacity call stack of MAX_CALL_DEPTH = 64 (neo-vm-rs/src/interpreter/state.rs:110; state/call_stack.rs:14,45,75 'if self.len >= MAX_CALL_DEPTH'). A pure script that recurses via CALL/CALLA to a depth between 65 and 1024 FAULTs in the external VM but HALTs in C#. The neo-core engine itself is correct (execution_engine/context.rs:10 uses limits.max_invocation_stack_size with >= and the 1024 default), so this divergence is specific to the external-VM path.
all_findings.recommendation: Remove the external VM from the consensus path (preferred), or raise/par its call-depth limit to 1024 and make it dynamic rather than a fixed-capacity array. If kept, add cross-engine differential tests asserting identical FAULT/HALT for CALL recursion at depths 64, 65, 1023, 1024.

all_findings.title: MaxComparableSize is never enforced by neo-core EQUAL/NOTEQUAL
all_findings.problem: neo-core's StackItem::equals_with_limits ignores the limits argument entirely and delegates to plain deep equality: the parameter is named `_limits` and the body is `self.equals(other)` (neo_vm/stack_item/stack_item.rs:646-652). It is called by EQUAL/NOTEQUAL (neo_vm/jump_table/bitwisee.rs:90,99). C# throws 'operand exceeds the maximum comparable size' when a ByteString/Struct operand size exceeds MaxComparableSize=65536 (ByteString.cs:60-72) and additionally decrements a shared comparable budget plus a per-item count budget (=MaxStackSize) across nested Struct comparisons (Struct.cs:98-130). Consequently, comparing large (>65536 byte) values or deeply/large nested structs HALTs in Rust but FAULTs in C#. The external-VM comparison path (neo-vm-rs/src/semantics/comparison.rs:12 equal_values returns bool with no limits) shares this gap.
all_findings.recommendation: Implement C#-equivalent comparison: in equals_with_limits, fault when operand Size > MaxComparableSize, and for Struct comparison maintain the iterative stack with both a count budget (MaxStackSize) and a decrementing maxComparableSize budget exactly as Struct.cs:91-132. Apply the same in the external VM's comparison.rs.

all_findings.title: neo-core MaxStackSize post-instruction check is off-by-one (>= vs >)
all_findings.problem: C# PostExecuteInstruction faults only when CheckZeroReferred() is STRICTLY greater than MaxStackSize: `if (ReferenceCounter.CheckZeroReferred() > Limits.MaxStackSize) throw` (ExecutionEngine.cs:303-304). The neo-core engine uses >=: `if current >= self.limits.max_stack_size as usize { return Err(...MaxStackSize exceed...) }` (neo_vm/execution_engine/execution.rs:239). When the reference count equals exactly 2048, C# does NOT fault but Rust does. A contract that legitimately reaches exactly MaxStackSize references HALTs in C# but FAULTs in Rust, diverging execution.
all_findings.recommendation: Change line 239 from `current >= self.limits.max_stack_size as usize` to `current > self.limits.max_stack_size as usize` to match C# strict greater-than. Add a boundary test reaching exactly 2048 references.

all_findings.title: Default MaxComparableSize is 65535, must be 65536
all_findings.problem: C# MaxComparableSize = 65536 (ExecutionEngineLimits.cs:47). neo-vm-rs default sets max_comparable_size: u16::MAX as u32 = 65535 (src/vm/limits.rs:44). Even once MaxComparableSize enforcement is added (see finding #5), the boundary will be off by one: a 65536-byte operand comparison is allowed in C# (Size > limits is 65536 > 65536 = false) but would fault in Rust with the 65535 default. This is latent until enforcement exists, but must be fixed alongside it to avoid a one-byte consensus divergence.
all_findings.recommendation: Set neo-vm-rs ExecutionEngineLimits::DEFAULT.max_comparable_size = 65536 (literal), not u16::MAX. Pair with finding #5's enforcement work and a 65536/65537 boundary test.

all_findings.title: ENDTRY executed inside a FINALLY block: Rust pops+jumps, C# faults
all_findings.problem: C# ExecuteEndTry throws 'The opcode ENDTRY can't be executed in a FINALLY block' when the current try context state is Finally (JumpTable.Control.cs:585-586). The neo-core execute_end_try treats state==Finally as a valid case: it pops the try context and jumps to end (neo_vm/execution_engine/exception.rs:77-84) with no error. For an adversarial or malformed script that hits ENDTRY while in a finally block, C# FAULTs while Rust continues, which can diverge HALT/FAULT outcome.
all_findings.recommendation: In execute_end_try, when current_try_snapshot.state() == ExceptionHandlingState::Finally, return an error mirroring C# ('The opcode ENDTRY can't be executed in a FINALLY block') instead of popping and jumping. Add a test script that triggers ENDTRY inside FINALLY.

all_findings.title: Two parallel VM execution engines on the consensus path (neo-vm-rs + neo-core/neo_vm)
all_findings.problem: ApplicationEngine::execute_allow_fault first attempts the external neo-vm-rs interpreter (try_execute_with_external_vm) and only falls back to the neo-core jump_table engine when the script uses unsupported host features (load_execute_storage.rs:93-95; external_vm.rs:254-332, predicate at external_vm.rs:413). This means consensus-critical bytecode runs through one of two complete, independently-maintained VM implementations that must agree with each other AND with C# byte-for-byte. The divergences in findings #2/#3/#4 exist precisely because the two engines enforce limits differently (e.g. MaxItemSize 1MB in external VM vs 65535 in neo-core vs 131070 in C#). This is the structural root cause of the parity gaps and a large ongoing maintenance/audit hazard.
all_findings.recommendation: Pick a single canonical execution engine for consensus. Given neo-core's engine already has a real ReferenceCounter, correct MaxInvocationStackSize, and host integration, route ALL consensus execution through it and reserve neo-vm-rs for its intended constrained (zk/TEE/proof) use case, or gate the external-VM fast path behind a strict differential-fuzz harness that proves identical results. Until unified, add cross-engine differential tests over the opcode set with edge-case sizes/depths.

all_findings.title: BinarySerializer.Deserialize maxSize does not clamp to data length (minor)
all_findings.problem: C# BinarySerializer.Deserialize(data, limits) passes maxSize = Math.Min(data.Length, limits.MaxItemSize) (BinarySerializer.cs:60). The Rust deserialize passes limits.max_item_size directly without the Min(data.Length, ...) clamp (smart_contract/binary_serializer.rs:60-65). Functionally near-equivalent because read_var_bytes fails when reading past the buffer, but the error path/threshold differs slightly. Not consensus-relevant on valid inputs but worth aligning.
all_findings.recommendation: Pass max_size = min(data.len(), limits.max_item_size) in BinarySerializer::deserialize to mirror C# exactly.

all_findings.title: Opcode set, opcode price table, StackItemType tags, and ScriptBuilder verified matching C#
all_findings.problem: Positive verification (no defect): the canonical OpCode enum (neo-vm-rs/src/vm/opcode.rs:122-319) matches Neo.VM/OpCode.cs exactly for all values 0x00-0xE1 including reserved gaps and operand sizes/prefixes. The neo-core opcode price table (smart_contract/application_engine_op_code_prices.rs:7-18) was checked programmatically against ApplicationEngine.OpCodePrices.cs across all 256 byte slots with zero mismatches. StackItemType byte tags (neo-vm-rs/src/abi/stack_value.rs:60-78) match StackItemType.cs (Any=0x00, Pointer=0x10, Boolean=0x20, Integer=0x21, ByteString=0x28, Buffer=0x30, Array=0x40, Struct=0x41, Map=0x48, InteropInterface=0x60). ScriptBuilder emit/EmitPush/EmitJump/EmitCall thresholds (neo-core/src/script_builder.rs) match ScriptBuilder.cs. Integer 32-byte overflow is enforced in arithmetic via numeric::integer_result (neo-vm-rs/src/semantics/numeric.rs:76-88).
all_findings.recommendation: No action needed; retain these as the reference-correct components. Add CI golden tests pinning the price table and opcode bytes to guard against regressions during refactors.

all_findings.title: Duplicate RawMessage in neo-p2p uses wrong LZ4 compression boundary (missing 64-byte threshold + wrong >= min-size comparison)
all_findings.problem: neo-p2p/src/message.rs RawMessage::to_bytes (lines 52-72) compresses when payload.len() >= COMPRESSION_MIN_SIZE (128) and keeps the compressed form whenever compressed.len() < self.payload.len(). The C# reference (Message.cs:100-107) compresses only when _payloadCompressed.Length > CompressionMinSize (strict >, i.e. > 128) AND keeps it only when compressed.Length < length - CompressionThreshold (a 64-byte margin). RawMessage therefore (a) compresses a 128-byte payload that C# would not, and (b) keeps marginally-compressed payloads C# would leave uncompressed. It also ignores the per-command compression whitelist (allows_compression) entirely, unlike C#'s ShallICompress. This would produce a different MessageFlags byte and different wire bytes vs C# for affected payloads. The code is dead in the live path (only re-exported at neo-p2p/src/lib.rs:232, never used) but is public API and a latent interop trap. The correct implementation already exists in neo-core/src/network/p2p/message.rs (lines 97-107), which is the path actually used by connection.rs/framed_codec.rs.
all_findings.recommendation: Either delete RawMessage entirely (it is unused) or fix its to_bytes to match C#: gate on command.allows_compression(), use strict payload.len() > COMPRESSION_MIN_SIZE, and require compressed.len() + COMPRESSION_THRESHOLD < payload.len() before setting COMPRESSED. Preferably remove it to eliminate the duplicate Message implementation and have neo-core consume the canonical neo-p2p type.

all_findings.title: InventoryType includes Consensus=0x2d which C# v3.9 rejects on deserialization
all_findings.problem: Rust neo-primitives/src/inventory_type.rs defines Consensus = 0x2d and InventoryType::from_byte(0x2d) returns Some(Consensus) (confirmed by test at inventory_type.rs:40-43). The C# v3.9 InventoryType enum (InventoryType.cs) only defines TX=0x2b, Block=0x2c, Extensible=0x2e — there is no 0x2d. C# InvPayload.Deserialize (InvPayload.cs:78) calls Enum.IsDefined and throws FormatException on undefined types, so a C# node rejects an Inv/GetData/NotFound message with type 0x2d while the Rust node accepts it. 0x2d is a legacy Neo2-era value not produced by either implementation, so impact is limited to input-validation strictness on a value never seen in practice.
all_findings.recommendation: Remove the Consensus=0x2d variant from InventoryType to match C# v3.9 exactly (so 0x2d is rejected on the receive path), or document explicitly why the legacy value is retained. Verify no native-contract or consensus code depends on the Consensus variant before removing.

all_findings.title: Outbound queue priority/single-queued sets diverge from the C# mailbox policy they claim to mirror
all_findings.problem: message_command.rs HIGH_PRIORITY_COMMANDS = {Alert, Extensible, FilterAdd, FilterClear, FilterLoad, GetAddr, Mempool} and SINGLE_QUEUED_COMMANDS = {Addr, GetAddr, GetBlocks, GetHeaders, Mempool, Ping, Pong}, with tests named '...matches_remote_node_policy'. The actual C# RemoteNodeMailbox (RemoteNode.cs:247-269) defines IsHighPriority = {Extensible, FilterAdd, FilterClear, FilterLoad, Verack, Version, Alert} and ShallDrop = {GetAddr, GetBlocks, GetHeaders, Mempool}. So Rust high-priority omits Verack/Version (slowing handshake under load) and adds GetAddr/Mempool; Rust single-queued adds Addr/Ping/Pong that C# never drops. Additionally, C#'s rules apply to INBOUND message processing in an Akka priority mailbox, whereas the Rust sets drive a Rust-specific OUTBOUND queue (outbound_queue.rs:91-172) — the Rust node has no equivalent inbound-prioritization/ShallDrop on receive. This is a network-behavior divergence, not a wire-format issue.
all_findings.recommendation: Reconcile the sets with the C# RemoteNodeMailbox: add Verack/Version to high-priority, restrict single-queued/drop to {GetAddr, GetBlocks, GetHeaders, Mempool}, and correct the misleading test/doc names. Decide deliberately whether inbound prioritization (C# IsHighPriority/ShallDrop) should be replicated on the Rust receive path; if intentionally only applied outbound, document the rationale.

all_findings.title: Compression-send decision uses local AND remote DisableCompression vs C# which uses only the remote peer's AllowCompression
all_findings.problem: C# RemoteNode.SendData (RemoteNode.cs:229) compresses using message.ToArray(Version?.AllowCompression ?? false), where Version is the REMOTE peer's version and AllowCompression is derived solely from the remote's capabilities. The Rust handshake (remote_node.rs:262-276) sets connection.compression_allowed = config.enable_compression && local_allows && remote_allows (both sides' DisableCompression considered). When the local node advertises DisableCompression but the remote does not, C# would still compress outbound (it keys off the remote flag) while Rust would not. Because the COMPRESSED flag is self-describing and the receiver decodes based on the flag regardless, this does not break decoding, but it changes which messages appear compressed on the wire and is a behavioral divergence from C#.
all_findings.recommendation: Match C# precisely: base the outbound-compression decision only on the remote peer's advertised DisableCompression capability (i.e. drop the local_allows term from the send-path gate), keeping config.enable_compression as the master switch. Add a cross-impl test asserting the COMPRESSED flag matches C# for the local-disables/remote-enables case.

all_findings.title: Two parallel Message/RawMessage implementations across neo-p2p and neo-core (crate-boundary overlap)
all_findings.problem: There are two independent wire-message types: neo-p2p/src/message.rs (RawMessage, flags+command+raw payload) and neo-core/src/network/p2p/message.rs (Message, with payload_raw/payload_compressed mirroring C# _payloadRaw/_payloadCompressed). Only the neo-core Message is on the live path (connection.rs:232, framed_codec.rs); RawMessage is exported but unused. The two also disagree on compression semantics (see the RawMessage finding). neo-p2p is intended to be the canonical P2P crate (recent commits made MessageCommand/MessageFlags canonical there and deleted neo-core duplicates), so the canonical Message framing belongs in neo-p2p too, with neo-core consuming it rather than maintaining its own.
all_findings.recommendation: Consolidate to a single Message type in neo-p2p that implements the exact C# Create/ToArray/TryDeserialize semantics (whitelist + strict >128 min-size + 64-byte threshold), and have neo-core re-export/consume it. Remove the now-redundant RawMessage and the duplicate neo-core Message body, keeping any neo-core-only optimizations (serialize_into buffer reuse) as thin wrappers.

all_findings.title: ExtensiblePayload deserializes witness count via var-uint where C# reads a single raw byte
all_findings.problem: C# ExtensiblePayload.Deserialize (ExtensiblePayload.cs:91-94) reads the witness count with reader.ReadByte() and requires it == 1, serializing it with writer.Write((byte)1). Rust extensible_payload.rs:268 reads it with reader.read_var_uint() and serializes with write_var_uint(1) (line 259). For the only legal value (1) both encode/decode an identical single 0x01 byte, so well-formed messages round-trip identically. The divergence only manifests on malformed input where a peer encodes a count using a multi-byte var-int (e.g. 0xFD 0x01 0x00): C# would interpret the first byte 0xFD (=253) and reject (count != 1), whereas Rust would parse the var-int as 1 and then fail on the witness body. Both reject the malformed message, but at different points — a minor strictness/error-path difference, not a wire divergence for valid payloads.
all_findings.recommendation: For exact byte-level parity on the parse path, read the ExtensiblePayload witness count as a single byte (reader.read_u8()) rather than a var-uint, matching C# ReadByte(); keep the ==1 check. Low priority since valid messages are unaffected.

all_findings.title: Block NextConsensus computed from current-round validators, not the next-block validator set
all_findings.problem: At block assembly the header's NextConsensus is set to NativeHelpers::get_bft_address(block_data.validator_pubkeys) where validator_pubkeys are the CURRENT round's validators (neo-consensus prepare_block_data copies self.context.validators). C# ConsensusContext.Reset(0) (ConsensusContext.cs:208-211) instead sets NextConsensus = Contract.GetBFTAddress(ShouldRefreshCommittee(height+1) ? NEO.ComputeNextBlockValidators(...) : NEO.GetNextBlockValidators(...)) — i.e. the validator set that will sign the NEXT block. On a steady-state chain current==next so the two coincide, but at a committee-refresh boundary (height % CommitteeMembersCount == 0, NeoToken.cs:206) where the freshly elected committee differs, the C# header's NextConsensus reflects the new set while the Rust header reflects the old set. NextConsensus is part of the SerializeUnsigned header that the commit signatures sign (Header.cs SerializeUnsigned order Version,PrevHash,MerkleRoot,Timestamp,Nonce,Index,PrimaryIndex,NextConsensus), so the produced block hash and witness diverge from C# on those blocks, producing an invalid/incompatible chain at rotation boundaries.
all_findings.recommendation: Compute NextConsensus from the next-block validator set: get_bft_address(ShouldRefreshCommittee(block_index+1) ? compute_next_block_validators : get_next_block_validators) and pass it through BlockData, mirroring C# Reset(0). Keep the current-round validators only for the signing multisig contract.

all_findings.title: load_validators applies the committee-refresh condition to the signing validator set instead of only to NextConsensus
all_findings.problem: ConsensusActor::load_validators(next_index) picks the round's validators via should_refresh_committee(next_index) ? compute_next_block_validators : get_next_block_validators (neo-node/src/consensus.rs:297-312), and these become both the signers and (via the previous finding) NextConsensus. In C#, ConsensusContext.Reset(0) always sets the round Validators = NEO.GetNextBlockValidators(Snapshot) (ConsensusContext.cs:216) — the previously-elected set — and applies the ShouldRefreshCommittee/ComputeNextBlockValidators choice ONLY to NextConsensus (lines 208-211). So on a refresh boundary the C# block is signed by the OLD committee and merely announces the NEW committee in NextConsensus, whereas the Rust node would have the NEW committee both sign and be announced. This changes who is authorized to produce the block at the boundary, diverging from C# consensus.
all_findings.recommendation: Always load the round signing validators with get_next_block_validators (no refresh branch); use the refresh-conditional set exclusively for the NextConsensus address. Confirm against C# that ComputeNextBlockValidators is never used to select the current round's signers.

all_findings.title: Primary's proposed block timestamp is not clamped to max(prevTimestamp+1, now)
all_findings.problem: When acting as primary, neo-consensus on_transactions_received sets proposed_timestamp = current_timestamp() with no lower bound (neo-consensus/src/service/proposal.rs:39-43). C# MakePrepareRequest sets Block.Header.Timestamp = Math.Max(UtcNow.ToTimestampMS(), PrevHeader.Timestamp + 1) (ConsensusContext.MakePayload.cs:110), guaranteeing a strictly increasing timestamp even under clock skew. The node-side incoming precheck does reject PrepareRequests with timestamp <= prev_timestamp (neo-node/src/consensus.rs:1091-1094), so a lagging-clock primary would have its own proposal rejected by every backup, stalling the round — a liveness regression C# cannot hit. neo-consensus does not have prev-block timestamp available to clamp, so the clamp must be plumbed in.
all_findings.recommendation: Plumb the previous block timestamp into the consensus context (it is already tracked at the node as prev_timestamp) and set proposed_timestamp = max(current_timestamp(), prev_timestamp + 1) in on_transactions_received, matching C# Math.Max.

all_findings.title: View-change timeout uses a single simplified curve with a shift clamp, diverging from C# per-role timer formulas
all_findings.problem: ConsensusContext::get_timeout returns base << (view_number + 1).min(5) (neo-consensus/src/context/mod.rs:419-428), a single formula for all roles with the shift exponent capped at 5. C# DBFTPlugin uses TimePerBlock << (viewNumber + 1) with NO clamp, and distinct per-situation timers: the primary's send timer is (TimePerBlock << (viewNumber+1)) - (viewNumber==0 ? TimePerBlock : 0) (ConsensusService.cs:214) so at view 0 the primary timeout is exactly one block time, not two; RequestChangeView uses << (expectedView+1) (line 231); commit resend uses << 1 (line 185). Consequences: (a) Rust caps the timeout growth at 32x block time for views >=4 while C# keeps doubling, and (b) the Rust primary at view 0 waits ~2x longer than C#. Timing affects only liveness/convergence speed, not consensus hashes, but it is a behavioral divergence from the reference and could slow recovery under sustained view changes.
all_findings.recommendation: Mirror C#: drop the .min(5) clamp (or raise it well beyond realistic view counts), and special-case the primary view-0 send timer as TimePerBlock rather than 2*TimePerBlock. Consider modeling the distinct timers (send/change-view/commit-resend) instead of one get_timeout.

all_findings.title: Block-finalization check omits the C# guard that all proposed transactions are present
all_findings.problem: check_commits finalizes (emits BlockCommitted) as soon as has_enough_commits() returns true (M signatures at current view) — neo-consensus/src/service/handlers/commit.rs:123-155. C# CheckCommits additionally requires context.TransactionHashes.All(p => context.Transactions.ContainsKey(p)) before calling CreateBlock/relaying the block (ConsensusService.Check.cs:60). In Rust the node layer compensates by requesting missing transactions before assembling the block (neo-node/src/consensus.rs around 930-942), so this is not a correctness break, but the consensus core declaring BlockCommitted without confirming tx availability is a divergence from the C# state machine and could surface as a premature commit event if the node-layer guard is ever bypassed.
all_findings.recommendation: Track tx availability in the consensus context (or gate the BlockCommitted event) so check_commits mirrors C#'s 'M commits AND all transactions present' precondition, keeping the core state machine self-consistent rather than relying solely on the node layer.

all_findings.title: RecoveryMessage compact arrays are sorted by validator_index before serialization (C# uses Dictionary insertion order)
all_findings.problem: RecoveryMessage::serialize sorts change_view_messages, preparation_messages, and commit_messages by validator_index before writing (neo-consensus/src/messages/recovery.rs:228-259). C# writes Dictionary.Values.ToArray() in insertion order, not sorted (RecoveryMessage.cs:117-131). The RecoveryMessage is gossip used to reconstruct state and is NOT a consensus-signed artifact (only the ExtensiblePayload witness over the message bytes is signed, and a node re-derives individual signed payloads from the compacts), so differing element order does not affect block hashes or consensus safety. However it means a Rust-serialized RecoveryMessage will not be byte-identical to the C# one for the same logical content, which can complicate cross-impl test vectors and any hash-based dedup of recovery payloads.
all_findings.recommendation: If exact byte parity of RecoveryMessage with C# is desired (e.g. for shared test vectors), preserve insertion/collection order rather than sorting; otherwise document explicitly that recovery payload byte-order intentionally differs and is consensus-irrelevant.

all_findings.title: Crate-level doc comment states an incorrect M threshold formula
all_findings.problem: The neo-consensus lib.rs module docs state 'M = (n + f) / 2 + 1 = (2n + 1) / 3 (same as 2f + 1)' (neo-consensus/src/lib.rs:91-98). The actual and correct implementation is m() = n - f with f = (n-1)/3 (context/mod.rs:230-238), matching C# M => Validators.Length - F (ConsensusContext.cs:67-68). The stated identity 'M=(n+f)/2+1' is wrong in general (e.g. n=7,f=2 -> 5.5) and misleads readers about the consensus threshold even though the code itself is correct.
all_findings.recommendation: Replace the doc formula with the implemented and reference-correct definition: F = floor((n-1)/3), M = n - F. Remove the spurious (n+f)/2+1 identity.

all_findings.title: NamedCurveHash Keccak256 variants use wrong byte values (0x18/0x19 vs C# 122/123)
all_findings.problem: neo-crypto/src/named_curve_hash.rs defines Secp256k1Keccak256 = 0x18 and Secp256r1Keccak256 = 0x19. The C# reference (neo_csharp/src/Neo/SmartContract/Native/NamedCurveHash.cs:22-37) defines secp256k1Keccak256 = 122 (0x7A) and secp256r1Keccak256 = 123 (0x7B). CryptoLib.verifyWithECDsa (crypto_lib.rs:190 parse_named_curve_hash → NamedCurveHash::from_byte) reads the curve selector byte directly from the VM argument. A post-Cockatrice contract that calls verifyWithECDsa(msg, pubkey, sig, 123) — the real secp256r1Keccak256 value — yields from_byte(123)=None in Rust, which returns 0 (verification failure), whereas C# performs a valid Keccak256 ECDSA verification. Conversely values 24/25 (0x18/0x19) are accepted by Rust but rejected by C#. This changes contract execution results and therefore consensus state/VM results for any contract using Keccak256 ECDSA verification (e.g. Ethereum-signature interop). The SHA256 variants (0x16/0x17) are coincidentally correct because C# uses 22/23. The in-tree unit test (named_curve_hash.rs:144-150) even asserts the wrong serialized value ("25" for Secp256r1Keccak256), locking in the bug.
all_findings.recommendation: Change the protocol_enum byte values to match C#: Secp256k1Keccak256 = 122 (0x7A), Secp256r1Keccak256 = 123 (0x7B). Update the unit tests (to_byte, from_byte, serde) accordingly and add a cross-check test asserting exact C# values 22/23/122/123. Verify no other code hard-codes 0x18/0x19 for these curves.
all_findings.dimension: Cryptography parity (neo-crypto vs neo_csharp/src/Neo/Cryptography + CryptoLib native)

all_findings.title: secp256k1 ECDSA verification rejects high-s signatures that C# accepts (malleability divergence)
all_findings.problem: Secp256k1Crypto::verify (neo-crypto/src/signature.rs:76-93) uses the libsecp256k1 C-binding crate secp256k1 v0.28.2. That crate's verify_ecdsa requires a low-s normalized signature: its own docs state 'The signature must be normalized or verification will fail' (verified in ~/.cargo/.../secp256k1-0.28.2/src/ecdsa/mod.rs:194). The code does NOT call normalize_s() before verifying. C# Crypto.VerifySignature (neo_csharp/src/Neo/Cryptography/Crypto.cs:140-164) uses .NET ECDsa.VerifyData / BouncyCastle ECDsaSigner.VerifySignature, neither of which enforces low-s — both accept high-s signatures. Therefore a high-s secp256k1 signature that C# Neo accepts is rejected by the Rust node. This path is reachable via CryptoLib.verifyWithECDsa for secp256k1SHA256/secp256k1Keccak256 (crypto_lib.rs:132 → Crypto::verify_signature_with_curve → verify_ecdsa_raw64_with_hash secp256k1 arms → Secp256k1Crypto::verify) and via Crypto::verify_signature_bytes used by the Notary native contract (notary.rs:632). The dominant secp256r1 path is NOT affected because it uses RustCrypto p256 whose ecdsa-0.16.9 verify_prehashed performs plain ECDSA without a low-s check (verified in ~/.cargo/.../ecdsa-0.16.9/src/hazmat.rs:270-292), matching C#.
all_findings.recommendation: Make secp256k1 verification s-agnostic to match C#. Either (a) call sig.normalize_s() on a clone before verify_ecdsa in Secp256k1Crypto::verify and in the secp256k1 arms of verify_ecdsa_raw64_with_hash, or (b) switch the secp256k1 verification path to RustCrypto k256 (which, like p256, does not reject high-s) instead of the libsecp256k1 binding — note ecc.rs already has a k256-based verify_signature_secp256k1. Add a regression test that constructs a high-s secp256k1 signature and asserts it verifies true.
all_findings.dimension: Cryptography parity (neo-crypto vs neo_csharp/src/Neo/Cryptography + CryptoLib native)

all_findings.title: NEP-2 key encryption uses AES-256-CBC (zero IV) instead of C# AES-256-ECB
all_findings.problem: NEP-2 encrypt/decrypt in neo-core/src/wallets/key_pair.rs uses the cbc crate: Encryptor::<Aes256>::new_from_slices(derived_half2, &[0u8;16]) and Decryptor with NoPadding (encrypt_nep2 lines 265-275, decrypt_nep2 lines 327-333). The C# reference KeyPair.Encrypt (neo_csharp/src/Neo/Wallets/KeyPair.cs:140-148) uses Aes with CipherMode.ECB and PaddingMode.None. The plaintext is the 32-byte XOR(privkey, derivedhalf1), i.e. two AES blocks. With a zero IV, CBC block 1 equals ECB block 1, but CBC block 2 XORs the plaintext with block 1's ciphertext before encryption, so byte range [16..32] of the encrypted key differs from ECB. Result: Rust-produced NEP-2 keys cannot be imported by C#/standard wallets, and Rust cannot decrypt standard NEP-2 keys (the Rust decrypt is symmetric so it round-trips only with itself; the address-hash check at decrypt_nep2:349 will fail against a genuine C# NEP-2 string). This breaks wallet portability for the NEP-2 export/import format.
all_findings.recommendation: Replace the cbc Encryptor/Decryptor with raw AES-256-ECB no-padding over the two 16-byte blocks (e.g. aes::Aes256 block cipher encrypting each 16-byte block independently). Add an interop test using a known C# NEP-2 vector (passphrase, address version, expected encrypted key string) to lock parity.
all_findings.dimension: Cryptography parity (neo-crypto vs neo_csharp/src/Neo/Cryptography + CryptoLib native)

all_findings.title: verify_signature_bytes tries secp256k1 before secp256r1 for 33-byte keys (fragile, ties into high-s bug)
all_findings.problem: Crypto::verify_signature_bytes (neo-crypto/src/signature.rs:478-515) infers the curve from key/signature length. For a 33-byte key it attempts Secp256k1Crypto::verify first, then falls back to Secp256r1Crypto::verify. The Notary native contract uses this for designated-notary keys which are secp256r1 ECPoints; C# instead verifies explicitly against the secp256r1 ECPoint (Notary.cs:134 Crypto.VerifySignature(hash, sig, n)). It happens to produce the correct boolean (a P-256 signature fails secp256k1 verify and then succeeds on secp256r1), but it relies on the secp256k1 attempt always failing, and combined with the high-s secp256k1 finding it muddies reasoning about consensus behavior. This is curve-inference not present in C#, which always knows the curve from the ECPoint.
all_findings.recommendation: Where the curve is known (Notary, contract groups), call the explicit secp256r1 verifier (Secp256r1Crypto::verify / verify_signature_secp256r1) rather than the length-inferring verify_signature_bytes. Reserve verify_signature_bytes for genuinely ambiguous inputs, and document the try-order.
all_findings.dimension: Cryptography parity (neo-crypto vs neo_csharp/src/Neo/Cryptography + CryptoLib native)

all_findings.title: MerkleTree.compute_root returns None for empty input; C# ComputeRoot returns UInt256.Zero
all_findings.problem: C# MerkleTree.ComputeRoot (neo_csharp/src/Neo/Cryptography/MerkleTree.cs:86-93) returns UInt256.Zero for an empty hash array. Rust MerkleTree::compute_root (neo-crypto/src/merkle_tree.rs:92-98) returns None for empty input. Callers must explicitly map None to UInt256::zero() to match C#; if any caller defaults differently (e.g. propagates None as an error or uses a non-zero sentinel) the block/MPT root for the empty-tx edge case would diverge. The single-element and general cases match C# (duplicate-last-on-odd, Hash256 of 64-byte concat). This is flagged as low because Neo blocks always contain at least the consensus/transactions, but the empty-input contract differs and should be audited at call sites.
all_findings.recommendation: Confirm every caller of compute_root maps None to UInt256::zero() (matching C# UInt256.Zero), or change compute_root to return UInt256::zero() for empty input to mirror C# exactly. Add a test asserting compute_root(&[]) semantics equal C# UInt256.Zero at the call boundary.
all_findings.dimension: Cryptography parity (neo-crypto vs neo_csharp/src/Neo/Cryptography + CryptoLib native)

all_findings.title: Unused BLS12-381 signature-scheme wrapper duplicates concept of native BLS arithmetic
all_findings.problem: neo-crypto/src/bls12381.rs implements a full BLS signature scheme (sign/verify/aggregate over G2 pubkeys and G1 sigs with DST BLS_SIG_BLS12381G1_XMD:SHA-256_SSWU_RO_NUL_). The consensus-relevant BLS surface — CryptoLib.bls12381Serialize/Deserialize/Add/Mul/Pairing/Equal (neo_csharp/src/Neo/SmartContract/Native/CryptoLib.BLS12_381.cs) — is implemented separately and correctly in neo-core/src/smart_contract/native/crypto_lib/bls12381.rs using blst raw point arithmetic (G1=48, G2=96, GT=576 byte encodings, interop tags 0x01-0x05). The neo-crypto Bls12381Crypto signature scheme is not used by the native contract; it appears to serve NeoFS/auxiliary use only. Having two BLS modules (one a signature scheme in neo-crypto, one raw arithmetic in neo-core) is potentially confusing and the signature-scheme module has no consensus consumer.
all_findings.recommendation: Confirm whether Bls12381Crypto has any live consumer (NeoFS); if not, remove it or gate it behind a clearly-named feature/module to avoid the impression that it backs the native CryptoLib BLS methods. Keep the native blst arithmetic in neo-core where it belongs.
all_findings.dimension: Cryptography parity (neo-crypto vs neo_csharp/src/Neo/Cryptography + CryptoLib native)

all_findings.title: Address (0x35) and Scrypt/NEP-2 encoding live in neo-core, not neo-crypto
all_findings.problem: The cryptography crate (neo-crypto) provides Base58/Base58Check and hashing, but Neo address encode/decode (version byte 0x35) and the Scrypt-based NEP-2 key derivation/encryption live in neo-core (constants::ADDRESS_VERSION at neo-core/src/protocol_settings.rs:126, WalletHelper::to_address, and neo-core/src/wallets/key_pair.rs Scrypt+AES). C# groups Base58Check address logic with Cryptography helpers and UInt160.ToAddress. This is a defensible boundary (address depends on protocol settings/script hash), but it spreads the encoding-format concern across two crates and the NEP-2 AES-mode bug (separate finding) sits outside neo-crypto where the rest of the symmetric-crypto helpers (AES256Encrypt/Decrypt analog) would naturally live.
all_findings.recommendation: Document the boundary decision; consider centralizing the symmetric-crypto primitive (AES for NEP-2) in neo-crypto so wallet code consumes a single audited AES helper, reducing the chance of cipher-mode mistakes like the CBC-vs-ECB NEP-2 bug.
all_findings.dimension: Cryptography parity (neo-crypto vs neo_csharp/src/Neo/Cryptography + CryptoLib native)

all_findings.title: WitnessCondition Group/CalledByGroup reject uncompressed (65-byte) ECPoints that C# accepts
all_findings.problem: C# GroupCondition/CalledByGroupCondition deserialize the group via ECPoint.DeserializeFrom -> DecodePoint, which accepts BOTH compressed (prefix 0x02/0x03, 33 bytes) and uncompressed (prefix 0x04, 65 bytes) encodings (neo_csharp/src/Neo/Cryptography/ECC/ECPoint.cs:154-163, 78-103). The Rust deserializer reads the correct length via read_group_bytes (helpers.rs:39-51, which does handle 0x04 -> 65 bytes), but then deserialize_group_bytes rejects anything != 33 bytes with 'Invalid ECPoint length' (witness_rule/serialization.rs:71-77, 117-119,124-126). A transaction/witness-rule carrying an uncompressed group point is therefore accepted by C# nodes but rejected by Rust, so a block containing such a tx would fork the network (Rust rejects, C# accepts). Serialize side also hard-requires 33 bytes (serialization.rs:33-39).
all_findings.recommendation: Make Group/CalledByGroup deserialization accept both 33-byte and 65-byte ECPoint encodings exactly like C# ECPoint.DecodePoint: decode the point with the curve (validating it lies on secp256r1) and store/normalize it. Do not reject 65-byte uncompressed points. Ensure round-trip serialization preserves the original encoding C# would emit (C# re-serializes via ECPoint which uses compressed form, so confirm whether the wire byte form must be re-emitted compressed; match C#'s ISerializable.Serialize behavior).

all_findings.title: Signer.allowed_groups deserialization reads fixed 33 bytes, misaligns stream on uncompressed ECPoints
all_findings.problem: C# Signer.Deserialize reads AllowedGroups via reader.ReadSerializableArray<ECPoint>(MaxSubitems) (neo_csharp/src/Neo/Network/P2P/Payloads/Signer.cs:115-116), where each ECPoint accepts 33-byte compressed OR 65-byte uncompressed encodings. The Rust Signer deserialize reads exactly ECPOINT_COMPRESSED_SIZE (33) bytes per group regardless of the encoding prefix (neo-core/src/network/p2p/payloads/signer.rs:387-391 via reader.read_bytes(ECPOINT_COMPRESSED_SIZE)). If a signer carries an uncompressed (0x04, 65-byte) group point, Rust consumes only 33 bytes and then misreads the remainder of the signer/transaction stream, producing a different (failed) deserialization than C#. This is the same encoding-parity gap as the WitnessCondition Group case and is consensus-relevant because Signer is part of the transaction hash preimage.
all_findings.recommendation: Read each allowed-group ECPoint with prefix-aware length detection (33 vs 65 bytes) and decode/validate on secp256r1, mirroring C# ECPoint.DeserializeFrom. Reuse the same shared ECPoint deserialization helper used for the WitnessCondition fix so both code paths stay consistent.

all_findings.title: Block::deserialize omits inline Merkle-root and duplicate-transaction checks that C# enforces during deserialization
all_findings.problem: C# Block.DeserializeTransactions rejects a block during deserialization if any transaction hash is duplicated (HashSet.Add) or if MerkleTree.ComputeRoot(txHashes) != Header.MerkleRoot (neo_csharp/src/Neo/Network/P2P/Payloads/Block.cs:104-125). The Rust Block::deserialize does neither (neo-core/src/network/p2p/payloads/block/serialization.rs:23-66); these checks live only in a separate Block::verify path (block/verification.rs:71-76, 261-310). Any Rust code path that deserializes a block without then calling verify() will accept blocks with a wrong Merkle root or duplicate txs that C# rejects at parse time. Conversely, Rust adds a cumulative MAX_BLOCK_SIZE (2MB) DoS check during deserialization (serialization.rs:36-57) that C# does NOT perform at deserialize time (C# enforces MaxBlockSize only at the DBFT/relay layer), so Rust will reject some large blocks that C# would deserialize.
all_findings.recommendation: Move (or duplicate) the duplicate-tx-hash and Merkle-root validation into Block::deserialize to match C#'s fail-at-parse semantics, guaranteeing every deserialization path is protected. Re-evaluate the cumulative-size check during deserialize: if kept, ensure its bound and trigger conditions cannot reject a block that C# would accept on the wire (consider enforcing the same 0x02000000 PayloadMaxSize bound the message layer uses rather than the 2MB consensus MaxBlockSize).

all_findings.title: Duplicate Message type in neo-p2p has incorrect LZ4 compression gating (parity divergence vs C#)
all_findings.problem: Two Message implementations coexist: the live neo-core NetworkMessage (neo-core/src/network/p2p/message.rs:97-107) correctly mirrors C# Message.Create — gating on command.allows_compression(), using strict '> COMPRESSION_MIN_SIZE', and requiring 'compressed.len() + COMPRESSION_THRESHOLD < payload.len()' (i.e. must save > 64 bytes). The separate neo-p2p crate's Message::to_bytes (neo-p2p/src/message.rs:52-72) does NOT gate on allows_compression(), uses '>= COMPRESSION_MIN_SIZE' instead of '>', and uses 'compressed.len() < payload.len()' with no 64-byte threshold. Per C# (neo_csharp/src/Neo/Network/P2P/Message.cs:87,100,103) all three rules are required; the neo-p2p version would emit the COMPRESSED flag for messages C# would send uncompressed, producing different wire bytes. The neo-p2p Message::to_bytes is not on the active connection path (connection.rs uses NetworkMessage), so this is currently a latent duplication hazard rather than a live wire bug.
all_findings.recommendation: Eliminate the duplicate Message wire codec: have neo-p2p re-export/use the neo-core NetworkMessage serialization (or move the canonical Message codec into neo-p2p and have neo-core depend on it), so there is a single compression decision implementation. If neo-p2p must keep its own Message, fix to_bytes to apply allows_compression() gating, strict '>' min-size, and the COMPRESSION_THRESHOLD savings requirement.

all_findings.title: Transaction combined-fee overflow check can panic in debug builds (i64 add) vs C# unchecked wrap
all_findings.problem: C# DeserializeUnsigned validates combined fee via 'if (SystemFee + NetworkFee < SystemFee)' relying on unchecked/wrapping i64 addition (neo_csharp/src/Neo/Network/P2P/Payloads/Transaction.cs:199-200). The Rust port uses the same expression 'if system_fee + network_fee < system_fee' (neo-core/src/network/p2p/payloads/transaction/serialization.rs:48), but Rust's i64 '+' panics on overflow in debug builds rather than wrapping. Both reject overflow in release builds (where '+' wraps), so wire/consensus behavior matches in release, but a debug build could panic (DoS) on a crafted tx instead of returning a clean FormatError.
all_findings.recommendation: Use checked_add and return IoError on None (e.g. 'system_fee.checked_add(network_fee).ok_or_else(|| invalid combined fee)?'), which is panic-free and matches C#'s reject-on-overflow semantics in all build profiles.

all_findings.title: UInt160/UInt256 GetHashCode differs from C# (in-memory only, not serialization)
all_findings.problem: C# UInt160.GetHashCode = HashCode.Combine(_value1,_value2,_value3) and UInt256.GetHashCode = (int)_value1 (neo_csharp/src/Neo/UInt160.cs:104-107, UInt256.cs:107). The Rust hash_code() implementations use a manual 17/31 FNV-style or XOR-fold scheme (neo-primitives/src/uint160.rs:63-72, uint256.rs:26-29). These hash codes are used only for in-memory HashSet/HashMap bucketing and never appear on the wire or in consensus state, so there is no serialization-parity impact. Flagged only to document the intentional divergence and avoid future confusion if anyone assumes hash-code parity.
all_findings.recommendation: No action required for serialization parity. If exact C# hash-code parity is ever desired for test vectors, align the helper with HashCode.Combine semantics; otherwise document that hash_code() is intentionally non-portable.

all_findings.title: Block import re-verifies every transaction (state-independent + state-dependent); C# does not
all_findings.problem: C# Block.Verify is literally `return Header.Verify(...)` (Block.cs:170-178). During OnNewBlock/OnImport C# never calls VerifyStateIndependent/VerifyStateDependent on the block's transactions, and Persist (Blockchain.cs:435-453) only EXECUTES tx scripts (Application trigger) — a faulting tx is committed as a no-op, the block still persists. The Rust Block::verify_internal calls verify_transactions (block/verification.rs:197-253) which runs verify_state_independent on every tx (incl. signature checks) and then verify_state_dependent_at_height (expiry, policy-blocked sender, attribute verify, fee/witness checks) and rejects the whole block on any failure. This path is live: block_processing.rs:64 and handlers.rs:241 call block.verify(_with_cache) on the import/relay path. A block that C# accepts but that contains a tx now failing state-dependent re-verification (e.g. ValidUntilBlock relative to the persisting snapshot, a sender blocked by Policy after consensus, or insufficient-funds after conflict reordering) is rejected by Rust -> chain halt/fork.
all_findings.recommendation: Make Rust Block::verify match C# semantics: it should perform only header verification (Header::verify/verify_with_cache). Remove per-transaction verify_state_independent/verify_state_dependent from the block-import path. Transaction validity is guaranteed by consensus pre-verification + the deserialization-time merkle/duplicate checks; block import must only validate the header witness and chain continuity.
all_findings.dimension: Ledger / blockchain semantics parity (Block, Transaction, Header, TransactionAttribute, MemoryPool, verification pipeline, genesis)

all_findings.title: Fabricated 2MB MAX_BLOCK_SIZE enforced on block deserialize and verify (no such consensus rule in C#)
all_findings.problem: Rust hard-caps blocks at MAX_BLOCK_SIZE=2_097_152 and enforces it both in Block::deserialize (serialization.rs:38-44,52-57) and Block::verify (validate_block_size). C# core has NO block-byte-size consensus limit: the only hard limit is the P2P Message.PayloadMaxSize=0x02000000 (32MB, Message.cs:30) which Rust already mirrors correctly (message.rs:20). C#'s MaxBlockSize is a DBFT *plugin* setting (default 262144 = 256KB; DbftSettings.cs:43) used only when PRODUCING a block, never when receiving one. Thus any valid block between 2MB and 32MB is accepted by C# but rejected by Rust at parse time -> fork. The constants.rs comment falsely claims 2MB is 'Neo N3 ProtocolSettings.Default'.
all_findings.recommendation: Remove the 2MB block-size gate from Block::deserialize and Block::verify. Rely solely on the P2P PAYLOAD_MAX_SIZE (32MB) limit at the message layer, exactly as C# does. Delete/correct the misleading MAX_BLOCK_SIZE comment.
all_findings.dimension: Ledger / blockchain semantics parity (Block, Transaction, Header, TransactionAttribute, MemoryPool, verification pipeline, genesis)

all_findings.title: Header/Block verify rejects future timestamps (15-min drift) and small witness scripts; C# checks neither
all_findings.problem: Header::verify and verify_with_cache call validate_self -> validate_timestamp_bounds and validate_witness_scripts (header/verification.rs:87-97). validate_timestamp_bounds (validation.rs:163-184) rejects any header whose timestamp is < MIN_TIMESTAMP_MS or > now + 15min (MAX_TIMESTAMP_DRIFT_MS). validate_witness_scripts (validation.rs:287-325) rejects witness invocation/verification scripts > 1024 bytes and any verification script whose first byte is 0xFF. C# Header.Verify (Header.cs:203-226) does NONE of this — it only checks PrimaryIndex<ValidatorsCount, prev exists, prev.Index+1==Index, prev.Hash==PrevHash, prev.Timestamp>=Timestamp (strictly increasing), and the witness. A block whose timestamp is legitimately ahead of the receiving node's wall clock (common during sync or clock skew) is accepted by C# but rejected by Rust -> halt. The 1024-byte/0xFF witness gates are similarly absent in C# and could reject valid consensus multisig witnesses.
all_findings.recommendation: Strip validate_timestamp_bounds and validate_witness_scripts from header verification. Keep only the C# checks: primary_index < validators_count, prev block continuity, strictly-increasing timestamp (validate_timestamp_progression), and witness verification. The timestamp future-bound and witness-size heuristics are not part of Neo consensus.
all_findings.dimension: Ledger / blockchain semantics parity (Block, Transaction, Header, TransactionAttribute, MemoryPool, verification pipeline, genesis)

all_findings.title: OracleResponse::verify omits 3 of 5 C# checks (request existence, fee match, designated-oracle signer)
all_findings.problem: C# OracleResponse.Verify performs five checks: (1) all signers Scopes==None, (2) tx.Script == FixedScript (Oracle.finish call), (3) Oracle.GetRequest(snapshot, Id) is non-null, (4) tx.NetworkFee + tx.SystemFee == request.GasForResponse, (5) a signer equals the BFT address of the role-designated Oracle nodes. The Rust OracleResponse::verify (oracle_response.rs:69-88) implements only checks (1) and (2), then returns true with a comment acknowledging the rest is unimplemented. Consequently a Rust node admits/relays oracle-response transactions that C# rejects — including responses not signed by the designated oracle nodes, with wrong fees, or for non-existent requests. On any oracle-enabled network this admits invalid transactions into the mempool and passes them through block-import attribute verification.
all_findings.recommendation: Implement the missing checks: look up the oracle request via OracleContract::get_request(snapshot, id) and fail if absent; verify network_fee + system_fee == request.gas_for_response; resolve the Oracle role's designated nodes via RoleManagement.GetDesignatedByRole(Oracle, current_index+1), compute the BFT address, and require a matching signer. Use the snapshot and settings parameters that are currently ignored.
all_findings.dimension: Ledger / blockchain semantics parity (Block, Transaction, Header, TransactionAttribute, MemoryPool, verification pipeline, genesis)

all_findings.title: Block deserialization does not enforce merkle-root match nor reject duplicate transactions
all_findings.problem: C# Block.Deserialize -> DeserializeTransactions (Block.cs:104-126) throws FormatException if MerkleTree.ComputeRoot(tx hashes) != Header.MerkleRoot, and throws if any duplicate tx hash is present — both at parse time. Rust Block::deserialize (serialization.rs:23-66) reads transactions and returns Ok without computing/comparing the merkle root and without a duplicate-hash check. These checks are deferred to Block::verify (verify_merkle_root/verify_no_duplicate_transactions). This means a block with a tampered merkle root or duplicate txs is byte-accepted by Rust but rejected at the wire level by C#. Any code path that deserializes a block without later calling verify (relay, tooling, RPC) would propagate an inconsistent block, and the accept/reject boundary differs from C# at the deserialization layer.
all_findings.recommendation: Move the merkle-root recomputation/comparison and duplicate-tx rejection into Block::deserialize to fail fast exactly like C# DeserializeTransactions, returning IoError on mismatch. Keep verify_merkle_root/verify_no_duplicate_transactions as defense-in-depth but make the deserializer the authoritative gate matching C#.
all_findings.dimension: Ledger / blockchain semantics parity (Block, Transaction, Header, TransactionAttribute, MemoryPool, verification pipeline, genesis)

all_findings.title: MemoryPool TryAdd diverges: runs state-independent verification and AlreadyInPool covers unverified set
all_findings.problem: C# MemoryPool.TryAdd (MemoryPool.cs:313-371) does NOT call VerifyStateIndependent (that happens earlier in OnNewTransaction) and returns AlreadyInPool only when the tx is in the VERIFIED set (_unsortedTransactions.ContainsKey at line 324). The Rust try_add (memory_pool/mod.rs:236-373) calls tx.verify_state_independent (line 273) inside try_add, and its AlreadyInPool check uses contains_key (views.rs:54) which covers BOTH verified and unverified sets. Effect: a tx present only in the unverified pool — which C# would re-admit and promote into the verified set via TryAdd — is rejected by Rust with AlreadyInPool, blocking re-verification/promotion. The extra state-independent call also changes which VerifyResult code is returned for malformed txs relative to C#'s ordering.
all_findings.recommendation: Mirror C#: in try_add, check only the verified set for AlreadyInPool, and do not perform state-independent verification inside try_add (perform it in the OnNewTransaction equivalent before reaching the pool). Align the returned VerifyResult sequencing with C# TryAdd.
all_findings.dimension: Ledger / blockchain semantics parity (Block, Transaction, Header, TransactionAttribute, MemoryPool, verification pipeline, genesis)

all_findings.title: Non-C# per-sender mempool limit can reject transactions C# admits
all_findings.problem: MemoryPool carries a max_transactions_per_sender field (memory_pool/mod.rs:86) and, when set, returns VerifyResult::PolicyFail for senders exceeding it (lines 283-296). C# MemoryPool has no per-sender cap; admission is governed purely by Capacity, fee-based eviction (RemoveOverCapacity), and conflict rules. If this limit is ever configured non-None, the Rust node will refuse transactions that C# accepts, causing mempool/relay divergence and potentially different block contents for a Rust-based proposer.
all_findings.recommendation: Remove the per-sender limit from the canonical MemoryPool, or gate it strictly behind an explicitly non-default, off-by-default operator setting documented as a deviation from C# and never enabled on mainnet/testnet profiles. Default behavior must equal C# (no per-sender cap).
all_findings.dimension: Ledger / blockchain semantics parity (Block, Transaction, Header, TransactionAttribute, MemoryPool, verification pipeline, genesis)

all_findings.title: MemoryPool block-import enforces MaxTransactionsPerBlock as a hard import gate (C# uses it only for reverify sizing)
all_findings.problem: Rust Block::verify_internal calls validate_transaction_count (block/verification.rs:55) which rejects any block with > MAX_TRANSACTIONS_PER_BLOCK (512) transactions. In C# core, MaxTransactionsPerBlock is referenced ONLY in MemoryPool reverification sizing (MemoryPool.cs:567,687) and DBFT block production — never as a block-import validity gate (grep of Neo core confirms no other usage). While legitimately consensus-produced blocks stay <=512, a block the network accepts with more txs (e.g. after a settings change or a non-DBFT producer) would be rejected by Rust but not by C#.
all_findings.recommendation: Remove validate_transaction_count from the block-import verification path. The transaction-count ceiling belongs to consensus block production and mempool reverify sizing, not to block import validity.
all_findings.dimension: Ledger / blockchain semantics parity (Block, Transaction, Header, TransactionAttribute, MemoryPool, verification pipeline, genesis)

all_findings.title: HeaderCache capacity is 50,000 vs C# 10,000, changing the Full predicate
all_findings.problem: Rust HeaderCache MAX_HEADERS=50_000 (header_cache.rs:7) vs C# HeaderCache.MaxHeaders=10_000 (HeaderCache.cs:27). The Full predicate (Count >= Max) gates OnNewHeaders acceptance and the 'skip reverify while header backlog present' logic in UpdatePoolForBlockPersisted. This changes header-sync/relay/mempool-reverify timing and memory footprint but does not affect block/tx hashes, serialization, or validity, so it is not consensus-breaking. The code comment acknowledges the intentional bump 'for faster sync'.
all_findings.recommendation: Set MAX_HEADERS=10_000 to match C# exactly so the Full-driven behaviors (header acceptance window, reverify gating) line up with the reference node. If a larger cache is desired for sync, document it as a deliberate, isolated deviation.
all_findings.dimension: Ledger / blockchain semantics parity (Block, Transaction, Header, TransactionAttribute, MemoryPool, verification pipeline, genesis)

all_findings.title: reverify_unverified_transactions always applies the verify_count=1 throttle; C# applies it only in the idle path
all_findings.problem: C# has two entry points: ReverifyTransactions(count,...) (called from UpdatePoolForBlockPersisted with MaxTransactionsPerBlock and no throttle) and ReVerifyTopUnverifiedTransactionsIfNeeded (idle) which reduces verifyCount to 1 when _sortedTransactions.Count > MaxTransactionsPerBlock (MemoryPool.cs:687). The Rust reverify_unverified_transactions (memory_pool/mod.rs:528-532) applies the 'verified.len() > MaxTransactionsPerBlock => 1' throttle UNCONDITIONALLY, so the post-persist reverification path is throttled where C# would reverify up to MaxTransactionsPerBlock. This only affects how many unverified txs get promoted per block; it does not affect validity or hashes.
all_findings.recommendation: Move the verify_count=1 throttle so it applies only to the idle reverify_top_unverified_transactions path (mirroring C# ReVerifyTopUnverifiedTransactionsIfNeeded), and have update_pool_for_block_persisted call the reverify routine with the full MaxTransactionsPerBlock budget unthrottled.
all_findings.dimension: Ledger / blockchain semantics parity (Block, Transaction, Header, TransactionAttribute, MemoryPool, verification pipeline, genesis)

all_findings.title: getunclaimedgas returns decimal GAS instead of raw datoshi
all_findings.problem: C# GetUnclaimedGas returns unclaimed.ToString() of the raw datoshi BigInteger (100000000 for 1 GAS). Rust unclaimed_gas.rs line 38 returns BigDecimal::new(unclaimed, decimals).to_string(); the BigDecimal Display in neo-primitives/src/big_decimal.rs divides by 10^8 and trims trailing zeros so 100000000 renders as 1. Any non-zero unclaimed balance mismatches C#. The unit test only checks unclaimed is a string on a zero-balance server where BigDecimal of 0 equals 0, masking the bug.
all_findings.recommendation: Return the raw integer string unclaimed.to_string() instead of wrapping in BigDecimal; add a test asserting the exact string for a known non-zero datoshi amount.

all_findings.title: getrawtransaction verbose emits an extra vmstate field not present in C#
all_findings.problem: C# GetRawTransaction verbose (RpcServer.Blockchain.cs lines 373 to 381) adds only blockhash, confirmations and blocktime to Transaction.ToJson; Transaction.ToJson has no vmstate. Rust rpc_server_blockchain/mod.rs lines 267 to 276 additionally inserts a vmstate field. Strict clients and response-diff tooling see an unexpected field.
all_findings.recommendation: Remove the vmstate insertion to match C# v3.9, or gate it behind a non-default compatibility flag and adjust the dedicated test.

all_findings.title: Relay-result error path drops the reason data and message suffix C# attaches
all_findings.problem: C# GetRelayResult (RpcServer.Node.cs lines 82 to 111) throws every non-success case with WithData(reason.ToString()), so the message gains a suffix and a data field. Rust map_relay_result (rpc_relay.rs lines 18 to 27) omits with_data for AlreadyExists, AlreadyInPool, OutOfMemory, InvalidScript, InvalidAttribute, InvalidSignature, OverSize, Expired, InsufficientFunds and PolicyFail, so both the message suffix and the data field are missing. Affects sendrawtransaction and submitblock error responses.
all_findings.recommendation: Append with_data of the matching VerifyResult variant name to every error arm in map_relay_result so message and data match C#.

all_findings.title: getblocksysfee is registered though C# v3.9 removed it
all_findings.problem: Rust registers getblocksysfee (rpc_server_blockchain/mod.rs line 46, handler lines 155 to 175). No GetBlockSysFee or getblocksysfee exists anywhere in the v3.9.1 C# RpcServer reference; it was removed. This is an extra method not in the C# surface and represents surface drift a parity test would flag.
all_findings.recommendation: Remove the registration and handler to match the C# v3.9 surface, or document it as an intentional extension after confirming the targeted C# tag.
all_findings.csharp_reference: neo_csharp/src/Plugins/RpcServer has no GetBlockSysFee in v3.9.1

all_findings.title: Faithful ParameterConverter is dead; handlers use divergent ad-hoc coercion helpers
all_findings.problem: parameter_converter (mod.rs 325 plus tests 674 plus signers 194 LOC) faithfully ports C# ParameterConverter with an RpcConvertible trait, but is invoked in exactly one place (smart_contract/helpers.rs line 60, SignersAndWitnesses). All other handlers parse via ad-hoc rpc_helpers.rs which diverges from C# coercion: expect_hash_param accepts hex or base64 whereas C# ToUInt256 is hex only; parse_verbose rejects string booleans whereas C# AsBoolean treats any non-empty string as true and numbers as nonzero; expect_u32_param uses as_u64 rejecting numeric strings and floats whereas C# ToNumeric uses AsNumber. About 1200 LOC of dead converter while the live path has parameter-coercion drift.
all_findings.recommendation: Route all handler parameter parsing through ParameterConverter to match C# coercion and delete the ad-hoc helpers, or delete ParameterConverter and align rpc_helpers with C# AsBoolean, AsNumber and ToUInt256 semantics; add string-boolean and numeric-string coercion tests.

all_findings.title: Three overlapping RpcError types; error_code.rs redundant with divergent messages
all_findings.problem: The crate defines src/server/rpc_error.rs RpcError (the faithful port used in responses), src/error_code.rs RpcErrorCode (233 LOC, referenced only by lib.rs re-export), and src/error.rs RpcError (client level). error_code.rs duplicates the code and message table and diverges: code 32603 message Internal error versus rpc_error and C# Internal server RpcError; code 32700 message Parse error versus rpc_error and C# Bad request. Not a wire bug today since error_code.rs is not used to build responses, but a maintenance trap.
all_findings.recommendation: Delete src/error_code.rs and keep server/rpc_error.rs as the single source of truth for codes and messages.

all_findings.title: 37K vs 4K LOC bloat is mostly a bundled client SDK plus tests
all_findings.problem: neo-rpc totals 37,818 LOC: about 13,242 tests (12,417 in src plus 825 tests dir), about 18,018 RpcClient SDK under src/client (5,875 LOC of response models corresponding to the non-vendored C# Neo.Network.RPC package), and about 10,476 server non-test versus C# RpcServer 4,107. Server inflation comes from genuine additions (rate limiter 661 LOC, TLS, prometheus, WebSocket subscriptions about 1,036 LOC absent in C#, env-var listplugins shim) plus the dead ParameterConverter and redundant error_code.rs.
all_findings.recommendation: Split the RpcClient SDK (src/client) into its own crate so the server is comparable to C# RpcServer and the dependency graph reflects server versus client boundaries; treat WebSocket subscriptions as a documented extension.
all_findings.csharp_reference: neo_csharp/src/Plugins/RpcServer is server only; Neo.Network.RPC is not vendored

all_findings.title: listplugins synthesized with env-var compat shims instead of real plugin enumeration
all_findings.problem: C# ListPlugins (RpcServer.Utilities.cs lines 35 to 48) enumerates Plugin.Plugins with version from u.Version and interfaces whose names end in Plugin. Rust list_plugins (rpc_server_utilities.rs lines 39 to 103) has no plugin system so it synthesizes entries from detected services with hardcoded interface names and two env-var modes (NEO_LISTPLUGINS_COMPAT and NEO_PLUGIN_VERSION) that switch names and version. Interface strings and version will not match a real C# node and env-var response shaping is a smell.
all_findings.recommendation: Align interface naming with the C# convention (names ending in Plugin) and move the env-var compat shims out of production code paths into test fixtures.

all_findings.title: Unhandled handler errors collapse to 32603 instead of preserving the original code
all_findings.problem: C# ProcessRequestAsync (RpcServer.cs lines 353 to 371) returns NewCustomError(unwrapped.HResult, unwrapped.Message) for non-RpcException failures, preserving the exception code and message. Rust invoke_rpc_handler (routes/handlers.rs lines 309 to 345) only catches panics via catch_unwind and always returns internal_server_error (code 32603); explicit RpcException errors propagate fine but any non-RpcException failure surfaced through a panic loses its specific code and message.
all_findings.recommendation: Map typed internal failures to the closest RpcError code and message rather than panicking; for genuine panics the generic 32603 is acceptable but consider including the panic message as data.

all_findings.title: RocksDB store/provider lives in neo-core but is a storage backend (C# ships it as a plugin)
all_findings.problem: neo-core/src/persistence/providers/rocksdb (1,336 LOC: store.rs 810, provider.rs 297, tests 222) is the real production persistence backend, but it lives in neo-core behind the `rocksdb` feature. It implements ONLY neo-storage traits (impl StoreProvider, ReadOnlyStore, WriteStore<Vec<u8>,Vec<u8>>, ReadOnlyStoreGeneric<StorageKey,StorageItem> — see store.rs:97,139,231,233 and provider.rs:100). Its only neo-core couplings are `crate::smart_contract::{StorageItem, StorageKey}` (rocksdb/store.rs:13), and BOTH of those are mere re-exports of neo_storage types (persistence/storage_item.rs:17 `pub use neo_storage::StorageItem`; persistence/storage_key.rs:6 `pub use neo_storage::StorageKey`). neo-storage itself has NO rocksdb dependency and ships only memory providers (neo-storage/src/persistence/providers: memory_store/snapshot/provider). This inverts the C# layering, where Neo.Persistence core has only abstractions+memory Providers and RocksDBStore/LevelDBStore are standalone plugin assemblies (neo_csharp/src/Plugins/RocksDBStore).
all_findings.recommendation: Move neo-core/src/persistence/providers/rocksdb into neo-storage behind a `rocksdb` feature (or a new neo-storage-rocksdb crate), depending only on neo-storage's own StorageItem/StorageKey/Store traits. neo-core then re-exports RocksDBStoreProvider when its `rocksdb` feature is on. Removes ~1.3K LOC from neo-core and matches C# plugin separation.
all_findings.csharp_reference: neo_csharp/src/Plugins/RocksDBStore/ (standalone plugin); neo_csharp/src/Neo/Persistence/Providers (core has memory only)

all_findings.title: Generic actor runtime (1.8K LOC, zero neo coupling) should be its own crate
all_findings.problem: neo-core/src/actors (1,814 LOC: actor_system, actor_ref, mailbox, event_stream, props, context, supervision) is a fully generic tokio/dashmap/uuid-based actor runtime. It has ZERO `crate::` couplings (verified: grep of `use crate::` in actors/*.rs returns nothing; deps are only std/tokio/async_trait/tokio_util/parking_lot/uuid/thiserror/dashmap). It is exported as `neo_core::runtime` (lib.rs:240, feature `runtime`) and is consumed CROSS-CRATE — neo-rpc/src/server/rpc_relay.rs:7 imports `neo_core::runtime::{Actor,ActorContext,ActorRef,ActorResult,ActorSystem,Props}`. Forcing neo-rpc to pull in all of neo-core just for the actor primitives is a heavyweight dependency. The network layer (local_node, remote_node, task_manager, peer) is the primary consumer (mirrors C# Akka.NET usage).
all_findings.recommendation: Extract neo-core/src/actors into a standalone `neo-actors` crate (no neo-* deps). neo-core depends on it and re-exports as `neo_core::runtime` for compatibility; neo-rpc depends on neo-actors directly instead of all of neo-core. Removes ~1.8K LOC from neo-core and breaks the neo-rpc→neo-core fat dependency for actor primitives.
all_findings.csharp_reference: C# uses external Akka.NET (Neo.Akka) — actor runtime is not part of Neo.csproj logic

all_findings.title: Four C# plugin assemblies are inlined into neo-core (oracle/state/tokens-tracker/application-logs, ~12.2K LOC)
all_findings.problem: C# ships OracleService, StateService, TokensTracker, ApplicationLogs as separate Neo.Plugins.* assemblies (confirmed under neo_csharp/src/Plugins/). neo-rs inlines all four into neo-core: oracle_service (5,294 LOC), state_service (4,386), tokens_tracker (2,068), application_logs (562) = ~12,310 LOC. The workspace Cargo.toml even documents this inlining as deliberate. Their core couplings are real but plugin-shaped: state_service touches smart_contract(11), persistence(4), network(3), ledger(3), wallets(2); application_logs touches smart_contract/persistence/ledger/neo_vm/neo_system; tokens_tracker touches persistence/neo_io/neo_ledger; oracle_service touches wallets/network/neo_system. These are consumers of core, not core itself — keeping them inlined inflates neo-core and prevents building a node without them.
all_findings.recommendation: Phase these out of neo-core into dedicated crates (neo-oracle-service, neo-state-service, neo-tokens-tracker, neo-application-logs) once neo-core exposes a stable public API for smart_contract/persistence/ledger/neo_system. application_logs and tokens_tracker are the lowest-coupling and best first candidates. If full extraction is deferred, at minimum feature-gate all four (oracle is already gated; state_service/tokens_tracker/application_logs largely are not) so a minimal node need not compile them. Targets ~12K LOC reduction.

all_findings.title: compression module is a 23-LOC pure facade over neo-io
all_findings.problem: neo-core/src/compression/mod.rs (23 LOC) only wraps neo_io_crate::{compress_lz4, decompress_lz4, COMPRESSION_MIN_SIZE, COMPRESSION_THRESHOLD}, converting IoError→CoreError. The actual LZ4 logic already lives in neo-io (neo-io/src/compression.rs). This top-level module exists solely as an error-type shim. C# Neo's compression helpers live in Neo.IO, not a separate core surface.
all_findings.recommendation: Eliminate the top-level `compression` module. Callers needing the CoreError conversion can call neo_io_crate directly and map at the call site, or the thin wrapper can be folded into persistence/compression.rs (which already re-uses crate::compression). Removes a top-level module and one indirection layer.
all_findings.csharp_reference: neo_csharp/src/Neo.IO (compression in IO layer, not a core module)

all_findings.title: neo-core/persistence partly duplicates neo-storage type/module surface (storage_item, storage_key, data_cache)
all_findings.problem: neo-core/src/persistence is a mixed facade: ~half its modules are `pub use neo_storage::persistence::...` re-exports (index, read_cache, read_only_store, seek_direction, store, store_cache, store_factory, store_provider, store_snapshot, track_state, transaction, write_store — see persistence/mod.rs:3-20), while storage_item.rs/storage_key.rs/data_cache/mod.rs are shims that re-export the neo_storage canonical types and then layer extension traits (StorageItemExt at storage_item.rs, which couples to smart_contract BinarySerializer/Interoperable + neo_vm ExecutionEngineLimits). This split surface (canonical type in neo-storage, extension trait in neo-core) is defensible but creates two import paths for the same types (neo_storage::StorageItem vs neo_core::persistence::StorageItem vs neo_core::smart_contract::StorageItem at smart_contract/mod.rs:69) and makes the boundary ambiguous.
all_findings.recommendation: Keep the StorageItemExt extension trait in neo-core (it genuinely needs smart_contract/neo_vm types) but collapse the pure re-export shims: have neo-core re-export the neo_storage modules in one place and document neo_storage as the single canonical import path. Drop redundant per-type shim files (storage_key.rs is just `pub use neo_storage::StorageKey` plus tests). Reduces import-path confusion without functional change.

all_findings.title: neo-core/telemetry overlaps neo-telemetry crate (two metrics implementations)
