all_findings.problem: neo-core/src/telemetry (796 LOC: metrics.rs Counter/Gauge/Histogram, recorder.rs MetricsRecorder/Snapshot) is a second metrics implementation alongside the neo-telemetry crate (1,988 LOC, which also has metrics.rs/node_metrics.rs plus health/logging/system). The neo-core module's own doc-comment (telemetry/mod.rs:3-20) acknowledges the overlap and tries to carve a boundary ("internal lightweight metrics" vs "production Prometheus endpoint"). This is two hand-rolled Counter/Gauge/Histogram implementations of the same primitives. C# uses a single telemetry surface; there is no reason neo-core needs its own atomic counters when neo-telemetry exists.
all_findings.recommendation: Move the lightweight Counter/Gauge/Histogram/MetricsRecorder primitives into neo-telemetry (e.g. a `core` or `lightweight` feature/module with no Prometheus/system deps) and have neo-core depend on that, deleting neo-core/src/telemetry. Removes ~0.8K LOC and a duplicate metrics primitive set. If neo-core cannot take a neo-telemetry dep for layering reasons, document the canonical owner and ensure the two are not silently divergent.

all_findings.title: neo-core/network/p2p consolidation with neo-p2p is correct and should continue
all_findings.problem: Positive finding (no defect, scope guidance). neo-core/network (16,964 LOC) keeps the actor-driven networking machinery (remote_node 2.4K, local_node 2.2K, task_manager 1.6K, peer/connection/framed) AND the ledger-coupled wire payloads (payloads/ 5.3K: Block, Transaction, Signer, Header, ExtensiblePayload, MerkleBlock — these embed ledger+smart_contract types so the coupling is genuine and justifies staying in core). Meanwhile the simple stateless wire payloads/enums are now correctly sourced from neo-p2p: network/p2p/mod.rs:69-83 and payloads/mod.rs:20-38 re-export neo_p2p::{MessageCommand, MessageFlags, ChannelsConfig, timeouts, addr/inv/ping/version/filter payloads, NodeCapability}. Recent commits (0e95b69d, 8c47e30f) deleted the neo-core duplicates of MessageFlags/MessageCommand. This is the right direction; only 26 neo_p2p references exist so far.
all_findings.recommendation: Continue migrating remaining stateless wire types to neo-p2p as the canonical owner (e.g. verify inventory_type, node_capability_type, verify_result, contains_transaction_type are sourced from neo-p2p not re-defined). Do NOT move Block/Tx/Signer/Header payloads — their ledger+smart_contract coupling is genuine and they correctly stay in neo-core, matching C# where Network.P2P.Payloads lives in Neo.csproj alongside Ledger. No action needed beyond finishing the in-progress consolidation.
all_findings.csharp_reference: neo_csharp/src/Neo/Network/P2P/Payloads (Block/Tx/Signer in core); message primitives shared

all_findings.title: smart_contract/native (21.4K LOC) is the dominant core mass; candidate for neo-native-contracts crate
all_findings.problem: smart_contract is 35,313 LOC, of which native/ is 21,444 LOC (NeoToken governance/bonus/committee, GasToken, PolicyContract, ContractManagement, RoleManagement, Notary, CryptoLib/bls12381, StdLib, LedgerContract, OracleContract, etc.). This is the single largest sub-area of neo-core and is consensus-critical native contract state. In C# these live within Neo.csproj (Neo.SmartContract.Native) so co-location with core is C#-faithful, but the sheer size (~21% of neo-core) means a clean module/crate seam (neo-native-contracts) would substantially shrink neo-core's apparent surface. It is tightly coupled to application_engine, persistence (StorageKey prefixes), and neo_vm, so extraction is non-trivial.
all_findings.recommendation: Lower priority than the plugin/RocksDB/actors extractions. If pursued, extract smart_contract/native into a neo-native-contracts crate that depends on a stable application_engine + persistence interface from neo-core. Otherwise leave in core (C#-faithful) and treat the test files (native/*/tests.rs total several thousand LOC, e.g. policy_contract/tests.rs 1231, contract_management/tests.rs 865) — consider whether large in-module test files should move to neo-core/tests to reduce src LOC. Verify byte-level prefix/price parity separately (other audit dimension).

all_findings.title: Server feature compiles the entire RPC client SDK (incl. reqwest) for a single type
all_findings.problem: neo-rpc's `server` feature transitively enables the full `client` feature (Cargo.toml:64 'client,  # Server uses client models for JSON serialization'), which pulls reqwest, the HTTP RpcClient, transaction_manager, nep17_api, wallet_api, policy_api, state_api, contract_client — ~13,373 LOC of client SDK. In reality the server imports client code in exactly ONE place: `use crate::client::models::RpcContractState;` (src/server/rpc_server_blockchain/mod.rs:3). neo-node, the only production binary, enables only `features=["server"]` (neo-node/Cargo.toml:41) and never uses the client API. So every node build compiles ~13K LOC of unused SDK plus the entire reqwest stack.
all_findings.recommendation: Split the client into its own crate (e.g. neo-rpc-client) mirroring C#'s separate Neo.Network.RPC package, OR at minimum move the handful of shared response DTOs (RpcContractState etc.) into a small `models` module that the server can depend on without the client/reqwest stack. Decouple `server` from `client` in Cargo.toml so building the node does not compile reqwest or the SDK.
all_findings.csharp_reference: neo_csharp/src/Plugins/RpcServer (server only; Neo.Network.RPC is a separate package)

all_findings.title: Block/header JSON wire format is encoded in three independent places (drift risk)
all_findings.problem: The consensus/interop-visible block & header JSON shape is implemented three times: (1) server-authoritative builder `header_fields_to_map`/`block_to_json` in src/server/rpc_server_blockchain/mod.rs:621-662 (e.g. nonce as `format!("{:016X}")`, fields hash/size/version/previousblockhash/merkleroot/time/nonce/index/primary/nextconsensus/witnesses/confirmations/nextblockhash); (2) client parser/serializer `RpcBlockHeader::from_json`/`to_json` in src/client/models/rpc_block_header.rs:37+ (parses nonce via from_str_radix base16); (3) a `#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize)]` on the same struct (rpc_block_header.rs:22). Unlike C#, where serialization lives once on the core type (`block.ToJson(system.Settings)` then RpcServer just appends confirmations/nextblockhash — neo_csharp/src/Plugins/RpcServer/RpcServer.Blockchain.cs:103,230), neo-core's Block/Header have NO to_json (transactions do: src/network/p2p/payloads/transaction/json.rs, and the server reuses it at rpc_server_blockchain/mod.rs:601). The three copies can drift in field names/encodings and silently break interop.
all_findings.recommendation: Add `to_json(&ProtocolSettings)` to neo-core Block/Header (matching the existing Transaction::to_json), make the RPC server call it and append only confirmations/nextblockhash (as C# does), and have the client model parse that same canonical output. Eliminate the server's hand-built header map and the client's parallel to_json.

all_findings.title: Two parallel parameter-parsing systems; the trait-based one (~1K LOC) is used once
all_findings.problem: The server has a full trait-based parameter converter mirroring C#'s ParameterConverter: `RpcConvertible`/`ParameterConverter::convert<T>` with impls for String/bool/numeric/Vec<u8>/Address/BlockHashOrIndex/ContractNameOrHashOrId/Uuid/SignersAndWitnesses/Vec<ContractParameter> (src/server/parameter_converter/mod.rs, 325 LOC + 674 test LOC + signers.rs 194). It is invoked in exactly ONE call site: ParameterConverter::convert::<SignersAndWitnesses> (src/server/smart_contract/helpers.rs:60). Every other handler instead uses ad-hoc `expect_*_param`/`parse_uint*` helpers (46 call sites across handler modules). So ~1K LOC of abstraction exists to serve a single conversion, while the rest of the server ignores it.
all_findings.recommendation: Pick one system. Either route all handlers through ParameterConverter (justifying its existence and matching C#'s single ParameterConverter), or delete the trait module and keep only the small `expect_*` helpers, replacing the one SignersAndWitnesses use with a direct parser. Do not maintain both.

all_findings.title: Duplicated expect_u32_param / expect_hash_param with divergent error messages
all_findings.problem: `expect_u32_param` is defined three times with non-identical behavior: rpc_helpers.rs:113 (error text 'expects integer parameter'), rpc_server_blockchain/mod.rs:697 (error text 'expects numeric parameter'), and rpc_server_state.rs:288 (`expect_u32`). `expect_hash_param` is likewise duplicated in rpc_helpers.rs:178, rpc_server_blockchain/mod.rs:711, and rpc_server_application_logs.rs:67. Beyond the copy-paste, the diverging error strings mean two methods reject the same bad input with different messages — an observable, untracked behavioral inconsistency for clients.
all_findings.recommendation: Delete the per-module copies and import the canonical helpers from rpc_helpers.rs everywhere. Standardize the invalid-params message to match C# RpcError.InvalidParams.WithData wording so error `data` strings are consistent.

all_findings.title: Hand-rolled HTTP/TLS plumbing duplicated across two accept loops; warp + manual hyper + tokio_rustls
all_findings.problem: rpc_server.rs (616 LOC) builds the listener by hand: warp::Filter for routing but then `hyper::Server::builder` with a manual `stream::unfold` accept loop, once for the TLS path (TlsAcceptor from tokio_rustls, ~lines 264-363) and a near-identical second copy for the plain path (~lines 371-452). TLS cert handling adds rpc_tls.rs (111) + rpc_transport.rs (146) with p12/sha1 parsing. Total ~870 LOC reimplement what C# gets from Kestrel in a few config lines (neo_csharp/src/Plugins/RpcServer/RpcServer.cs:158-182 UseKestrel/UseHttps). The Cargo.lock already includes hyper and tokio-rustls, so axum/tower (same ecosystem) or jsonrpsee's built-in server would replace the accept loops, TLS wiring, and routing.
all_findings.recommendation: Replace warp + manual hyper/TLS with axum + tower (axum-server or tokio-rustls acceptor handles both plain and TLS through one path) or commit to jsonrpsee's server for the JSON-RPC transport. Collapse the duplicated TLS/plain accept loops into one parameterized path. Expected reduction ~400-600 LOC and removal of the warp dependency.

all_findings.title: Two HTTP/RPC stacks coexist: warp transport plus a jsonrpsee adapter
all_findings.problem: The primary transport is warp (src/server/routes/mod.rs, rpc_server.rs), but there is also a separate `jsonrpsee_adapter` (src/server/jsonrpsee_adapter.rs, 116 LOC + tests/jsonrpsee_adapter.rs 286 LOC) behind the `jsonrpsee-server` feature, plus conditional re-exports `invoke_rpc_handler`/`resolve_rpc_handler` (routes/mod.rs:27). Maintaining two RPC server stacks doubles the surface that must stay parity-faithful (dispatch, error mapping, batch handling) and adds the jsonrpsee 0.24.10 dependency pin (Cargo.toml:53-55 notes it is held back).
all_findings.recommendation: Decide on one transport. If jsonrpsee is the intended target (it gives batch handling, error codes, and a server for free), migrate fully and drop warp + the hand-rolled routes/handlers; if warp/axum is canonical, remove the jsonrpsee adapter and feature. Avoid shipping both long-term.

all_findings.title: Client models double-maintain serde derive and hand-rolled JToken from_json/to_json
all_findings.problem: Of the client model files, 33 carry `#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize)]` yet there are 72 hand-written `from_json`/`to_json` methods over the same structs (src/client/models/*.rs). Example: RpcBlockHeader derives Serialize/Deserialize (rpc_block_header.rs:22) and also hand-parses every field from neo_json::JToken (rpc_block_header.rs:37-90), because the Neo wire format uses non-serde-friendly encodings (nonce as hex string, addresses, base64). Maintaining both a derive and a manual codec per type is error-prone and inflates the client by several thousand lines.
all_findings.recommendation: For each model, keep ONE serialization path. Prefer serde with `#[serde(with=...)]`/`serialize_with` helpers for the Neo-specific encodings (hex nonce, base64, UInt256 strings) so the derive is authoritative and the hand-rolled JToken codecs are deleted. Where neo_json::JToken interop is required, generate it from the serde Value rather than re-implementing field-by-field.

all_findings.title: Reducibility estimate: ~6-9K source LOC removable; clarify the 3.7x is partly client SDK + added features, not pure bloat
all_findings.problem: The 3.7x figure conflates three distinct things. Verified split: tests ~13,242 LOC; client SDK ~13,373 non-test (no in-tree consumer; corresponds to a separate C# package, not RestServer); server ~10,476 non-test. Of the server, genuinely-added features with no C# RpcServer equivalent are the rate limiter (src/server/middleware/rate_limiter.rs, 661 — confirmed absent in C# RpcServer) and WebSocket subscriptions (src/server/ws/*, 1,053 — absent in C# RpcServer). So the inflation vs C# RpcServer's 4.1K is not 3.7x of duplicated logic; the reducible portion is the duplication/over-abstraction above.
all_findings.recommendation: Achievable source reductions: (a) extract client to its own crate so node builds drop ~13K LOC + reqwest; (b) replace hand-rolled HTTP/TLS with axum/tower or jsonrpsee (-400 to -600); (c) collapse the two param systems (-300 to -1000); (d) centralize block/header JSON in neo-core and drop client double-codec (-1000 to -2000 across server+client). Net realistic source reduction ~6-9K LOC plus a cleaner crate boundary, without losing the rate-limiter/WS features.
all_findings.rust_location: neo-rpc/ (whole crate); features Cargo.toml; client vs server split

all_findings.title: StorageItem carries an is_constant flag that does not exist in C# v3.9 (latent serialization footgun)
all_findings.problem: neo-storage/src/types/storage_item.rs:31 defines `is_constant: bool` and `StorageValue::to_storage_bytes()` (line 245) serializes `[is_constant_byte] ++ value`. The in-tree C# StorageItem (neo_csharp/src/Neo/SmartContract/StorageItem.cs) has NO IsConstant field at all in v3.9 and its only serialization is `Serialize(writer) => writer.Write(Value.Span)` (line 273-276) — raw value bytes with no flag. The `Constant` concept was removed from Neo's StorageItem. If `to_storage_bytes()`/`from_storage_bytes()` were ever wired into the DB or MPT value path (they currently are not — the live path uses `to_value()`), every stored value would gain a leading byte and corrupt state root / break interop. The `is_constant` field also pollutes PartialEq (line 186) so two items with identical bytes but different flags compare unequal, which diverges from C# StorageItem semantics.
all_findings.recommendation: Remove the `is_constant` field and `constant()` constructor from StorageItem to match C# v3.9. If a non-consensus 'constant' marker is needed for the syscall layer, track it outside the persisted type. At minimum, delete the `StorageValue::to_storage_bytes/from_storage_bytes` impl for StorageItem (storage_item.rs:244-268) so no code can accidentally serialize the flag, and drop `is_constant` from the PartialEq.

all_findings.title: persistence/serialization.rs uses bincode but is documented as 'matches C# Neo exactly'
all_findings.problem: neo-core/src/persistence/serialization.rs:10-25 provides `serialize`/`deserialize` via `bincode::serialize`/`bincode::deserialize` with doc comments 'production implementation matching C# Neo exactly'. Bincode is a Rust-specific format with no relationship to C# (Neo uses its own ISerializable little-endian/var-length encoding). The comment is actively misleading and the functions are not used anywhere in the persistence write path (grep for call sites returns none outside the module). A future caller trusting the doc comment could persist bincode bytes into a consensus-relevant store and silently break parity.
all_findings.recommendation: Either delete the bincode `serialize`/`deserialize` helpers (dead code) or, if a generic binary helper is genuinely needed, remove the false 'matches C# Neo exactly' claim and rename to make clear it is an internal-only format. The `serialize_neo_binary`/`deserialize_neo_binary` helpers (using the real Serializable trait) are the correct C#-compatible path and should be the only documented one.

all_findings.title: Split ownership: RocksDB backend lives in neo-core/persistence, not neo-storage (or its own crate)
all_findings.problem: neo-storage defines the `Store`/`StoreSnapshot`/`StoreProvider` traits and a global `StoreFactory` registry (neo-storage/src/persistence/store_factory.rs) but contains only the memory backend. The sole persistent backend, RocksDB (neo-core/src/persistence/providers/rocksdb/{store.rs,provider.rs}, ~700+ lines), lives in neo-core and registers itself into the factory at runtime. This mirrors C#'s core (Neo.Persistence with MemoryStore) vs plugin (Plugins/RocksDBStore) split, so it is defensible, but burying the 'plugin' inside the monolithic neo-core crate means neo-core unconditionally carries the librocksdb-sys build dependency (behind a feature) and the storage backend cannot be consumed without pulling all of neo-core. C# isolates it in its own assembly.
all_findings.recommendation: Extract the RocksDB provider into a dedicated `neo-rocksdb-store` crate that depends only on neo-storage (mirroring the C# RocksDBStore plugin). This makes neo-storage + neo-rocksdb-store a self-contained storage stack, lets non-core consumers use persistent storage, and removes the rocksdb dependency from neo-core's surface. The StoreFactory registration pattern already supports this cleanly.

all_findings.title: neo-core/persistence is largely a re-export facade duplicating neo-storage module structure
all_findings.problem: neo-core/src/persistence/mod.rs re-exports ~15 modules verbatim from neo_storage::persistence (index, read_cache, read_only_store, seek_direction, store, store_cache, store_factory, store_provider, store_snapshot, track_state, transaction, write_store) and the files storage_key.rs, storage_item.rs(partial), data_cache/mod.rs, providers.rs are thin `pub use neo_storage::...` shims (e.g. storage_key.rs:6 `pub use neo_storage::StorageKey;`, data_cache/mod.rs re-exports cache::*/trackable::*). The result is two parallel module trees for the same types, so call sites import StorageKey/StorageItem through ~6 different paths (`crate::smart_contract::StorageKey`, `crate::persistence::storage_key::StorageKey`, `neo_core::persistence::StorageItem`, `neo_storage::StorageKey`, etc.), increasing cognitive load and making it ambiguous which crate is canonical. neo-storage's lib.rs already declares itself the 'single source of truth'.
all_findings.recommendation: Collapse the facade: in neo-core, keep only the genuinely neo-core-specific code (StorageItemExt, write_batch_buffer, the rocksdb provider until extracted) and re-export the canonical types from a single location (e.g. `pub use neo_storage::{StorageKey, StorageItem, ...}` in smart_contract/mod.rs only). Delete the per-module re-export shims (storage_key.rs, storage_item.rs re-export portion, providers.rs, data_cache/mod.rs) and update imports to go through neo-storage or the single smart_contract facade. Pick one canonical import path and enforce it.

all_findings.title: neo-storage carries an index.rs (custom secondary indexing) with no C# counterpart
all_findings.problem: neo-storage/src/persistence/index.rs defines IndexConfig/IndexType (BTree/Hash) secondary-index machinery. There is no equivalent in C# Neo.Persistence — Neo's storage is a flat key/value store with prefix-seek only; secondary indexes are not part of the protocol or the C# storage layer. This is speculative feature surface (YAGNI) that adds maintenance burden and risks divergence if it ever influences seek/find ordering. Similarly, the read_cache bloom-filter + prefetch modules (neo-storage/src/persistence/read_cache/, data_cache/prefetch.rs) are performance add-ons beyond C#; they appear correctly isolated from the find/seek ordering but should be confirmed not to alter iteration semantics.
all_findings.recommendation: Confirm index.rs has no live callers and remove it (and any unused read_cache/prefetch surface) to keep neo-storage aligned with the C# flat-KV model. If a caching/prefetch layer is retained for performance, add a regression test asserting find()/seek() ordering and membership exactly match the no-cache path so it can never drift from C# DataCache.Seek semantics.

all_findings.title: DataCache/StoreCache and ClonedCache analog correctly centralized in neo-storage (positive parity confirmation)
all_findings.problem: Confirmed correct layering for the cache hierarchy: DataCache + Trackable (neo-storage/src/persistence/data_cache/cache.rs) implement the C# DataCache change-tracking semantics (Added/Changed/Deleted/None/NotFound), `clone_cache` (cache.rs:199) is the ClonedCache analog (matching neo_csharp/src/Neo/Persistence/ClonedCache.cs), and StoreCache (store_cache.rs) corresponds to C# StoreCache/SnapshotCache backing the cache with a store or snapshot, committing via raw `key.to_array()`+`item.to_value()` (store_cache.rs:202) exactly like C# StoreCache.AddInternal/UpdateInternal using `key.ToArray()`+`value.ToArray()`. No action required beyond ensuring future changes keep DataCache the single canonical implementation (the neo-core data_cache/mod.rs shim already re-exports it).
all_findings.recommendation: No change needed; documented as a parity-correct anchor. When collapsing the neo-core facade, keep DataCache/StoreCache/Trackable as the canonical neo-storage implementations and ensure the commit path continues to use to_value() (raw bytes) rather than any flag-prefixed serialization.

all_findings.title: RPC JSON output escaping diverges from C# Utf8JsonWriter (JavaScriptEncoder.Default)
all_findings.problem: C# serializes all RPC responses via JToken.ToString -> ToByteArray -> Utf8JsonWriter (neo_csharp/src/Neo.Json/JToken.cs:240-247, and RpcServer.cs:310 `response.ToString()`). The default Utf8JsonWriter encoder is JavaScriptEncoder.Default, which escapes '<', '>', '&', '\'', '+' and ALL non-ASCII characters to \uXXXX. The Rust RPC server instead builds a serde_json::Value and serializes it with serde_json::to_vec (neo-rpc/src/server/routes/mod.rs:172), and serde_json only escapes the JSON-mandatory set (\", \\, control chars) while emitting raw UTF-8 for non-ASCII and leaving <, >, &, +, ' unescaped. Any RPC field carrying a non-ASCII or special character (NEP-17/NEP-11 token name/symbol, contract manifest, notification string args, error data) yields byte-different responses. neo-json's own JToken serializer (j_token.rs:270-300) also uses serde_json's PrettyFormatter and therefore has the same divergence, so it would not fix it either.
all_findings.recommendation: Match C#'s escaping for RPC output. Implement a serde_json custom Formatter that escapes '<','>','&','+','\'' and all code points > 0x7F as \uXXXX (surrogate pairs for astral chars), matching JavaScriptEncoder.Default, and route all RPC response serialization (and neo-json JToken::to_byte_array) through it. Add round-trip parity tests against C# output for strings containing '<', '&', and non-ASCII (e.g. a token symbol like an emoji or CJK).
all_findings.dimension: Foundation crates cohesion + idiomatic Rust + ecosystem reuse

all_findings.title: Duplicate, divergent ProtocolSettings in neo-config vs neo-core (RPC client signs with the wrong one)
all_findings.problem: Two ProtocolSettings structs exist. The canonical consensus one is neo-core/src/protocol_settings.rs (19.5K): standby_committee: Vec<ECPoint>, validators_count: i32, hardforks: HashMap<Hardfork,u32>, memory_pool_max_transactions: i32, initial_gas_distribution: u64, with defaults matching C# (max_transactions_per_block=512, memory_pool=50_000, max_valid_until_block_increment=5760, max_traceable_blocks=2_102_400). The second is neo-config/src/protocol.rs: standby_validators: Vec<String> (hex), validators_count: u32, custom HardforkHeights/NativeActivationHeights structs, memory_pool_max_transactions: u32, initial_gas_distribution: i64, ms_per_block: u64. These model the same protocol concept with different field names, types, and a fundamentally different validator/hardfork representation. The neo-rpc CLIENT (transaction_manager.rs:58/204/209, utility.rs:78/144) reads neo_config::ProtocolSettings.network and .address_version to compute transaction sign data and script hashes. If the two ever drift, client-side signing produces invalid signatures / wrong addresses. Magic values currently agree (860833102) but are duplicated as literals in neo-config/protocol.rs:193,249 instead of referencing neo_primitives::constants::MAINNET_MAGIC/TESTNET_MAGIC (which the neo-core path uses).
all_findings.recommendation: Pick one canonical ProtocolSettings. Preferred: make neo-config own the single ProtocolSettings (aligned field-for-field with C# ProtocolSettings: StandbyCommittee as ECPoint, Hardforks as Hardfork-enum map, matching integer signedness) and have neo-core re-export/consume it instead of defining its own. At minimum, replace neo-config's hardcoded magic literals with neo_primitives::constants references and add a conversion + equality test asserting the two structs produce identical network/address_version/defaults to prevent signing drift.
all_findings.dimension: Foundation crates cohesion + idiomatic Rust + ecosystem reuse

all_findings.title: Parallel, near-unused BinaryReader/Writer extension traits duplicate MemoryReader var-int logic
all_findings.problem: neo-io ships two reader/writer systems. The dominant one is MemoryReader (neo-io/src/memory_reader.rs) which has its own read_array, read_var_int(max), read_var_bytes, read_u8/16/32/64 and is used in ~53 sites across neo-core and neo-p2p (e.g. neo-p2p/src/payloads/network_address_with_time.rs:84 read_array::<16>, neo-p2p/src/message.rs:90 read_var_bytes). In parallel, neo-io/src/extensions/binary_reader.rs defines BinaryReaderExtensions over std::io::Read with its OWN read_var_int/read_var_bytes/read_array implementations (a third copy of the var-int decode after var_int.rs and memory_reader.rs). Searching neo-core/neo-p2p/neo-rpc/neo-consensus/neo-storage/neo-crypto/neo-node finds only re-export sites for BinaryReaderExtensions, no actual method call sites, indicating it is effectively dead duplicate code.
all_findings.recommendation: Confirm with a workspace-wide call-site search, then delete extensions/binary_reader.rs and extensions/binary_writer.rs (and their re-exports in neo-io/src/lib.rs and neo-core/src/extensions/io/mod.rs) if unused, or refactor them to delegate to the shared var_int.rs helpers so the var-int decode lives in exactly one place. This removes a third copy of consensus-relevant var-int parsing.
all_findings.dimension: Foundation crates cohesion + idiomatic Rust + ecosystem reuse

all_findings.title: neo-json is justified but bypassed in the RPC hot path (partial dead weight)
all_findings.problem: neo-json correctly does NOT reinvent serde_json: JToken::parse/to_byte_array delegate to serde_json::Deserializer/Serializer/PrettyFormatter (j_token.rs:258-300) and OrderedDictionary wraps indexmap::IndexMap (ordered_dictionary.rs:9). The bespoke layer (JToken/JObject/JArray, ordered keys, duplicate-key rejection, depth limit, JSONPath, C#-style AsBoolean/AsNumber coercions) is genuine Neo semantics and warrants a wrapper. However, the actual RPC server response/parameter path operates on serde_json::Value and Map (neo-rpc/src/server/routes/mod.rs:142-172, rpc_server_state.rs:198-208), using neo-json mainly for inbound parameter parsing. So the ordered-output and C#-escaping guarantees neo-json was built to provide are not actually exercised on the wire, while a second JSON model is maintained.
all_findings.recommendation: Decide neo-json's role explicitly: either (a) route RPC response serialization through neo-json JToken (so ordered keys + C#-matching escaping are guaranteed end-to-end), or (b) if serde_json::Value remains the wire model, trim neo-json to the value-add pieces (coercions, JSONPath, depth/duplicate-key validation) layered on serde_json::Value and drop the parallel JToken value type. Keep MAX_JSON_DEPTH=64 enforcement either way.
all_findings.dimension: Foundation crates cohesion + idiomatic Rust + ecosystem reuse

all_findings.title: neo-crypto: idiomatic, audited-crate-based, no hand-rolled primitives (positive finding)
all_findings.problem: Audit confirms neo-crypto does not hand-roll any cryptographic primitive: ECC via k256/p256/ed25519-dalek/secp256k1 (ecc.rs:14-33, signature.rs), hashing via sha2/sha3/ripemd/blake2/blake2b_simd (hash.rs:10-13), BLS12-381 via blst min_sig (bls12381.rs:8-9, DST = BLS_SIG_BLS12381G1_XMD:SHA-256_SSWU_RO_NUL_), murmur via murmur3 crate, constant-time via subtle and secret zeroization via zeroize. Merkle hash_pair concatenates left||right then Crypto::hash256, matching C# MerkleTree.Concat (neo_csharp MerkleTree.cs:67-78). One minor robustness note: EcPoint::from_bytes tries secp256r1 then falls back to secp256k1 (ecc.rs:187-191), whereas Neo selects the curve by context (CheckSig defaults to secp256r1); the fallback is harmless for parsing but verification callers should always pass an explicit curve.
all_findings.recommendation: No security action required; keep the established crates. Add a brief doc note that curve-explicit verify_signature_with_curve is the canonical path and from_bytes' secp256r1->secp256k1 fallback is parse-only, to avoid future misuse.
all_findings.dimension: Foundation crates cohesion + idiomatic Rust + ecosystem reuse

all_findings.title: neo-primitives UInt160/UInt256 Ord and word layout verified consensus-correct
all_findings.problem: UInt160 (fields value1:u64,value2:u64,value3:u32) and UInt256 (four u64 words) are generated by the uint_type! macro (neo-primitives/src/macros.rs:7-64) with little-endian word storage and an Ord that compares the highest word first (__uint_type_cmp @reverse, macros.rs:171-242). This exactly matches C# UInt160.CompareTo (_value3 then _value2 then _value1) and UInt256.CompareTo (_value4..._value1) in neo_csharp/src/Neo/UInt160.cs:71-79 and UInt256.cs:70-79, which is consensus-critical for sorted collections and MPT key ordering. Hex parse/format reverse bytes (uint_hex.rs) matching C#'s 0x big-endian display. The 44-file/8K-LOC layout is mostly one-type-per-file protocol enums (27-122 LOC each), which is idiomatic, not over-fragmented.
all_findings.recommendation: No change needed; this is a verification-positive. Keep the property tests for ordering transitivity and roundtrip. Consider adding a direct cross-check test pinning a few UInt256 comparison results against known C# orderings to lock the invariant.
all_findings.dimension: Foundation crates cohesion + idiomatic Rust + ecosystem reuse

all_findings.title: Fragmented telemetry: two crates and four separate metrics backends covering the same ground
all_findings.problem: Metrics collection is split across four mutually-incompatible surfaces with two different backends: (1) neo-telemetry/src/metrics.rs defines a Metrics struct holding prometheus IntCounter/Gauge/Histogram registered to its OWN Registry::new() (metrics.rs:49); (2) neo-telemetry/src/node_metrics.rs defines LazyLock<Gauge> statics registered to the prometheus DEFAULT registry via register_gauge (node_metrics.rs:14-30); (3) neo-core/src/telemetry/metrics.rs HAND-ROLLS Counter and Gauge using raw AtomicU64 with manual compare_exchange_weak for f64 gauges (metrics.rs:8-70), entirely outside prometheus; (4) neo-rpc/src/server/rpc_server.rs registers LazyLock<Counter> to the prometheus default registry (rpc_server.rs:82-90). neo-node/src/metrics.rs wraps the neo-core hand-rolled Telemetry (metrics.rs:13-14) while ALSO holding its own AtomicU64 statics. The neo-core/src/telemetry/mod.rs doc comment itself admits the overlap: 'For production deployment ... use the neo_telemetry crate instead' (mod.rs:5,14-19). Because these use different registries (and one bypasses prometheus entirely), a single /metrics endpoint cannot expose all node state, and the same logical metric (e.g. neo_block_height) is defined in multiple places.
all_findings.recommendation: Pick ONE backend and registry. Standardize on the prometheus crate (already used) with a single shared Registry passed explicitly (reth/substrate pattern), or adopt the metrics + metrics-exporter-prometheus facade so call sites use metrics::counter!/gauge! macros and the exporter owns the registry. Delete the hand-rolled AtomicU64 Counter/Gauge/Histogram in neo-core/src/telemetry/metrics.rs (it reimplements prometheus::IntCounter/Gauge poorly). Collapse neo-telemetry's Metrics struct and node_metrics.rs statics into one module on one registry. Have neo-node/src/metrics.rs delegate to that single source rather than maintaining parallel AtomicU64 statics. Target: one telemetry crate, one registry, zero hand-rolled metric primitives.
all_findings.dimension: Ecosystem / best-in-class crate adoption (reth + polkadot-sdk patterns)

all_findings.title: RPC server built on warp 0.3.7 (last release, EOL hyper 0.14) while jsonrpsee + hyper 1.x are already dependencies
all_findings.problem: The primary RPC stack (HTTP, WebSocket, CORS, basic-auth, batch handling, response shaping) is hand-built on warp: build_rpc_routes/build_ws_route in neo-rpc/src/server/routes/mod.rs:54-134 and rpc_server.rs:35-36 (warp::Filter, warp::filters::compression). warp 0.3.7 is the final warp release and is pinned to the hyper 0.14 line, which is EOL. Cargo.lock confirms the build pulls BOTH hyper 0.14.32 (via warp) AND hyper 1.8.1 (via jsonrpsee 0.24.10 / reqwest 0.12) — two hyper major versions compiled into one binary. Meanwhile a jsonrpsee adapter ALREADY exists (neo-rpc/src/server/jsonrpsee_adapter.rs) but is only a thin optional shim that re-dispatches through the same warp handler registry (jsonrpsee_adapter.rs:81-90), so dispatch logic is effectively maintained twice. reth and substrate/polkadot-sdk both standardize on jsonrpsee for the JSON-RPC server (it is built on hyper 1.x + tower, already transitively present per Cargo.lock).
all_findings.recommendation: Migrate the RPC server transport from warp to jsonrpsee's server (RpcModule + ServerBuilder), which the codebase already depends on and already has an adapter for. This removes the warp dependency and the duplicate hyper 0.14 tree, eliminates the double-dispatch maintenance burden, and aligns with reth/substrate. Keep the existing handler registry (RpcHandler/descriptor) as the method source feeding RpcModule (the adapter already demonstrates this in registered_public_methods/register_neo_method). For WebSocket subscriptions, jsonrpsee provides native subscription support to replace the hand-rolled warp ws route + SubscriptionManager. Risk: jsonrpsee 0.24's server API differs from warp's filter model and the Cargo.toml note flags the 0.26 migration as unaudited — do the transport swap behind the existing 'server' feature and validate JSON-RPC response byte-shape (jsonrpc/result/error/id ordering) against the warp path before removing it. This does not touch any protocol-critical byte layout.
all_findings.dimension: Ecosystem / best-in-class crate adoption (reth + polkadot-sdk patterns)

all_findings.title: Hand-rolled Akka.NET-style actor system (1.3K LOC) transliterated from C#, now the orchestration spine
all_findings.problem: neo-core/src/actors is a near 1:1 port of Akka.NET: ActorPath with system/segments and string parsing (actor_system.rs:18-67), Props factories, ActorRef with tell/tell_from/ask/watch/unwatch (actor_ref.rs), SupervisorDirective Restart/Resume/Stop/Escalate (actor_system.rs:385-397), a DefaultMailbox that re-wraps a VecDeque on top of the tokio mpsc channel that already buffers (mailbox.rs + actor_system.rs:316-332 dequeue-then-recv), a Guardian root actor, and an actor registry in a DashMap. The prompt explicitly warns against transliterating Akka. This is the orchestration spine of NeoSystem — Blockchain, LocalNode, and TaskManager are all spawned as actors (neo_system/core.rs:190,558-566) — so it is load-bearing, not vestigial. The mailbox layer is notably redundant: messages flow channel -> drain into VecDeque -> dequeue, duplicating buffering and adding a system-message-priority reordering that a second priority channel would express more simply.
all_findings.recommendation: Do not rip this out wholesale (high blast radius across blockchain/p2p/consensus). Instead simplify incrementally toward idiomatic Rust: each long-lived component becomes a tokio task owning its state with a typed command enum over an mpsc::Sender handle (the 'actor = task + channel' pattern reth and tokio docs recommend), replacing dynamic Box<dyn Any> message dispatch with compile-time-typed enums. As a cheap first step, delete DefaultMailbox and the drain-into-VecDeque loop (actor_system.rs:316-332, mailbox.rs) — use two mpsc channels (system + user) selected with biased tokio::select! to preserve system-message priority, removing one redundant buffering layer. Drop ActorPath string parsing/registry and actor_selection unless a concrete feature needs name-based lookup (YAGNI). Avoid pulling in a general actor framework crate (actix/ractor) — that re-imports the same Akka baggage.
all_findings.dimension: Ecosystem / best-in-class crate adoption (reth + polkadot-sdk patterns)

all_findings.title: No OpenTelemetry/OTLP export path; telemetry is prometheus-pull + plain tracing only
all_findings.problem: A grep across all crate src trees for opentelemetry/otlp/tracing-opentelemetry returns nothing. Observability is limited to prometheus pull-scrape metrics plus tracing/tracing-subscriber with json/ansi/appender layers (neo-telemetry/src/node_logging.rs, logging.rs). There is no distributed-tracing/span export, which reth and substrate-based nodes commonly offer via tracing-opentelemetry + an OTLP exporter. This is a feature gap, not a correctness issue.
all_findings.recommendation: If operators need distributed tracing or push-based telemetry, add an optional, feature-gated tracing-opentelemetry layer wired into the existing tracing-subscriber stack in neo-telemetry, exporting via opentelemetry-otlp. Keep it off by default to avoid pulling the dependency into minimal builds. Lower priority than consolidating the existing metrics fragmentation (do that first so there is a single source to export).
all_findings.dimension: Ecosystem / best-in-class crate adoption (reth + polkadot-sdk patterns)

all_findings.title: Always-compiled tonic/prost/protoc build-deps for an off-by-default feature
all_findings.problem: neo-core/Cargo.toml:109-116 unconditionally lists tonic-build and protoc-bin-vendored as [build-dependencies], with an inline note acknowledging the problem: these are only needed when the (default-off) neofs-grpc feature is enabled, but 'Cargo does not support feature-gating build-deps, so they are always compiled.' This compiles a protobuf toolchain and tonic-build for every build of the workspace's largest crate even when gRPC is disabled, slowing default builds. The runtime deps tonic/prost are correctly optional (Cargo.toml:39-40) but the build-deps are not gated.
all_findings.recommendation: Move the proto/gRPC code generation into a separate optional sub-crate (e.g. neo-neofs-proto) that is only a dependency when the neofs-grpc feature is enabled, or into an xtask invoked manually, so tonic-build/protoc are not in neo-core's always-compiled build-deps. This is the standard workaround the inline comment already gestures at and matches how reth isolates proto/codegen crates. No protocol impact.
all_findings.dimension: Ecosystem / best-in-class crate adoption (reth + polkadot-sdk patterns)

all_findings.title: tokio enabled with full feature set workspace-wide instead of per-crate features
all_findings.problem: The workspace pins tokio = { version = "1.35", features = ["full"] } with an explicit TODO to reduce features (Cargo.toml:140-141). 'full' pulls every tokio component (process, signal, fs, net, all macros, etc.) into every crate that uses the workspace tokio, inflating compile times. neo-core already overrides with a narrow set (sync/time/fs/io-util/rt, Cargo.toml:28), demonstrating the per-crate approach works, but the workspace default remains full and several crates inherit it. reth and tokio's own guidance is to enable only the needed tokio features per crate.
all_findings.recommendation: Make the workspace tokio dependency feature-minimal (e.g. default-features off with a small base set) and have each crate opt into exactly the components it uses, following the neo-core pattern at Cargo.toml:28. This is a pure build-time/dependency-hygiene change with no runtime or protocol effect. Low effort, measurable compile-time win across the large neo-core crate.
all_findings.dimension: Ecosystem / best-in-class crate adoption (reth + polkadot-sdk patterns)

all_findings.title: Positive baseline: framing, rate limiting, CLI, config, shutdown, and storage abstraction already use idiomatic crates
all_findings.problem: Several areas the prompt flagged as candidates for reinvention are already idiomatic and need no change, so engineering effort should NOT be spent here: (1) P2P framing uses tokio-util codec Decoder/Encoder with FramedRead/FramedWrite (neo-core/src/network/p2p/framed_codec.rs:5,28-81) rather than hand-rolled buffering — correct, and full libp2p would be wrong for Neo's custom TCP protocol. (2) RPC rate limiting uses governor's keyed GCRA limiter (neo-rpc/src/server/middleware/rate_limiter.rs:1-7) not a hand-rolled token bucket. (3) CLI uses clap derive (neo-node/src/cli.rs, main.rs). (4) Config uses toml + serde with a single TOML schema (neo-node/src/config/node_config.rs:37-42) — figment/config would only help if multi-source layered config were required, which it currently is not (YAGNI). (5) Graceful shutdown uses idiomatic tokio signals + select! (neo-node/src/startup/signal.rs) and the actor scheduler uses tokio-util CancellationToken + DropGuard (neo-core/src/actors/scheduler.rs:7,29-50,105-118). (6) Storage is cleanly abstracted behind Store/ReadOnlyStore/WriteStore/StoreProvider traits in neo-storage (neo-storage/src/persistence/*.rs) with rocksdb as one pluggable provider.
all_findings.recommendation: Leave these as-is. Specifically: do NOT introduce libp2p (incompatible with Neo's wire protocol), do NOT swap rocksdb for reth-db/mdbx or redb purely for fashion — the storage trait boundary already makes a provider swap a localized change if ever justified by benchmarks, so any such migration should be benchmark-driven, not speculative. Do NOT add figment/config until a real multi-source/layered-config requirement appears. Concentrate ecosystem effort on the telemetry consolidation and the warp-to-jsonrpsee migration instead.
all_findings.dimension: Ecosystem / best-in-class crate adoption (reth + polkadot-sdk patterns)

all_findings.title: P2P ownership inversion: neo-p2p is a 2.1K type-bag while the 16.6K real networking stack lives in neo-core/network/p2p
all_findings.problem: neo-p2p (2140 LOC) contains only protocol value types (MessageCommand, InventoryType, NodeCapability, payload structs, ChannelsConfig, timeouts). The actual P2P node — LocalNode actor, RemoteNode, TaskManager, handshake, connection, framed codec, message handlers, routing, bloom filter — lives in neo-core/src/network/p2p (16,593 LOC across ~50 files). neo-core depends on neo-p2p and re-exports its types (neo-core/src/network/p2p/mod.rs:69-83, payloads/mod.rs:20-38), but only 23 neo_p2p:: references exist in all of neo-core. The crate whose name and stated purpose (neo-p2p/src/lib.rs:7-13 'foundational types and abstractions for Neo's peer-to-peer networking layer') promises P2P networking actually contains almost none of it. This is the documented inversion: who owns P2P? Today neo-core does. Verified there is no cycle: neo-p2p does NOT depend on neo-core, so a migration is mechanically feasible.
all_findings.recommendation: Decide one owner. Preferred: promote neo-p2p to own the full networking stack — move neo-core/src/network/p2p/{local_node,remote_node,task_manager,connection,framed,handshake,message handlers} into neo-p2p, leaving neo-core to consume it (matching the declared neo-core→neo-p2p edge direction). This requires neo-p2p to gain access to Block/Transaction/Header payloads, so either move those verifiable payloads down too or introduce a trait seam. Alternative (lower effort): if neo-core must own networking, demote neo-p2p to an honest name like neo-p2p-types and fix its doc comment, removing the false 'full networking' framing. Do not leave the current split where the small crate claims the responsibility the large crate actually holds.

all_findings.title: Duplicated ProtocolSettings: neo-config copy (used by RPC client for tx building) vs neo-core copy (used by NeoSystem/consensus)
all_findings.problem: Two independent ProtocolSettings types exist with overlapping consensus-critical fields. neo-core::protocol_settings::ProtocolSettings (484 LOC) is the authority for the running node — used by NeoSystem (neo-core/src/neo_system/core.rs:82,126,171,182,361,550). neo-config::ProtocolSettings (379 LOC) is a SEPARATE struct imported at 22 sites in the neo-rpc CLIENT (e.g. neo-rpc/src/client/rpc_client/client.rs:21, transaction_manager.rs:304, utility.rs:49) to build and sign transactions. Each maintains its OWN hardcoded mainnet/testnet defaults: neo-config/src/protocol.rs:193,249 hardcodes network=860833102/894710606 and default_address_version()=0x35 / default_max_valid_until_block_increment()=5760 (protocol.rs:140,148), while neo-core derives the same from neo_primitives::constants (MAINNET_MAGIC=0x334F454E=860833102, ADDRESS_VERSION=0x35, in neo-primitives/src/constants.rs:70,182). The values coincide today, but they are two unsynchronized sources of truth. If a future change (or a network parameter like address_version or max_valid_until_block_increment) is updated in neo-primitives/neo-core but not in neo-config's hardcoded copy, the RPC client will build transactions with a wrong magic or validUntilBlock window — rejected by the network or signed against the wrong network (replay/interop hazard).
all_findings.recommendation: Collapse to one ProtocolSettings type. Make neo-config the single definition (it is the correct layer for configuration) and have neo-core re-export/wrap it, OR have neo-config's mainnet()/testnet() defaults read from neo-primitives::constants instead of re-hardcoding magic/address_version/max_traceable_blocks. At minimum, add a cross-crate test asserting neo_config::ProtocolSettings::mainnet() field-by-field equals neo_core::ProtocolSettings::mainnet() for network, address_version, max_valid_until_block_increment, max_traceable_blocks, and validators_count, so divergence fails CI.

all_findings.title: Two parallel telemetry stacks both consumed by neo-node; neo-core/telemetry is dead inside neo-core
all_findings.problem: neo-core/src/telemetry (796 LOC) defines its own Counter/Gauge/Histogram and a Telemetry/MetricsRecorder API, while neo-telemetry (1988 LOC) defines a Prometheus-backed Metrics registry with overlapping fields (block_height, blocks_processed, transactions_processed, connected_peers, mempool_size — neo-telemetry/src/metrics.rs:152-180). The neo-core::telemetry public API (Telemetry, MetricsRecorder, record_gauge, increment_counter) is NOT referenced anywhere inside neo-core outside its own module — it is dead within its owning crate. It is only consumed externally by neo-node (neo-node/src/metrics.rs:7,13 instantiates a global neo_core::telemetry::Telemetry), which simultaneously calls neo_telemetry::update_node_metrics / gather_prometheus (metrics.rs:61,71,107). The node thus runs two metric systems in parallel for the same node metrics. The doc comments in both modules (neo-core/src/telemetry/mod.rs:1-44, neo-telemetry/src/lib.rs:1-22) try to rationalize the split as 'internal vs production', but in practice the internal one is bypassed by neo-core and only adds confusion.
all_findings.recommendation: Eliminate one. Since neo-core never uses its own telemetry module, delete neo-core/src/telemetry and have neo-node use only neo-telemetry. If neo-core components genuinely need to emit metrics, expose a lightweight metrics trait (or use the `metrics` facade crate) that neo-node wires to neo-telemetry, rather than a second concrete Counter/Gauge implementation. Drop the dead `pub mod telemetry` from neo-core/src/lib.rs:157.

all_findings.title: neo-node binary contains the 1315-LOC dBFT ConsensusActor runtime that belongs in neo-consensus
all_findings.problem: neo-consensus (7528 LOC) holds the pure dBFT protocol: ConsensusContext, message types (prepare/commit/change-view/recovery), and the ConsensusService state machine with handlers and tests. But the runtime orchestration — the actor that drives consensus (timer ticks, start_round, on_service_event, request_recovery_on_start, on_persist_completed, message relay into the P2P layer) — lives in the binary at neo-node/src/consensus.rs:204-546 (ConsensusActor, DbftConsensusController, WalletConsensusSigner). This is ~1315 LOC of consensus logic in an application binary, tightly coupled to neo-core runtime (Actor/ActorRef), neo-core P2P payloads, NeoSystem, and wallets. It is not reusable or unit-testable as a library, and it forces neo-consensus' 'engine' to be incomplete without binary-side glue. C# Neo keeps the ConsensusService actor inside the DBFTPlugin module, not in the node entry point.
all_findings.recommendation: Move the ConsensusActor + DbftConsensusController + WalletConsensusSigner from neo-node/src/consensus.rs into neo-consensus (e.g. a neo-consensus::runtime module), depending on the neo-core actor/system traits it already uses. Leave neo-node with only thin construction/wiring (build settings, register handler). This makes neo-consensus a complete, testable unit and shrinks the binary to glue.

all_findings.title: RocksDB store/provider and write-batch buffer live in neo-core/persistence rather than neo-storage that owns the Store traits
all_findings.problem: The persistence trait layer (Store, StoreSnapshot, StoreProvider, ReadOnlyStore, WriteStore, StorageKey, StorageItem, DataCache, memory providers) is single-sourced in neo-storage (neo-storage/src/persistence/store.rs:12 etc.) and correctly re-exported by neo-core via facade (neo-core/src/persistence/mod.rs:6-37 — confirmed re-export, not duplication). However the production RocksDB Store implementation (810 LOC, neo-core/src/persistence/providers/rocksdb/store.rs) and the write_batch_buffer (621 LOC) reside in neo-core, not neo-storage. The only genuine neo-core coupling is CoreError/CoreResult (rocksdb/store.rs:2) and the rocksdb optional feature is declared on neo-core (neo-core/Cargo.toml) while neo-storage has none. This places the concrete storage backend in a different crate from the storage trait abstraction it implements — neo-storage owns the contract and the in-memory provider, but the on-disk provider sits a layer up.
all_findings.recommendation: Move the RocksDB provider + write_batch_buffer into neo-storage (behind an optional `rocksdb` feature on neo-storage) so neo-storage owns all storage backends, and switch its error usage from CoreError to neo-storage's StorageError (which already exists). This keeps neo-core free of a concrete DB backend and gives neo-storage a single clear responsibility: storage abstractions AND their implementations. Low priority — current facade works and is acyclic.

all_findings.title: neo-primitives mixes pure primitives with higher-level domain types (rpc_exception, log_event_args, blockchain, verifiable, inventory)
all_findings.problem: neo-primitives is documented as Layer 0 'primitive types (UInt160, UInt256, BigDecimal)' with no neo-* deps. In practice it also exports domain/protocol concepts that are not primitives: rpc_exception, log_event_args, log_level, blockchain, verifiable, inventory/inventory_type, oracle_response_code, trigger_type, transaction_attribute_type, node_capability_type (neo-primitives/src/lib.rs:33-58). These are enum/trait definitions tied to blockchain semantics, not foundational value types. Their presence keeps the dependency graph acyclic (everything depends on them) which is why they were pushed down, but it overloads the crate's single-responsibility: it is really a 'shared definitions' grab-bag, not a primitives crate. Similarly neo-crypto carries mpt_trie and bloom_filter (neo-crypto/src/lib.rs:54,41) which are blockchain data structures rather than cryptographic primitives.
all_findings.recommendation: Accept as-is for now (no parity impact, no cycle), but document neo-primitives honestly as 'shared protocol value types and enums' rather than 'primitives'. If a future cleanup is desired, extract the trait-only/enum-only protocol definitions (verifiable, inventory, trigger_type, transaction_attribute_type) into a small neo-protocol-types crate so neo-primitives can shrink to genuine value types, and consider moving mpt_trie out of neo-crypto into neo-storage (it is a state data structure). Do not prioritize over the ProtocolSettings and P2P findings.
all_findings.csharp_reference: neo_csharp/src/Neo (verifiable/inventory live in core, not a primitives assembly)

all_findings.title: neo-rpc bundles a full client SDK (18K) and server (18K) in one crate gated only by features
all_findings.problem: neo-rpc (36993 LOC) contains two near-equal halves: client/ (18018 LOC — RpcClient, transaction_manager, contract_client, nep17/nep11 APIs, wallet_api, models) and server/ (18361 LOC — the RpcServer plugin). They share only JSON models. The crate has three feature sets (client, server, jsonrpsee-server) with default=[] (neo-rpc/Cargo.toml). The client is an external-facing SDK (consumed standalone), while the server is a node plugin (only built via neo-node with features=['server']). Bundling an SDK and a server plugin in one crate means external SDK users pull a crate whose default does nothing and whose server feature drags in warp/hyper/rustls/p12 etc. It is not broken (features isolate the cost) but conflates two distinct responsibilities/audiences.
all_findings.recommendation: Consider splitting into neo-rpc-client (SDK, depends on neo-core via a thin model layer) and neo-rpc-server (node plugin), sharing a neo-rpc-types crate for JSON models. This matches C# where RpcClient and RpcServer are separate projects. Low priority: the feature gating already prevents accidental cost; revisit only if the client SDK is published separately.

all_findings.title: neo-tee and neo-hsm are correctly isolated optional integrations (positive finding)
all_findings.problem: Verification confirms neo-tee (5033 LOC) and neo-hsm (1732 LOC) are well-bounded: each depends ONLY on neo-crypto (no neo-core, no node types — grep for neo_core in neo-tee/src returns nothing). Their public surface is signing/wallet/enclave abstractions (neo-tee/src/lib.rs:48-52; neo-hsm exposes pkcs11/ledger/simulation signers). All node-side wiring is feature-gated and lives in neo-node behind `tee`/`hsm` features (neo-node/Cargo.toml: neo-tee/neo-hsm optional; neo-node/src/tee_integration.rs, hsm_integration.rs, tee_wallet.rs, hsm_wallet.rs). This is the correct pattern for node-optional integrations and should be the template for other optional subsystems. No action needed beyond keeping them as separate optional crates (do not merge into neo-core).
all_findings.recommendation: No change. Keep neo-tee and neo-hsm as standalone optional crates depending only on neo-crypto, with node wiring feature-gated in neo-node. Use this isolation pattern as the reference model when deciding placement of other optional subsystems (e.g. oracle/state services currently inlined in neo-core).

all_findings.title: Target dependency graph and split/merge recommendation (master architecture summary)
all_findings.problem: Current verified graph (acyclic, no cycles found): Layer 0 neo-primitives, neo-config, neo-json (no neo deps); Layer 1 neo-io→primitives, neo-crypto→{primitives,io}, neo-storage→primitives; Layer 2 neo-p2p→{primitives,crypto,io}; Layer 3 neo-core→{primitives,config,crypto,io,json,storage,p2p,neo-vm-rs}; Layer 4 neo-consensus→{core,crypto,io,primitives,neo-vm-rs}, neo-rpc→{core,config,crypto,io,json,primitives,neo-vm-rs}; Layer 5 neo-node→{core,consensus,p2p,rpc,crypto,telemetry,tee?,hsm?}. neo-tee/neo-hsm→crypto only; neo-telemetry→none. The graph is sound; the defects are placement/duplication: (a) neo-p2p under-owns P2P (neo-core over-owns it); (b) ProtocolSettings duplicated across neo-config and neo-core; (c) telemetry duplicated across neo-core and neo-telemetry; (d) consensus runtime stranded in neo-node.
all_findings.recommendation: MERGE: fold neo-core/telemetry into neo-telemetry (delete the core copy). UNIFY: make ProtocolSettings a single type (neo-config canonical, neo-core re-exports). MOVE-UP: relocate the full P2P networking stack from neo-core/network/p2p into neo-p2p so the crate matches its name (or rename neo-p2p→neo-p2p-types if neo-core stays the owner). MOVE: relocate the dBFT ConsensusActor from neo-node into neo-consensus. CONSIDER (later): split neo-rpc into client/server, move rocksdb provider into neo-storage. KEEP: neo-tee/neo-hsm as-is. CREATE (optional, lower priority): neo-protocol-types to drain the non-primitive enums/traits out of neo-primitives. Target: each crate has one responsibility, neo-core becomes orchestration over owned subsystems rather than the home of P2P+telemetry, and neo-node becomes thin glue.
all_findings.csharp_reference: neo_csharp/src/{Neo, Neo.IO, Neo.Json, Neo.Extensions} + Plugins/* module separation
